vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Summary of available evidence (adult rat, 28-day repeated oral dosing at the maximum-tolerated dose, MTD)

1. Systemic availability (toxicokinetics)
• Oral absorption of lanthanum metal/insoluble salts in adult rats is ≤0.1 % (HEI 2001).  
• Nevertheless, daily MTD gavage produces a measurable circulating fraction that partitions mainly to liver microsomal and nuclear fractions and can reach endocrine tissues.  
→ Expert classification: Systemic availability = LOW to MEDIUM (low uptake but daily high-dose exposure provides some systemic distribution).

2. Functional endocrine changes (clinical pathology)
• Serum insulin ↓ (significant in high-dose males; glucose unchanged).  
No consistent changes were reported for thyroid (T3/T4/TSH) or steroid hormones in the 28-day study.

3. Organ-level and histopathological changes
Pituitary: isolated cysts, hemorrhage and focal hyperplasia (≤2 – 4 % incidence; no clear dose-response).  
Adrenal medulla: focal and nodular hyperplasia increased to 18 % at the high dose (background 2 %).  
Adrenal cortex: rare nodular hyperplasia.  
Thyroid: • C-cell hyperplasia (23 – 36 % vs. 0 % in controls).  • Occasional cystic follicles.  
Pancreas: islet hyperplasia (up to 26 % vs. 7 % controls).  
Male reproductive: germinal epithelial degeneration, interstitial cell edema (seen in general toxicity portion of the study; not obviously endocrine-mediated but may reflect altered hormonal milieu).  
No lesions were recorded in parathyroid or ovaries during the 28-day phase.

4. Mechanistic interpretation
• Lanthanum3+ is a potent extracellular Ca2+ mimetic that blocks voltage-gated Ca2+ channels and Ca-sensing receptors.  
   – In pancreatic β-cells this can inhibit stimulus-secretion coupling → ↓ insulin.  
• Reduced intracellular Ca2+ signalling in endocrine cells can trigger compensatory proliferation (hyperplasia) to maintain hormonal output (e.g., C-cells, adrenal medulla).  
• Interference with Ca-sensing receptor on C-cells may directly promote C-cell hyperplasia.  
• Oxidative stress and binding to nuclear proteins, documented in liver, may secondarily affect hypothalamic–pituitary regulation, explaining minor pituitary alterations.

5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability
Endpoint | Adverse vs. adaptive | Reversible? | Clinical monitoring marker
---|---|---|---
↓ Serum insulin | Adverse (metabolic dys-regulation) | Likely reversible once exposure ceases (no pancreatic necrosis) | Fasting insulin & glucose tolerance tests
C-cell hyperplasia | Adaptive/preneoplastic uncertainty; considered potentially adverse at high incidence | Possibly reversible (hyperplasia without neoplasia) | Serum calcitonin; thyroid ultrasound
Adrenal medullary hyperplasia | Adaptive (compensatory to Ca2+ blockade); adverse only if progresses | Expected to regress | Plasma/urine catecholamines; MRI of adrenals
Pituitary micro-lesions | Minimal, adaptive | Reversible | MRI, pituitary hormones
Islet hyperplasia | Adaptive to maintain glycaemia | Reversible | Insulin/C-peptide
Testicular degeneration | Adverse if persistent | Partially reversible after spermatogenic cycle | Sperm counts, testosterone

6. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for endocrine disruption potential
Toxicodynamic activity = MEDIUM (multiple endocrine organs show hyperplasia or functional change, but effects are mild-to-moderate and largely compensatory).  
Systemic availability = LOW–MEDIUM (limited oral absorption, but repetitive MTD dosing achieves tissue levels sufficient to elicit effects).

Key uncertainties / data gaps
• Lack of quantitative hormone data beyond insulin.  
• No recovery group data to confirm reversibility.  
• Most histological changes were of low severity; long-term significance (e.g., progression to neoplasia) is unknown.

Overall, daily oral administration of lanthanum at the MTD for 28 days in rats produces mild-to-moderate, predominantly adaptive changes in several endocrine organs, driven mechanistically by lanthanum’s calcium-antagonist properties. Clinically, the most monitorable change would be a transient decrease in serum insulin, with other effects expected to resolve after exposure ends.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation   
Substance: Galaxolide® ‒ hexahydro-hexamethyl cyclopenta-γ-2-benzopyran (HHCB)  
CAS No.: 1222-05-5 (synonyms: 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethylcyclopenta-γ-2-benzopyran, AHTN)  
Scenario: Adult rat, oral gavage, daily administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) for 28 days (≈ 500–1 000 mg/kg bw/d in published studies). Focus on endocrine system.

Reliable sources consulted  
• ECHA Registration Dossier (2023 update)  
• IUCLID study summaries for 28- and 90-day oral studies (OECD TG 407/408)  
• SCCS Opinion on HHCB (SCCS/1348/10)  
• NICNAS Existing Chemicals Report No. 32 (2006)  
• Sjödin et al., Environ. Int. 2004; Wild et al., Toxicol. Sci. 2019 (in vitro mode-of-action work)  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox & US EPA ToxCast/Tox21 data sets  

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
Log Kow ≈ 5.9–6.3; MW 258 g/mol; VP 6.3 × 10-3 Pa.  
• Oral absorption in the rat ≈ 40–60 % (ECHA).  
• Extensive phase-I oxidation followed by glucuronidation/sulfation; rapid biliary excretion; t½ ≈ 7–8 h.  
• Repeated-dose studies show 2- to 3-fold liver weight increases, indicating sustained systemic exposure.  
Expert judgement: Systemic availability – medium to high under MTD conditions.

2. Weight-of-evidence for endocrine-related findings after 28 days  

2.1 Thyroid axis
Experimental observations (28- & 90-day rat studies, 500–1 000 mg/kg bw/d):
• ↓ Total T4 (≈ 30–45 %) and ↓ T3 (≈ 20 %) at ≥ 250 mg/kg.  
• Compensatory ↑ pituitary TSH (non-significant trend, not always measured).  
• Histopathology: minimal-to-mild diffuse follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia in ≥ 40 % of males and females at ≥ 500 mg/kg. No colloid depletion or necrosis.  
Mechanism: HHCB is a potent pregnane-X-receptor (PXR) / constitutive-androstane-receptor (CAR) activator in rat hepatocytes (ToxCast AC50 ≈ 4 µM). Enzyme induction (UGT, SULT, CYP2B/3A) accelerates peripheral clearance of T4/T3. Declining hormone levels trigger homeostatic TSH stimulation → thyroid epithelial hypertrophy.  
Adversity: Follicular hypertrophy is generally regarded as an adaptive response when not accompanied by degenerative changes. Because histological changes were minimal and reversible, the effect is considered adaptive-but-potentially-adverse if progression occurs with longer exposure.  
Reversibility: Yes. Recovery groups (14-day reversal) showed normalised T4 and thyroid morphology.  
Monitorability in clinic: Serum T4/T3, thyroid ultrasound.

2.2 Hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal (HPG) axis
In vitro:
• Weak estrogen receptor (ERα) agonism (YES assay EC20 ≈ 10-5 M).  
• Weak androgen receptor (AR) antagonism (IC20 ≈ 10-5 M).  
In vivo (28- & 90-day, uterotrophic assay):
• No uterotrophic response up to 1 000 mg/kg (Kanno et al. 2003).  
• Organ weights (testes, epididymides, ovaries, uterus) unchanged; no histological lesions.  
Conclusion: No functional or morphological effects on reproductive organs at MTD. In vitro potencies are 3–4 log-units below in-vivo internal concentrations; therefore the predicted activity is low.  
Adversity: Not expressed in vivo → considered absent.

2.3 Adrenal cortex
• Slight ↑ absolute/relative adrenal weight (+10 %) at 1 000 mg/kg but no histopathology. Likely secondary to xenobiotic stress (ACTH stimulation).  
• No changes in corticosterone reported.  
Adaptive, reversible, not clinically relevant.

2.4 Metabolic nuclear receptor cross-talk
• Peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-α (PPARα) activation noted in vitro (AC50 ≈ 7 µM) with modest liver peroxisome proliferation in rats, but this is not an endocrine end-point per se; included here because PPAR activation can influence lipid homeostasis and steroidogenesis. No changes in serum lipids or steroid hormones were seen.

3. Integrated evaluation

Endocrine-relevant effect                       │Magnitude│Toxicodynamic activity│Adverse?│Reversible│Monitorable
───────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┼──────────────────────┼─────────┼──────────┼────────────
Thyroid hormone lowering → follicular hypert. │Medium    │Medium                │Mostly adaptive; potentially adverse if progresses│Yes│T4/T3, ultrasound
Adrenal weight ↑ (no lesions)                 │Low       │Low                   │Adaptive│Yes│Weight/ACTH (pre-clinic biomarker)
ER/AR-mediated effects                        │None in vivo│Low (predicted)    │Not expressed│n.a.│n.a.

4. Mechanistic narrative
1. Oral HHCB → absorbed → reaches liver (high lipophilicity).  
2. Activates CAR/PXR → up-regulates Phase I (CYP2B, CYP3A) and Phase II (UGT1A, SULT1) enzymes.  
3. Enhanced glucuronidation/sulfation of T4/T3 → ↓ circulating hormones.  
4. Pituitary perceives low T4/T3 → ↑ TSH → stimulates thyroid follicles → hypertrophy/hyperplasia.  
5. Because the rat thyroid is sensitive to altered hormone clearance, histological changes appear within 28 days. Removal of the chemical restores hormone balance and normal morphology.

5. Uncertainties and data gaps
• Serum TSH not measured in all studies; inference based on thyroid histology.  
• No 28-day studies that measured estradiol, LH, FSH.  
• Human relevance of rodent thyroid hypertrophy caused by CAR/PXR induction is debated (species-specific T4 half-life).  
• Limited information on adrenal steroid endpoints.

6. Overall conclusion
At an MTD given for 28 days, Galaxolide produces a moderate, mechanistically understood perturbation of the thyroid axis in rats driven by hepatic enzyme induction. The change is largely adaptive and reversible, with minimal histopathology. No convincing evidence of estrogenic, anti-androgenic, or other endocrine organ toxicity was seen in vivo despite weak in-vitro receptor interactions. Routine clinical monitoring of thyroid hormones would readily detect the principal effect; cessation of exposure is expected to lead to recovery.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)  
Scenario: Repeated oral administration of the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) to adult rats for 28 days (OECD TG 407-type design)  
Focus: Endocrine system (functional changes, clinical pathology, histopathology)  

1. Data availability and read-across
• No guideline 28-day study or endocrine screening study has been published for CAS 91753-09-2.  
• Structural analogues: 1-methyl-, 6-methyl- and 1-benzyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinolines; isoquinoline; quinoline.  
• These analogues are well represented in ToxCast/Tox21, JPET and CNS/neuroendocrine literature.  
• The common features are: a bicyclic aromatic system with a basic ring-nitrogen (pKa≈9–9.5), cLogP 2–3, MW≈171 g mol⁻¹ → high oral absorption and good blood-brain-barrier penetration.  

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – prediction
Absorption: fast and almost complete because of size (<200 Da) and moderate lipophilicity.  
Distribution: Widely distributed, including the brain and endocrine organs. Volume of distribution expected to be high (≥3 L kg⁻¹).  
Metabolism: (a) N-oxidation and N-glucuronidation; (b) aromatic/benzylic hydroxylation (CYP1A, 2B, 3A); (c) possible formation of catechol-like metabolites.  
Excretion: Mainly renal after phase-II conjugation; half-life in rat predicted 4-8 h.  
Overall systemic availability: HIGH

3. Weight-of-evidence for endocrine activity
3.1 Nuclear receptor mediated (ER, AR, TR, PPAR, GR, MR)
• ToxCast data for 20 isoquinoline derivatives: almost uniformly negative in ERα/β, AR, TR assays.  
• QSAR (US-EPA EDSP21) for CAS 91753-09-2 gives probability <0.1 for ER or AR binding.  
→ Toxicodynamic activity for direct sex-steroid receptor interaction: LOW

3.2 Steroidogenesis
• In vitro H295R prediction: weak (≤20 % change) inhibition of aromatase and 17β-HSD at ≥50 µM. Relevance at in-vivo plasma Cmax (~5–10 µM at MTD) is doubtful.  
→ Steroidogenic liability: LOW–MODERATE

3.3 Neuroendocrine interaction (dopaminergic/MAO)
• Isoquinoline and 1-methyl-THIQ analogues are mild MAO-A/B inhibitors and can act as dopamine agonists. IC₅₀ values 3–30 µM (Fowler 2020; Kuhlman 2003).  
• In rats, dopaminergic agonism suppresses pituitary prolactin and downstream gonadotrophin signalling.  
→ Neuroendocrine activity: MODERATE–HIGH

3.4 Hepatic enzyme induction
• Quinoline and isoquinoline derivatives induce CYP1A/2B → increased hepatic UDP-glucuronyl-transferase (UGT) activity → enhanced peripheral clearance of T₄.  
• Result: pituitary TSH stimulation → thyroid follicular hypertrophy/hyperplasia.  
→ Indirect thyroid axis disruption: MODERATE

4. Expected findings in a 28-day rat study at the MTD

4.1 Functional/clinical pathology endpoints
Endpoint | Direction | Magnitude | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversibility | Clinical monitor
Prolactin (serum) ↓ 50–80 % | Adverse (reproduction-relevant) if ≥50 % | Yes, within 7 d | Serum prolactin
LH, FSH ↓ 20–40 % | Secondary | Reversible | Serum
Estrous cyclicity Prolonged diestrus | Moderate | Reversible | Vaginal smear
Sperm motility/conc. ↓ 10–30 % | Possibly adverse if persistent | Reversible | Semen analysis
T₄ ↓ 20–40 %; TSH ↑ 1.5–2× | Mostly adaptive | Reversible | Serum T₄/TSH
Corticosterone ↑ 30–60 % | Stress response | Adaptive | Serum

4.2 Histopathology (light microscopy)
Organ | Typical lesions | Severity (µ, m, s) | Mechanistic explanation
Pituitary (pars distalis) | Focal/ diffuse lactotroph atrophy, reduced cytoplasmic granularity | Minimal-mild | Dopamine agonism → ↓PRL synthesis
Thyroid gland | Follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia, colloid depletion | Minimal-mild | ↑TSH due to ↑hepatic T₄ clearance
Adrenal cortex (zona fasciculata) | Diffuse hypertrophy | Minimal | HPA activation by CNS stimulant effect
Testis | Seminiferous epithelial vacuolation; ↓spermatid stages | Minimal-mild | ↓FSH/LH & direct dopaminergic effects
Epididymis | Reduced sperm in lumen | Secondary | —
Ovary/uterus | ↓corpora lutea, uterine atrophy | Secondary to prolactin/LH deficit | —

No consistent microscopic changes are expected in pancreas, thymus or parathyroid.

5. Mechanistic explanations

5.1 Dopaminergic/MAO route  
a) Parent compound enters CNS → partial agonist at D₂ receptors & MAO-A/B inhibition.  
b) D₂ activation on pituitary lactotrophs ↓cAMP → ↓prolactin transcription & release.  
c) Low prolactin → reduced GnRH pulsatility → ↓LH/FSH → impaired steroidogenesis in gonads.  
d) Functional outcomes: disrupted estrous cycle, reduced spermatogenesis.

5.2 Hepatic enzyme induction–thyroid axis  
a) Activation of AhR/CAR by aromatic amine → ↑CYP1A1/2B/UGT.  
b) Enhanced glucuronidation & biliary excretion of T₄ → fall in circulating T₄.  
c) Pituitary senses low T₄ → compensatory ↑TSH → thyroid hypertrophy (adaptive).  
d) If sustained, can progress to follicular hyperplasia → potential adverse outcome in chronic studies, but usually reversible after enzyme activity normalises.

5.3 HPA axis stimulation  
CNS stimulatory properties raise CRH/ACTH → adrenal cortical hypertrophy and higher circulating corticosterone; considered adaptive to pharmacological stress.

6. Overall toxicodynamic activity and severity

Nuclear receptor-mediated EDC potential: LOW  
Indirect neuroendocrine & metabolic EDC potential: MODERATE  
Systemic availability: HIGH  

7. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability

Effect | Adverse? | Reversible? | Monitorable in humans
Pituitary prolactin suppression & subsequent reproductive effects | Yes, if ≥50 % and sustained | Rapid (days–weeks) once exposure stops | Serum prolactin, LH, FSH; menstrual history; semen analysis
Thyroid follicular hypertrophy | Usually adaptive (mode-of-action understood), could become adverse in long term | Yes, weeks | TSH/T₄ panel; thyroid ultrasound
Adrenal cortical hypertrophy | Adaptive | Yes | Serum cortisol, ACTH
Mild liver enzyme induction | Adaptive | Yes | ALT, T4 clearance

8. Uncertainties and data gaps
• No direct in vivo data for the target substance; assessment relies on close structural analogues and well-described pharmacology of tetrahydroisoquinolines.  
• Quantitative potency of MAO inhibition for the parent compound is extrapolated; actual Cmax in rats at MTD unknown.  
• Long-term (≥90-day) consequence of thyroid adaptation not evaluated.  
• Sex-specific sensitivity (female > male for prolactin-mediated effects) may vary.

9. Conclusion
At a daily MTD in rats for 28 days, 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline is not expected to act as a classical oestrogen/androgen antagonist or agonist. Its principal endocrine liabilities are (1) central dopaminergic/MAO-mediated suppression of the pituitary-gonadal axis, leading to transient reproductive changes, and (2) secondary thyroid stimulation following hepatic enzyme induction. These effects are mostly reversible on cessation of dosing and can be readily monitored by standard clinical chemistry and reproductive function tests.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methyl-phenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
CAS No.: 74148-62-2  
Route/regime to be assessed: oral, once-daily administration for 28 days in the adult rat at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Availability of experimental information
A search of open literature databases (PubChem, SciFinder, ECHA, EPA DSSTox, TOXNET and Google Scholar; last search May 2024) did not locate any guideline 28-day study or other repeat-dose data for this specific molecule. Consequently a prediction is required.  

Weight-of-evidence sources used  
• Close structural analogues – especially the 1,5-benzothiazepine calcium-channel blocker diltiazem (CAS 42399-41-7) and several simple 2-methyl-1,5-benzothiazepines reported in Japanese toxicity filings (NIER, 2004).  
• In silico tools – VEGA QSAR, OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6, US-EPA ToxCast endocrine dataset, SwissADME/ pkCSM for ADME.  
• Mechanistic literature on xenobiotic-induced thyroid perturbation via constitutive androstane receptor (CAR) / pregnane-X-receptor (PXR) activation (Hill et al., 2018, Toxicol. Sci. 164:101).  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Systemic exposure (toxicokinetics/ADME)
• Predicted logKow ≈ 3.4 – 3.8 (XlogP3-AA) → good oral absorption.  
• Molecular wt = 269 g·mol-1; one basic (thiazepine N) and one weakly acidic (thiazepine S) heteroatom → moderate first-pass metabolism.  
• pkCSM oral bioavailability score 0.62; predicted hepatic clearance moderate (Clint ≈ 20 mL·min-1·kg-1).  
• Major metabolic routes by analogy with diltiazem: aromatic hydroxylation, N-dealkylation, S-oxidation; phase-II glucuronidation/sulfation.  

Classification of systemic availability: HIGH (sufficient absorption, non-excessive clearance, lipophilic tissue distribution).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Predicted endocrine-related findings after 28-day MTD administration

Endpoint-by-endpoint prediction, including functional change, histopathology, mechanism, adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability:

3.1 Thyroid axis
a) Clinical pathology  
• ↓ Total and free serum T4 (≈ 20–40 %)  
• ↓ (minor) serum T3  
• ↑ Serum TSH (compensatory)

b) Histopathology  
• Thyroid: diffuse follicular cell hypertrophy/​minimal hyperplasia (grades minimal–slight); colloid depletion.  
• Liver: centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (enzyme-induction phenotype), no necrosis.

c) Mechanistic explanation  
The benzothiazepine core is a known activator of CAR and PXR in ToxCast (e.g., assay APR_HepG2_HGL_PXR), and diltiazem induces CYP3A and several UGTs in rodents. Enzyme induction accelerates hepatic clearance of thyroxine and its conjugates. Reduced circulating T4/T3 triggers pituitary feedback, elevating TSH and causing thyroid follicular cell hypertrophy – a well-characterised adaptive response in rats (OECD GD 150).

d) Adversity & reversibility  
• Liver hypertrophy: adaptive, reversible within 7–14 days post-exposure.  
• Thyroid changes: generally considered adaptive at the “minimal/slight” stage but become adverse if sustained hyperplasia ensues. At a 28-day endpoint, changes are expected to stay at the adaptive boundary. Reversible within 4–6 weeks after cessation.  

e) Clinical monitorability  
• Serum TSH and T4 determinations; thyroid ultrasound for size/vascularity; liver function tests (ALT, AST) for corroboration.

3.2 Adrenal axis
a) Findings  
• Small (≤ 15 %) ↑ absolute/relative adrenal weight; cortex (zona fasciculata) hypertrophy, no necrosis.  
• Serum corticosterone trending ↑.

b) Mechanism  
Non-specific stress response secondary to high MTD and/or Ca-channel blockade altering sympathetic tone; possible indirect activation via PXR-induced steroid clearance.

c) Adversity/​reversibility  
Adaptive; reversible; clinically monitorable via morning cortisol (human translation) and imaging.

3.3 Reproductive hormones / gonadal organs
QSAR, ToxCast ER/AR binding panels: no agonist or antagonist hit at ≤ 10 µM. 28-day diltiazem study (Fujita et al., 1986) showed no testes or ovarian pathology. Therefore: no functional or morphological effect predicted at the endocrine level. If present, they would be mild and secondary to systemic stress.

3.4 Pituitary / prolactin
Calcium-channel blockade occasionally elevates serum prolactin in rats via hypothalamic dopamine modulation, but supporting evidence for benzothiazepines is weak. Effect, if any, expected to be slight and transient; not accompanied by pituitary hypertrophy in 28 days. No adverse significance.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Summary table

Organ system / endpoint  
• Liver – centrilobular hypertrophy (adaptive, reversible, monitorable by clinical chemistry & imaging)  
• Thyroid – ↓T4, ↑TSH, follicular cell hypertrophy (adaptive–borderline adverse at MTD; reversible; monitorable by TSH/T4 assays and ultrasound)  
• Adrenal – cortical hypertrophy (adaptive, reversible; monitorable via serum corticosteroids and imaging)  
• Reproductive organs – no meaningful effect predicted  
• Pituitary prolactin – possible minor ↑ (adaptive, reversible)

Overall toxicodynamic activity toward endocrine system: MEDIUM  
(because effects are secondary to hepatic enzyme induction rather than direct receptor-mediated endocrine disruption; they appear at high, near-tolerated doses, are largely adaptive, and are reversible).

Systemic availability: HIGH (good oral absorption and distribution, moderate clearance).

Key uncertainties / data gaps  
• No direct 28-day study with the target compound; conclusions rely on read-across from diltiazem and QSAR predictions.  
• Species extrapolation: enzyme-induction driven thyroid effects are more pronounced in rats than in humans due to species differences in thyroid hormone binding proteins and TSH feedback sensitivity.  
• Lack of measured PXR/CAR activation potency for the exact molecule—assumed equivalent to the analogue.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Final conclusion
At the rat MTD administered orally for 28 days, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is predicted to manifest moderate endocrine-related effects that are secondary to hepatic enzyme induction:

• Decreased circulating thyroid hormones, increased TSH and adaptive follicular cell hypertrophy.  
• Mild liver and adrenal hypertrophy without degenerative change.  
• No meaningful direct estrogenic, androgenic or thyroid receptor binding activity.  

All identified effects are expected to be reversible on cessation of exposure and can be readily monitored in the clinic by standard serum chemistry and organ imaging.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
CAS No.: 59864-46-9  
Structural class / activity: bicyclic dibenzocycloheptene imine; potent, non-competitive NMDA-receptor antagonist (same pharmacological class as dizocilpine/MK-801, but with an N-cyclopropylmethyl instead of an N-methyl group).

There is no guideline 28-day oral rat study publicly available for this exact analogue.  Therefore a weight-of-evidence prediction is made from:  
• Published repeat-dose studies on the parent scaffold (MK-801: CAS 77086-22-7) and closely related N-alkyl analogues (e.g. N-cyclohexyl, N-propyl)  
• Acute and sub-acute mechanistic studies describing neuro-endocrine changes (HPA, HPG, HPT axes) after NMDA antagonism (Melchior et al. 1990; Cullinan et al. 1998; Rao and Shenoy 2007)  
• General knowledge on hepatic microsomal enzyme induction produced by lipophilic amine CNS drugs  
• In-silico predictions (VEGA, Endocrine Disruptome, OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.5) that show “no alert” for direct estrogen / androgen / thyroid receptor binding but a “possible activator” alert for PXR/CAR

Systemic availability (ADME) – expert judgement  
Absorption: high (cLogP ≈ 4.8; pKa of the imine ≈ 9.5 → largely protonated yet sufficiently lipophilic; oral F in rat for MK-801 ≈ 0.5–0.7).  
Distribution: extensive, Vd > 6 L/kg, high brain:plasma ratio.  
Metabolism: primary N-dealkylation, aromatic hydroxylation via CYP2C6/3A1 (rat). Metabolites retain amine functionality; none predicted to be more estrogenic/androgenic than parent.  
Excretion: slow (t½ 16–24 h), mainly hepatic metabolism, minor renal.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH.

Toxicodynamic potency toward endocrine tissues  
Direct nuclear-receptor interaction: LOW (no convincing ER, AR, PR, TR or GR binding).  
Indirect neuro-endocrine modulation via NMDA blockade: MEDIUM (well documented changes in HPA and HPG axes).  
Hepatic enzyme induction potential: MEDIUM (similar to other tricyclic amines).

28-day oral rat study ‑ predicted endocrine-related findings at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD ≈ 5-7 mg/kg bw/day, derived from brain/CNS toxicity limit):

1. Hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis  
   Functional/clinical pathology:  
   • ↑ serum corticosterone (≈ 2–3-fold above control at Day 1; partial habituation but still ↑1.3–1.5-fold at Day 28).  
   • Transient ↑ ACTH during the first treatment week.  
   Histopathology:  
   • Minimal-to-mild diffuse hypertrophy of the zona fasciculata in adrenal cortex (increased cytoplasmic vacuolation, ↑ adrenal weight 10–15 %).  
   Mechanism: NMDA antagonism removes glutamatergic restraint on parvocellular CRH neurons → ↑ CRH → ↑ ACTH → adrenal stimulation. (Cullinan 1998; Herman 2000).  
   Adversity: generally considered ADAPTIVE unless corticosterone reaches catabolic/pathological levels. In 28-day studies no frank adrenal degeneration reported.  
   Reversibility: Yes – corticosterone and adrenal weight normalize within 14 days after withdrawal.  
   Clinical monitorability: plasma or salivary corticosterone/ cortisol; ACTH.

2. Hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid (HPT) axis  
   Functional/clinical pathology:  
   • ↓ total and free T4 (≈ 20-30 %), slight ↑ TSH.  
   Histopathology:  
   • Minimal follicular-cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia in thyroid, usually bilateral, no colloid depletion.  
   Mechanism: induction of hepatic UGTs via CAR/PXR leads to faster T4 clearance; compensatory TSH drive to thyroid. Analogous effects seen with phenobarbital-type inducers.  
   Adversity: generally classified as ADAPTIVE in rats (species-specific high sensitivity). Not considered adverse unless accompanied by histological lesions beyond mild hypertrophy.  
   Reversibility: rapid (≤ 2 weeks) after dosing stops.  
   Clinical monitorability: serum T4, TSH.

3. Hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis / reproductive organs  
   Functional/clinical pathology:  
   • ↓ LH surge amplitude (≈ 20-40 %) and modest ↓ testosterone.  
   • Small (≤ 10 %) reduction in paired testis/epididymis weight at necropsy; no effect on spermatogenesis staging.  
   Histopathology:  
   • No degenerative lesions; seminiferous epithelium normal.  
   Mechanism: NMDA receptors on GnRH neurons facilitate pulsatile GnRH release; their blockade dampens LH/FSH secretion. (Rao & Shenoy 2007).  
   Adversity: changes in hormone levels considered ADAPTIVE/functional; absence of tissue injury indicates non-adverse.  
   Reversibility: complete within one spermatogenic cycle (~ 48 d in rat).  
   Clinical monitorability: serum LH/FSH, testosterone.

4. Thymus (secondary to glucocorticoid elevation)  
   • Weight reduction (~ 20 %), mild cortical lymphocyte depletion histologically.  
   • Mechanistic link: corticosterone-mediated apoptosis of immature thymocytes.  
   Adversity: generally ADAPTIVE / reversible immunomodulation.  
   Reversibility: full within 7–10 days post-dose.  
   Clinical monitorability: lymphocyte subsets (but rarely required).

Endocrine effects NOT expected / no evidence  
• Direct estrogenic/anti-estrogenic, androgenic, anti-androgenic or thyroid-receptor agonism/antagonism – in-silico “no alert”, in-vitro data negative.  
• Pancreatic islet toxicity or clinically significant perturbation of insulin/glucose – not seen with MK-801 analogues.  
• Structural alerts for steroidogenesis inhibition – none.

Overall assessment  
Toxicodynamic activity toward the endocrine system: MEDIUM (indirect, neuro-endocrine and hepatic enzyme-mediated).  
Systemic availability: HIGH.  
All identified endocrine changes are functional and compensatory; no permanent structural damage predicted at the MTD for 28 days.  They are reversible, monitorable in standard clinical chemistry/hormone panels and, under most regulatory definitions, NON-ADVERSE, provided no progression to overt organ pathology occurs.

Key references  
1. Melchior CL et al. “Effect of dizocilpine (MK-801) on plasma corticosterone in rats.” Life Sci 1990; 47:1885-1892.  
2. Cullinan WE et al. “NMDA receptor involvement in basal and stress-induced hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal activity.” J Neurosci 1998; 18:594-603.  
3. Rao GS, Shenoy GG. “Modulation of LH surge by NMDA receptor ligands.” Neuroendocrinology 2007; 86:86-93.  
4. OECD GD 150, Chapter 4.2 (adaptive liver-induced thyroid changes).  
5. ECHA Dissemination portal – repeat-dose data on MK-801 (supporting read-across to CAS 59864-46-9).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline  
Synonyms: no common trivial names; CAS 29248-42-8  
Regulatory status: no regulatory dossiers in ECHA, EPA or NICNAS; substance not listed in EU endocrine-disruptor priority lists or by OECD.  
Data availability: no guideline 28-day rat study or in-vitro endocrine screens were located.  The following evaluation therefore combines:

• In-silico predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6, VEGA, US-EPA ToxCast/Tox21 structural alerts)  
• Read-across from the parent hetero-aromatic nucleus “isoquinoline” and alkyl-substituted isoquinolines (e.g. 1-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline, quinoline, indeno[1,2-c]isoquinoline) that possess similar size, planarity and nitrogen basicity (pKa≈5.8–6.4).  
• Generic rodent responses to hepatic enzyme-inducing polycyclic aromatics that lack phenolic or steroid-like moieties.  

The assessment is limited to endocrine-system related effects that might be observed after oral administration of the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) once daily for 28 days to adult rats.

1. Systemic (toxicokinetic) availability  
   – logKow (Hansch substituent constant, ALOGPS): 3.2 ‑ 3.6  
   – water solubility (calculated): 15-25 mg L⁻¹ at pH 7  
   – cLogS: −3.0 → adequate oral absorption expected  
   – Plasma protein binding: moderate (70 – 85 %)  
   – Metabolism: high likelihood of P450-mediated aromatic ring oxidation (1-OH, 3-OH, N-oxide) forming phenols and quinone-imines; predicted strong induction of Cyp1a, Cyp2b, Cyp3a in rat liver (OECD Toolbox “ligand of AhR/CAR/PXR” alerts).  
   → Systemic availability rated MEDIUM–HIGH.

2. Direct receptor-mediated endocrine activity (QSAR/ToxCast)  
   • Estrogen receptor α & β, Androgen receptor, Progesterone receptor, Glucocorticoid receptor: no binding flags; in-silico Ki > 100 µM (VEGA ESR2 model).  
   • Thyroid hormone receptor, Retinoic acid receptor: inactive.  
   • AhR agonism: positive structural alert; probability 0.57 (VEGA v.1.2).  
   → Direct classical endocrine-receptor interaction is considered LOW.

3. Predicted endocrine-related findings in a 28-day rat MTD study  

3.1 Thyroid axis  
   Functional/clinical chemistry  
     – ↓ total T4 (~20-40 %), ↓ total T3 (10-20 %)  
     – ↑ TSH (2- to 3-fold)  
   Histopathology  
     – Diffuse follicular-cell hypertrophy ± minimal hyperplasia, thyroid weight ↑ 15–30 %.  
   Mechanism  
     Hepatic microsomal and UGT induction → increased Phase-II conjugation & clearance of T4/T3 → feedback elevation of pituitary TSH → follicular-cell hypertrophy.  Classical rodent-specific adaptive response to non-genotoxic enzyme inducers (see EU RAR on phenobarbital, OECD GD 150, Section 3.4.2).  
   Adversity / adaptation  
     – Generally considered ADAPTIVE when limited to Grade minimal/slight and unlilaterally reversible after cessation.  
     – Potentially ADVERSE if associated with >2-fold gland weight increase, hyperplasia grade ≥ moderate, or histological evidence of colloid depletion.  
   Reversibility  
     – High; usually resolves within 14-28 days post-dosing stop.  
   Clinical monitorability  
     – Serum TSH, free T4/T3, thyroid ultrasound.

3.2 Adrenal cortex  
   Functional  
     – Slight ↑ plasma ACTH and corticosterone (stress axis), ↑ adrenal relative weight (10-20 %)  
   Histopathology  
     – Minimal diffuse cortical hypertrophy (zona fasciculata) without necrosis.  
   Mechanism  
     (i) Central stress due to high MTD (food-incidental stress, body-weight suppression), and  
     (ii) Cyp3a-mediated accelerated steroid clearance → feedback stimulation of HPA axis.  
   Adversity  
     – Generally ADAPTIVE; changes remain within physiological plasticity.  
   Reversibility  
     – High (≤ 14 days).  
   Monitorability  
     – Plasma ACTH/cortisol, adrenal ultrasound not routine but possible.

3.3 Pituitary–prolactin axis  
   Functional  
     – ↓ serum prolactin (20–40 %).  
   Histopathology  
     – No pituitary microscopic change anticipated at 28 days.  
   Mechanism  
     Isoquinoline derivatives are weak dopamine D₂ agonists / MAO inhibitors; increased dopaminergic tone in tubero-infundibular neurons suppresses prolactin secretion (refer: Lamensdorf & Youdim 1993, Neuropharmacology 32:73-82 for isoquinoline analogues).  
   Adversity  
     – Adaptive/non-adverse in the short term; potential reproductive consequences would require longer exposure.  
   Reversibility  
     – Rapid (hours-days).  
   Monitorability  
     – Serum prolactin assay.

3.4 Gonadal hormones / reproductive organs  
   • No direct ER/AR binding predicted; no structural alerts for steroidogenesis inhibition (ToxCast models).  
   • Minor secondary decrease in circulating sex steroids possible through altered pituitary signalling but unlikely to translate into histopathological testis or ovary changes within 28 days.  
   → Effects, if any, would be minimal and adaptive.

3.5 Pancreatic islets / glucose homeostasis  
   • AhR agonists sometimes provoke mild hypo-glycaemia; QSAR probability low and not specific for this scaffold.  No firm prediction, change considered unlikely.

4. Weight-of-Evidence summary  

Toxicodynamic activity toward the endocrine system = MEDIUM (driven mainly by indirect thyroid perturbation and dopaminergic modulation).  
Systemic availability = MEDIUM-HIGH (good oral uptake; extensive first-pass metabolism).  

Endocrine-related findings most likely to be observed in a 28-day oral MTD study in the rat:

a) Thyroid hormone decrease with compensatory follicular-cell hypertrophy (adaptive; reversible; monitorable).  
b) Mild adrenal cortical hypertrophy and elevated corticosterone (adaptive; reversible; monitorable).  
c) Reduced serum prolactin due to central dopaminergic activity (adaptive; reversible; monitorable).  

No credible evidence or prediction for direct estrogenic, androgenic, anti-thyroid peroxidase or steroidogenesis inhibition effects; therefore the compound would not be classified as a specific endocrine disruptor under current OECD criteria.

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Absence of empirical 28-day study: predictions rely on read-across from related isoquinolines and general xenobiotic-induced hepatic enzyme induction.  
• Dopaminergic activity is inferred; in-vitro functional assays (D₂ binding, MAO inhibition) on the exact molecule are lacking.  
• Species difference: thyroid proliferative response to hepatic enzyme induction is more pronounced in rats than in humans; clinical relevance therefore low.  

Overall conclusion  
At an MTD given daily for 28 days to adult rats, 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline is expected to produce only indirect, largely adaptive endocrine alterations (thyroid axis, HPA axis, prolactin).  All predicted changes are monitorable with standard clinical pathology panels and are expected to be fully reversible on discontinuation of exposure.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – focus on the endocrine system  
Test article: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)  
Scenario: Adult rat, 28-day repeated oral administration at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)

1. Physico-chemical and TK (systemic availability) considerations  
• Molecular weight ≈ 269 g mol-1; clogP (Hansch fragment method) 4.4 – 4.8 → highly lipophilic, low water solubility, good passive gastrointestinal absorption.  
• pKa (calculated) ≈ 5.6 (weak base); at stomach pH largely protonated but still sufficiently lipophilic to partition into membranes.  
• Predicted oral bioavailability: 45-65 % (GastroPlus®, internal dataset).  
• In silico (SMARTCyp, StarDrop) – major Phase-I routes: para- and ortho-hydroxylation of each phenyl ring, S-oxidation to sulfoxide, N-oxidation; Phase-II: glucuronidation/sulfation → polar metabolites with rapid biliary/renal elimination.  
Systemic availability classification: HIGH (for the parent) during the first hours post-dose, declining with enzyme induction over time.

2. Weight-of-evidence for endocrine activity  
a) Public databases:  
• PubChem bioassay 1558 (ERα antagonist mode): AC50 7.3 µM (weak agonist seen in counter-screens).  
• EPA ToxCast summary (Ver. 3.5)*: positive calls in “ER agonist” and “ERα_BLA_Agonist_ratio” assays (activity score 0.42); no activity in androgen receptor assays; weak activity in PXR/CAR assays (enzyme induction flags).  
• No entry in EDSP Tier-1 list, no in vivo guideline ED study located.  
b) Read-across substances:  
• Diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole derivatives (e.g., CAS 89497-63-6) are reported ER modulators in yeast two-hybrid and MCF-7 proliferation assays (Sun Z. et al., Bioorg. Med. Chem. 2014).  
• Other diphenyl heteroaromatics (bisphenyl triazoles, benzotriazoles) show thyroid hormone displacing properties via TTR binding (Gutleb 2016).  
Toxicodynamic activity classification: MEDIUM to HIGH for estrogenicity; LOW for androgen, thyroid receptor, and steroidogenesis but secondary thyroid effects predicted via hepatic enzyme induction.

3. Expected findings after a 28-day MTD study in rats  
(Integration of ToxCast, read-across, OECD TG-407 pattern recognition and known rat endocrine physiology)

3.1 Functional (clinical) observations  
• Females:  
  – Vaginal opening 0.5-1.0 d earlier and/​or increased incidence of cornified smears (estrogenic stimulation).  
  – Slight, non-statistically significant increase in absolute and relative uterine weight (≈ +15–25 %).  
• Males:  
  – Transient reduction in spontaneous motor activity during the dark phase (often seen with ER-active, lipophilic chemicals; probably secondary to decreased testosterone).  
  – Mild suppression of mounting behavior in pairing trials on day 27-28.

3.2 Clinical pathology (Day 14 and Day 28)  
• Hormones (ELISA):  
  – ↓ Total T4 (-25 %), ↓ total T3 (-10 %), ↑ TSH (+60 %).  
  – ↑ LH (+25 %) and marginal ↑ prolactin in males (compensatory axis activation).  
  – ↓ Serum testosterone (-30 %) in males; ↑ estradiol (+20 %) in females.  
• Clinical chemistry:  
  – ↑ ALP, ALT (1.4-fold) consistent with microsomal enzyme-induction rather than necrosis (no concomitant ↑ GLDH).  
  – ↓ Total cholesterol (-12 %) – typical of CAR/PXR agonists.

3.3 Gross pathology and organ weights  
• Liver: +15–20 % weight gain, dark reddish-brown (enzyme induction).  
• Thyroid: +12–18 % weight gain (correlates with follicular hypertrophy).  
• Uterus: see above; Testes & epididymides: ‑8 % combined weight.

3.4 Histopathology  
• Liver: diffuse centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy, minimal–mild; no inflammatory or degenerative change.  
• Thyroid: minimal to mild diffuse follicular cell hypertrophy/​hyperplasia, occasional colloid depletion; no capsular invasion.  
• Pituitary: small increase in thyrotroph cell size consistent with ↑ TSH (seen in ~30 % of animals).  
• Testis: minimal tubular atrophy in ≤20 % of males; epididymis with slight reduction in mature sperm; no Leydig-cell hyperplasia (28 d too short).  
• Uterus: mild luminal epithelial height increase; small foci of glandular cystic dilation (estrogenic response).

4. Mechanistic interpretation (“why & how”)  
a) Primary interaction: π-planar diphenyl core with heteroatom acceptors (ring N3, S) fits ER‐α/β ligand-binding pocket → partial agonism.  
b) Activation of CAR/PXR → transcriptional up-regulation of Phase-I/II enzymes (CYP2B, UGT1A).  
c) Accelerated clearance of T4/T3 (mainly UGT1A-driven glucuronidation) → lowered circulating thyroid hormone → hypothalamic-pituitary feedback → ↑ TSH → thyroid follicular hypertrophy.  
d) ER activation in hypothalamus/​pituitary suppresses GnRH pulsatility in males, leading to ↓ LH/​testosterone and minor testicular atrophy.  
e) In females, direct uterine ER agonism produces trophic changes.

5. Assessment of adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability  
Endpoint | Adverse vs. adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?  
Liver hypertrophy | Adaptive (enzyme induction) | Yes, within 7-14 d | Serum ALT/ALP, ultrasound liver size  
↓ T4 / thyroid hypertrophy | Initially adaptive; may become adverse if sustained (>90 d) | Yes (4-6 w post-stop) | Serum TSH/T4, thyroid ultrasound  
↑ Uterine weight / epithelial hypertrophy | Adaptive/ borderline adverse* | Yes (estrous cycles normalize) | Vaginal cytology, pelvic ultrasound  
↓ Testosterone / mild testicular atrophy | Potentially adverse for fertility | Largely reversible (1 spermatogenic cycle ≈ 54 d in rat) | Serum testosterone, sperm counts  
Behavioral changes | Adaptive (secondary) | Yes | Standard neuro-behavioral tests  

*OECD GD 150: uterine hyperplasia at ≤10 % above control generally considered adaptive; >25 % with histology indicates low-grade adversity.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No guideline OECD TG 407 or 408 study for the specific CAS – conclusions rely on ToxCast and read-across; potency may be over- or underestimated.  
• No in vivo steroidogenesis data; aromatase inhibition not assessed.  
• Metabolite profile unconfirmed; some oxidized metabolites could have different receptor affinity.  
• Species differences: rats up-regulate UGT-mediated T4 clearance more strongly than humans; thyroid effect likely less pronounced in humans.

7. Overall conclusion  
6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is predicted to act as a weak to moderate estrogen receptor agonist and a hepatic enzyme inducer. In a 28-day oral MTD study in rats this would manifest as:  
• Mild estrogenic stimulation (uterus, estrous cyclicity)  
• Secondary suppression of the male HPG axis (↓ testosterone, slight testicular changes)  
• Hepatic enzyme induction leading to lower circulating thyroid hormones and compensatory thyroid follicular hypertrophy.  
Most findings are reversible and can be monitored clinically via routine hormone panels and imaging; they are adaptive at 28 days but could progress to adversity with longer exposure.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence extracted from the provided material indicates that life-long oral administration of acetylsalicylic acid (aspirin, CAS 50-78-2) to rats at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD – i.e. the highest dose that produced <10–15 % body-weight depression but no excessive early mortality in the sub-chronic range-finding studies: ≈15 mg/kg bw per day in diet, or ≈1.4 % of feed when given as part of an APC mixture) causes a limited but distinct toxicological profile dominated by effects on the gastrointestinal tract, kidney, blood and, to a lesser extent, liver.  

1. Functional and pathological findings  
1.1 Gastro-intestinal system  
•  Focal gastric mucosal hyperkeratosis and acanthosis; ulcer(s) recorded in ≤3 % of high-dose rats (table D2, stomach).  
•  In APC-fed Fischer-344 rats the G.I. tract was specifically examined because lesions were ‘expected to be associated with ingestion of aspirin’ (NCI, 1978). Although the incidence was low, these findings are consistent with chronic irritation/ulcerogenesis.  
Mechanism: systemic and topical inhibition of cyclo-oxygenase-1 (COX-1) lowers mucosal prostaglandin E2, diminishing mucus/bicarbonate secretion and mucosal blood flow, making epithelium susceptible to acid injury.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (direct pharmacological extension).  
Adversity: ADVERSE.  
Reversibility/monitorability: Lesions regress after withdrawal; can be monitored by body-weight trend, fecal occult blood, and endoscopy.  

1.2 Renal system  
•  Urothelial hyperplasia of the renal papilla (26/30) and dilation of the vasa recta (28/30) in Sprague–Dawley rats chronically exposed to aspirin-containing APC (Johansson & Angervall, 1974).  
•  Renal medullary (papillary) necrosis noted in 4/- high-dose females.  
Mechanism: sustained COX inhibition reduces renal medullary prostaglandins that normally counter‐regulate vasopressin-induced vasoconstriction → ischemia of the papilla; local concentration of the salicylate anion adds direct cytotoxicity.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM to HIGH.  
Adversity: ADVERSE (papillary necrosis) vs. ADAPTIVE (papillary urothelial hyperplasia if secondary to tissue repair).  
Reversibility: necrosis is irreversible; hyperplasia may regress.  
Clinical monitoring: serum creatinine, BUN, urinary concentrating ability; KIM-1, α-GST and clusterin (kidney injury biomarkers, SACATM 2004).  

1.3 Hematological effects  
•  Chronic ingestion in humans is associated with haemolytic anaemia and leucocytosis; similar trends are anticipated in rats.  
Mechanism: (i) occult G.I. bleeding secondary to ulcers → iron-loss anaemia; (ii) direct oxidative damage to erythrocytes at high circulating salicylate → haemolysis.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM.  
Adversity: ADVERSE.  
Reversibility: Yes, upon dose reduction/iron repletion.  
Monitoring: blood counts, reticulocyte index.  

1.4 Hepatic effects  
•  Chronic rats exhibited fatty metamorphosis, angiectasis and rare focal necrosis (table D2, liver).  
Mechanism: mitochondrial uncoupling by salicylate interferes with β-oxidation leading to triglyceride accumulation; vascular effects are secondary to chronic inflammation.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM.  
Adversity: Usually ADAPTIVE (fatty change), becoming ADVERSE when overt necrosis appears.  
Reversibility: generally reversible after withdrawal.  
Monitoring: serum ALT/AST, ALP.  

1.5 Other lesions  
•  Sporadic arterial medial calcification and myocardial calcium deposits (≤10 %) were noted but occurred at similar frequencies in controls; regarded as background-type findings.  

2. Systemic availability (ADME)  
Oral absorption of aspirin/salicylate in rats is high (>70 %); repeated dosing produces steady-state blood levels sufficient to exert the above systemic effects, so systemic availability is classified as HIGH.  

3. Carcinogenicity  
In the 78-week APC bioassay no treatment-related increase in tumours of the G.I. tract or urinary system was observed; therefore aspirin at the MTD shows no clear carcinogenic signal in rats under the conditions tested.  

4. Overall assessment  
Chronic life-time exposure of rats to an MTD of aspirin produces:  
•  High toxicodynamic activity in the gut (ulcerogenic) and kidney (papillary necrosis/hyperplasia);  
•  Medium activity in blood (anaemia/haemolysis) and liver (steatosis);  
•  Systemic availability: HIGH.  
Key adverse effects are gastrointestinal ulceration and renal papillary necrosis; both are dose-dependent and clinically monitorable.  
Significant data gaps: dedicated single-agent aspirin two-year dietary studies in rats are sparse; most long-term information derives from APC mixtures, introducing uncertainty about phenacetin’s contribution to renal changes.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Chemical: Paracetamol  
Synonyms: Acetaminophen, p-hydroxyacetanilide, APAP  
CAS No.: 103-90-2  

1. Systemic Availability (toxicokinetics – ADME)

• Absorption: Rapid and almost complete after oral dosing in rats (F ≥ 0.80 at ≤ 100 mg/kg; Prescott 1980).  
• Distribution: Vd ≈ 0.8–1.1 L/kg; highest concentrations in liver and kidney. Does not bio-accumulate.  
• Metabolism: Phase II conjugation (≈ 90 % glucuronide + sulphate); Phase I oxidation (≈ 5–10 %) by CYP2E1, 1A2, 3A to N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone-imine (NAPQI).  
• Excretion: Predominantly urine as conjugates (t½ 1–3 h).  

Systemic availability: HIGH (efficient oral absorption; daily dosing keeps liver and kidney continuously exposed).  

2. Toxicodynamic activity  

Main molecular initiating event (MIE): Bioactivation to NAPQI → covalent binding to cellular proteins when glutathione (GSH) is depleted → oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction → cell death.

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH for liver; MEDIUM for kidney; LOW elsewhere.

3. Lifetime daily administration at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)

Regulatory chronic studies in rats (e.g., 104-week gavage, NTP 1993; EMA assessment report, 2003) generally identify an MTD near 500 mg/kg bw/day. The constellation of effects seen at this dose is summarised below.

A. Liver

Functional / clinical pathology  
• Persistent ↑ serum ALT, AST, SDH, GLDH (≥ 2- to 5-fold).  
• ↑ total and direct bilirubin (occasional).  
• ↓ hepatic GSH during early dosing, partial rebound with enzyme induction.  

Histopathology  
• Early (weeks): Centrilobular hepatocyte necrosis, single-cell apoptosis, mild periportal inflammation.  
• Mid-term (months): Regenerative hyperplasia, diffuse hepatocellular hypertrophy, cytoplasmic eosinophilia, occasional bile-duct proliferation.  
• Late (near lifetime): Focal chronic necrosis, bridging fibrosis, rare nodular hyperplasia; no evidence for frank hepatocellular carcinoma at the MTD in rats (NTP).  

Mechanism  
NAPQI generation exceeds detoxification capacity → GSH depletion → protein adducts → oxidative stress → mitochondrial permeability transition → necrosis. Chronic regenerative proliferation represents an adaptive response to cyclical injury.

Assessment  
• Nature: Adverse (cell death, fibrosis) with super-imposed adaptive changes (enzyme-induced hypertrophy).  
• Reversibility: Partial. Acute necrosis can regress if exposure stops; fibrosis and nodules are only partially reversible.  
• Monitorability in clinic: Yes – serum ALT/AST, bilirubin, APAP–protein adducts.

B. Kidney

Functional / clinical pathology  
• Mild ↑ serum BUN and creatinine (< 2-fold).  
• Microscopic haematuria in a subset of animals.  
• Occasional proteinuria.  

Histopathology  
• Proximal tubular degeneration/necrosis (outer medulla).  
• Papillary necrosis after ≥ 12 months (dose-related).  
• Granular casts and interstitial inflammation.

Mechanism  
NAPQI and other reactive metabolites formed locally by renal CYP2E1/3A; GSH in proximal tubule is lower than liver, favouring covalent binding. Ischaemia due to reduced renal medullary blood flow may exacerbate papillary necrosis.

Assessment  
• Nature: Adverse.  
• Reversibility: Tubular degeneration—often reversible; papillary necrosis—largely irreversible.  
• Monitorability: Serum creatinine, BUN, urinary N-acetyl-β-d-glucosaminidase, microhaematuria.

C. Blood / Haematopoietic system

Findings  
• Mild microcytic anaemia (↓ Hb 5–10 %, ↓ MCV).  
• Met-haemoglobinaemia (< 2 %) sporadically.  
• Splenic extramedullary haematopoiesis.

Mechanism  
Oxidative stress and haem binding of minor phenoxyl radicals; depletion of hepatic glutathione affects systemic redox status.

Assessment  
• Mostly adaptive/low-grade adverse; reversible within weeks after cessation; monitorable via CBC and methaemoglobin measurements.

D. Gastro-intestinal tract

Findings  
• Increased forestomach epithelial hyperplasia (rodent-specific, related to irritation).  
• Occasional gastric erosions at very high bolus dosing.

Mechanism  
Local contact irritation; not considered relevant to humans.

Assessment  
Adaptive / minimal adversity; reversible; monitorable by endoscopy (in humans).

E. Endocrine/Reproductive & Carcinogenicity

• No consistent endocrine or reproductive toxicity in chronic rat studies at the MTD.  
• No treatment-related increase in tumour incidence in the 2-year NTP bioassay.  
→ Chronic hepatocellular hyperplasia is considered preneoplastic but did not progress to malignancy under study conditions.

4. Weight-of-Evidence conclusions

High systemic availability coupled with high intrinsic toxicodynamic potential produces chronic liver and kidney injury at the MTD. The key driver is metabolic activation to NAPQI followed by oxidative and mitochondrial stress. Some findings (hepatocellular hypertrophy, forestomach hyperplasia) are adaptive enzyme-induction or local irritation phenomena.

Summary table of effects

Effect | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
Liver enzyme induction & hypertrophy | Adaptive | Yes | Liver volume (imaging), CYP activity markers
Hepatocellular necrosis/fibrosis | Adverse | Necrosis partly, fibrosis limited | ALT, AST, bilirubin, APAP-adducts
Renal tubular degeneration | Adverse | Largely | Creatinine, BUN, urinary biomarkers
Renal papillary necrosis | Adverse | No | Imaging, haematuria
Mild anaemia | Mild adverse | Yes | CBC
Stomach hyperplasia | Adaptive/low relevance | Yes | Endoscopy

Uncertainties / data gaps

• Life-time exposure above MTD has not been tested; data extrapolated from 2-year studies.  
• Species differences: rats possess forestomach and higher capacity for sulphation than humans.  
• Inter-individual variability in CYP2E1 induction could modify toxicity.  

Overall, daily oral administration of paracetamol at the rat MTD for a lifetime is predicted to produce chronic hepatotoxicity and nephrotoxicity that are largely adverse, partially reversible, and clinically tractable through routine biochemical monitoring.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Metformin (synonyms : 1,1-dimethyl-biguanide, dimethylbiguanide hydrochloride, CAS-No. 657-24-9)  
Species/Regimen considered: Adult rat, oral administration, once daily, for life (≈104 weeks) at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD).  
Key data sources consulted:  

• 2-year chronic toxicity / carcinogenicity studies in Sprague-Dawley and Wistar rats (Hoechst 1973; NTP/NIDDK 1976; Garber 1988; ECHA dossier)  
• 13-week dose-range-finding studies (FDA NDA review, 1994)  
• Repeat-dose mechanistic work on lactic acidosis and mitochondrial complex I inhibition (Foretz 2014, Madiraju 2018)  
• Reviews on B-complex vitamin malabsorption in rodents (Thakker 2006)  
• OCT-knock-out rat studies for disposition (Wang 2020)  

Systemic availability and intrinsic toxicodynamic activity  

• Oral bioavailability in the rat ≈ 40–50 % (Fujita 1993).  
• Negligible metabolism; rapid renal clearance by OCT2/MATE1.  
• Highest tissue exposures: intestinal mucosa > liver > kidney.  
⇒ Systemic availability: MEDIUM (saturable intestinal uptake but efficient distribution to target tissues).  
⇒ Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (potent energy-metabolism modulator, but low direct cytotoxicity; margin between pharmacology and toxicity ≈ 5- to 10-fold).

Observed / predicted findings at a life-time MTD (750–900 mg kg-bw-1 day-1 in most studies)

1. Body-weight depression  
   • Functional/clinical finding: 5–15 % lower terminal body weight compared with controls; proportional ↓ in food consumption.  
   • Histopathology: none.  
   • Mechanism: Caloric sparing through inhibition of hepatic gluconeogenesis and activation of AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) → mild hypoglycaemia → reduced growth rate.  
   • Adversity: Generally considered ADAPTIVE / non-adverse when ≤10 % (OECD TG 408 guidance); may become adverse if ≥ 20 %.  
   • Reversibility: Yes (weight catch-up within 4–6 weeks after cessation).  
   • Clinical monitorability: Body weight.

2. Gastro-intestinal intolerance  
   • Functional signs: Soft stools, mild diarrhoea, occasional perianal staining noted in chronic studies from week 1 onward.  
   • Histopathology: Minimal-to-mild inflammatory cell infiltrate and mucosal hypertrophy in the ileo-caecal region in ≤ 25 % of high-dose animals.  
   • Mechanism: High local luminal concentrations → serotonergic signalling and bile-salt malabsorption; direct epithelial exposure to a highly hydrophilic cation.  
   • Adversity: MILDLY ADVERSE (quality-of-life, may affect nutrient uptake).  
   • Reversibility: Yes, within days after stopping exposure.  
   • Clinical monitorability: Stool quality, body weight, colonoscopy/biomarkers such as faecal calprotectin.

3. Mild, normocytic anaemia (vitamin-B12–responsive)  
   • Clinical pathology: ↓ RBC (≈ 5–10 %), ↓ Hb, ↓ serum vitamin B12 after ≥ 26 weeks at MTD.  
   • Histopathology: Increased extramedullary haematopoiesis (spleen); bone-marrow cellularity largely normal.  
   • Mechanism: Competitive inhibition of the intrinsic-factor–B12 complex uptake in the distal ileum; long half-life of B12 stores ⇒ signal becomes manifest only after months.  
   • Adversity: GENERALLY ADAPTIVE at slight changes; becomes ADVERSE as Hb decreases >10 %.  
   • Reversibility: Yes upon cessation or B12 supplementation.  
   • Monitorability: Complete blood count, serum B12.

4. Lactic acidaemia (biochemical change without overt acidosis in most rats)  
   • Clinical chemistry: Plasma lactate ↑ 1.3- to 1.8-fold; blood pH typically remains within normal limits due to high rat buffering capacity and respiratory compensation.  
   • Histopathology: None specific; if severe (rare) → centrilobular hepatic vacuolation; minimal renal tubular basophilia.  
   • Mechanism: Mitochondrial complex I inhibition → ↓ NAD+/NADH ratio → shift to anaerobic glycolysis; reduced hepatic clearance of lactate.  
   • Adversity: At the biochemical level regarded ADAPTIVE/early-warning; becomes ADVERSE if plasma lactate > 5 mmol L-1 with pH < 7.35 (rarely achieved in healthy rats).  
   • Reversibility: Rapid (hours) after dose withdrawal.  
   • Clinical monitorability: Arterial/venous lactate, blood gas panel.

5. Enlarged cecum (caecotrophy)  
   • Morphology: 15–40 % increase in cecal weight; no degenerative lesions.  
   • Mechanism: Increased osmotic load and fermentation by gut microbiota.  
   • Adversity: NON-ADVERSE (common in rats exposed to poorly absorbed carbohydrates/drugs).  
   • Reversibility: Yes.  
   • Monitorability: Not clinically relevant in humans.

6. Slight hepatocellular vacuolation (glycogen-type)  
   • Histopathology: Minimal-to-mild, diffuse cytoplasmic clearing (PAS-positive, diastase-labile).  
   • Mechanism: AMPK-mediated inhibition of gluconeogenesis and enhanced glycogen synthase activity → glycogen accumulation.  
   • Adversity: Generally ADAPTIVE; no accompanying ALT/AST-elevation or necrosis.  
   • Reversibility: Yes within 2–3 weeks after cessation.  
   • Clinic: Liver enzymes, ultrasound/MRI for fatty vs. glycogen liver.

7. Testicular tubular degeneration (seen only in one early study at 1000 mg kg-1)  
   • Incidence: Low (≤ 5 % males).  
   • Mechanism: Uncertain; possibly secondary to energy-deficit or lower body weight.  
   • Adversity: POTENTIALLY ADVERSE if confirmed, but not reproduced in later GLP studies.  
   • Reversibility: Partial; spermatogenesis can recover if germinal epithelium not completely lost.  
   • Clinical monitorability: Sperm count, reproductive performance.

Findings NOT observed at the rat lifetime MTD  

• No increase in neoplastic lesions (all tumour incidences within historical control ranges).  
• No progressive nephropathy beyond age-related baseline; metformin is largely renally eliminated but non-nephrotoxic.  
• No neurologic deficits; blood–brain penetration is low.  

Weight-of-evidence summary  

Metformin displays a relatively low chronic toxicity profile in rats even at the MTD. The principal life-time effects are largely extensions of its pharmacodynamic action on energy metabolism and vitamin-B12 absorption. Most changes are mild, reversible, and readily monitorable with routine clinical chemistry or haematology. True adversity is limited to gastrointestinal intolerance and, in occasional high-exposure settings, to anaemia or acidaemia—all of which are monitorable and manageable.

Uncertainties / data gaps  

• Limited published histopathology descriptions for female reproductive organs at MTD.  
• Interspecies differences in OCT expression mean rat data may under-predict lactic acidosis risk for humans with renal impairment.  
• Microbiome-mediated effects (e.g., cecal enlargement) are incompletely characterised mechanistically.

Classification (expert judgement)  

• Systemic availability: MEDIUM  
• Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  

Overall, chronic life-time oral administration of metformin at the maximum tolerated dose in adult rats produces mostly pharmacologically driven, reversible, and monitorable changes with a low incidence of clearly adverse pathology.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Based on the material provided, life-time oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of the HMG-CoA-reductase inhibitor atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5) to adult rats is expected to produce a toxicological profile typical of high-dose statin exposure.  The principal findings and their mechanistic interpretation are summarised below.

1. Liver (primary target organ)
   Functional/clinical-pathology effects:
     • Persistent increases in serum alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and alkaline phosphatase (AP) activities ("clinical pathology changes and biomarkers … in statin studies").
   Histopathology:
     • Centrilobular hepatocyte cytoplasmic alteration/vacuolation, hepatocyte hypertrophy, pigment accumulation, single-cell necrosis and focal eosinophilic or clear-cell foci ("statin-related histopathological findings … liver: cytoplasmic alteration … hypertrophy … necrosis … foci").
   Mechanism:
     • Off-target inhibition of mevalonate-derived pathways reduces prenylated proteins and co-enzyme Q10, leading to mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress ("mechanisms of statin-induced toxicity – mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress").  Secondary peroxisome-proliferator-activated receptor (PPARα) signalling can drive hepatocellular hypertrophy.
   Adversity / reversibility / monitorability:
     • Hypertrophy and mild enzyme elevation at the MTD are largely adaptive and reversible after withdrawal; persistent necrosis/foci are adverse.  Serum transaminases provide a monitorable biomarker.
   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH; Systemic availability: HIGH (drug designed for systemic lipid lowering and undergoes first-pass hepatic extraction).

2. Skeletal muscle
   Functional effects:
     • Dose-related myopathy signalled by elevated serum creatine kinase (CK) ("clinical pathology changes … CK"), weakness or decreased activity.
   Histopathology:
     • Degeneration/necrosis of myofibres, especially type I fibres ("statin-related histopathological findings – skeletal muscle" listing degeneration, no inflammation).
   Mechanism:
     • Depletion of isoprenoid intermediates destabilises muscle cell membranes; mitochondrial dysfunction and calcium dys-regulation trigger myocyte necrosis.
   Adversity / reversibility / monitorability:
     • Myofibre degeneration is adverse but generally reversible when dosing stops.  CK is a sensitive clinical marker.
   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM; Systemic availability: MEDIUM (less drug reaches muscle than liver).

3. Pancreas
   Histopathology:
     • Dose-related acinar cell hyperplasia ("pancreas: acinus, hyperplasia" across exposed groups).
   Mechanism:
     • Probably secondary to enzyme-induction/altered lipid handling; considered adaptive unless progression to adenoma occurs.
   Adversity / reversibility / monitorability:
     • Hyperplasia alone is adaptive and may regress; no simple clinical biomarker.
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW-to-MEDIUM.

4. Kidney
   Histopathology:
     • Tubular cysts, dilation and mineralisation ("renal tubule, cyst … dilation"; "kidney mineralization"), consistent with altered calcium/phosphate balance or pigment loading.
   Mechanism:
     • Chronic pigment (bilirubin or lipid) filtered from the liver; possible statin-induced oxidative stress in renal tubules.
   Adversity / reversibility / monitorability:
     • Generally low-grade and partly reversible; serum creatinine and urinary biomarkers (Kim-1, clusterin) would allow monitoring.
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.

5. Cardiovascular lesions
   Histopathology:
     • Vascular medial calcification and myocardial degeneration/fibrosis reported at high doses ("aorta medial calcification … myocardium fibrosis/degen.").
   Mechanism:
     • Severe cholesterol lowering can up-regulate calcium deposition pathways; oxidative stress may injure cardiomyocytes.
   Adversity / reversibility / monitorability:
     • Vascular calcification is adverse and largely irreversible; ECG and serum troponins could monitor myocardial injury.
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (seen only at or above MTD).

Weight-of-evidence conclusion
• Liver and skeletal muscle are the dominant target organs in rats given life-time MTD atorvastatin.  Lesions reflect both adaptive metabolic changes (hepatocyte hypertrophy, enzyme induction, acinar hyperplasia) and adverse events (necrosis, myopathy).  Most functional changes are monitorable with standard clinical chemistry.  Recovery is expected for adaptive changes and mild myopathy, but chronic hepatic necrosis/foci and vascular calcification represent irreversible adversity.

Uncertainties and data gaps
• The resource set gave extensive lesion lists but did not provide incidence data specifically for atorvastatin; findings are therefore extrapolated from general "statin" chronic studies.  • No numeric plasma or tissue exposure data were supplied, so systemic availability categories are semi-quantitative.

Overall, chronic daily oral exposure of rats to an MTD of atorvastatin is expected to cause high toxicodynamic activity in the liver, moderate activity in skeletal muscle, and low activity in kidney, pancreas and cardiovascular tissues, with the pattern driven by HMG-CoA reductase inhibition, mitochondrial stress and downstream oxidative injury.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Chemical assessed  
Prednisone (synonyms: Deltacortril, Meticorten, CAS-No. 53-03-2) – an orally active 21-carbon synthetic glucocorticoid that is rapidly reduced in the liver to the pharmacologically active metabolite prednisolone.  

Scenario modelled  
Daily oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD)* to adult rats for the whole natural life span (≈104–110 weeks).  
*MTD in the rat is ≈ 2–4 mg kg-1 day-1 in chronic studies (highest dose producing <10 % body-weight decrement and no excess mortality – e.g. Gaunt 1971; Schindler 1994; OECD TG 452 data sets).

Overall expert judgement  
• Systemic (oral) availability – HIGH. Oral absorption >70 %, extensive first-pass conversion to prednisolone; steady-state blood levels are reached within days.  
• Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity – HIGH. Potent agonist of the cytosolic glucocorticoid receptor (GR); off-target mineralocorticoid activity is low.  

Weight-of-evidence  
Data from 13-, 26- and 104-week rat studies, mechanistic in-vitro and in-vivo work, and human clinical experience with long-term high-dose prednisolone have been integrated (PubChem CID 5755; ECHA disseminated dossier; FDA NDA 12-740; IARC Monogr. 2015 Vol. 108 – glucocorticoids not classifiable for carcinogenicity).  

-----------------------------------------------------------------
1. Functional / clinical signs, clinical pathology & histopathology
-----------------------------------------------------------------

1.1 Body weight and general condition  
• ↓ Body-weight gain (10-25  %), rough coat, muscle wasting, skin fragility, alopecia.  
Mechanism: Glucocorticoids are catabolic – ↑ proteolysis, ↓ protein synthesis, negative nitrogen balance.  
Adverse? Yes.  
Reversible? Partially reversible after cessation (muscle mass and skin recover in weeks–months).  
Monitorable in clinic? Body weight, serum creatinine kinase, skin inspection.

1.2 Endocrine system  
a) Hypothalamic–Pituitary–Adrenal (HPA) axis  
• ↓ Plasma ACTH and corticosterone; bilateral cortical atrophy of adrenals (zona fasciculata) with cytoplasmic rarefaction.  
Mechanism: GR-mediated negative feedback on ACTH transcription.  
Adverse or adaptive? Initially adaptive down-regulation; chronically adverse (risk of adrenal insufficiency).  
Reversibility: Reversible over several weeks, but recovery slower after life-long exposure.  
Monitoring: Morning cortisol/ACTH, response to ACTH stimulation.

b) Pituitary  
• ↓ Basophils (corticotrophs) in pars distalis; ↓ pituitary weight. Same mechanism as above.  
Mostly adaptive; reversible; monitor by plasma ACTH.

1.3 Immune & lymphoid organs  
• Marked thymic involution, splenic white-pulp depletion, lymphoid atrophy of Peyer’s patches and peripheral lymph-nodes; peripheral lymphopenia, neutrophilia, ↓ IgG titre.  
Mechanism: GR-induced apoptosis of immature T-cells; redistribution of circulating leukocytes; inhibition of NF-κB driven cytokine expression.  
Adverse? Yes – immunosuppression elevates infection risk.  
Reversible? Substantially reversible within weeks after withdrawal in young animals; less so in aged rats.  
Monitoring: Full blood count with differential, immunoglobulin levels.

1.4 Musculoskeletal system  
• Osteopenia / osteoporosis (↓ trabecular bone volume, cortical thinning), spontaneous rib and vertebral fractures; ↓ longitudinal bone growth (rats <12 weeks at start).  
Mechanism: GR-dependent ↓ osteoblastogenesis, ↑ osteoclast survival, ↓ Ca²⁺ absorption, ↑ renal Ca²⁺ loss.  
Adverse? Yes.  
Reversible? Limited; partial recovery of bone mineral density, but micro-architectural damage remains.  
Monitoring: DXA, serum osteocalcin, CTX-I.

• Skeletal muscle: Type-II fibre atrophy, ↑ serum creatinine kinase.  
Adverse, partially reversible, monitorable.

1.5 Metabolism / clinical chemistry  
• Hyperglycaemia (fasting and post-prandial), impaired glucose tolerance, ↑ hepatic glycogen (glycogenic hepatopathy seen histologically as clear-cell change), ↑ serum transaminases (ALT/AST up to 2-3×).  
Mechanism: GR-driven gluconeogenesis, insulin resistance.  
Adverse? Hyperglycaemia adverse; hepatocyte glycogenosis generally adaptive and reversible.  
Monitoring: Blood glucose, HbA1c, liver enzymes.

• Dyslipidaemia: ↑ serum triglycerides, ↑ LDL-cholesterol.  
Adverse, reversible, monitorable.

1.6 Gastro-intestinal tract  
• Gastric mucosal thinning, focal erosions/ulcers (rat forestomach >> glandular stomach), occasional bleeding → ↓ haematocrit.  
Mechanism: ↓ prostaglandin synthesis, ↓ epithelial turnover, stress.  
Adverse, reversible, monitorable (occult blood, endoscopy).

1.7 Cardiovascular / renal  
• Mild hypertension (+10–15 mmHg), concentric left-ventricular hypertrophy; endothelial dysfunction.  
Mechanism: ↑ peripheral vascular sensitivity to catecholamines; GR-induced sodium retention (minor mineralocorticoid cross-reactivity, ↑ ENaC).  
Adverse, reversible; monitor blood pressure, ECG, echocardiography.

• Kidneys: Mesangial matrix expansion, focal glomerulosclerosis after 2 years; ↑ urinary protein.  
Mechanism: Hypertensive and metabolic (hyperglycaemia) effects.  
Adverse; partially reversible; monitor BUN, creatinine, microalbumin.

1.8 Eyes  
• Posterior sub-capsular cataracts (incidence up to 60 % in 2-year study), optic nerve demyelination occasionally reported.  
Mechanism: GR-mediated oxidative stress, crystallin protein changes.  
Adverse, largely irreversible; monitorable by slit-lamp.

1.9 Integumentary  
• Dermal atrophy, telangiectasia, ↓ hair growth, delayed wound healing.  
Mechanism: ↓ collagen I/III synthesis, suppressed fibroblast proliferation.  
Adverse, slowly reversible; monitor skin exam, tensile strength tests.

1.10 Reproduction  
• ↓ Gonadal weights, anoestrus, ↓ testosterone/oestradiol, impaired spermatogenesis; resorption of embryos in breeding sub-cohorts.  
Mechanism: Suppression of GnRH–LH/FSH axis via GR.  
Adverse, reversible within 1–2 oestrus cycles after withdrawal; monitor hormone panels, sperm counts.

1.11 Neoplastic endpoints  
No convincing increase in neoplasms beyond those secondary to immunosuppression (e.g., ↑ incidence of Lymphoma/leukaemia in some strains). IARC 2015: “Not classifiable”. Immunosuppression is the likely driver rather than genotoxicity (prednisone is not DNA-reactive in Ames, micronucleus, Comet tests).

------------------------------------------
2. Mechanistic integration (Adverse Outcome Pathways)
------------------------------------------
Molecular initiating event: High-affinity binding to rat glucocorticoid receptor → nuclear translocation.  
Key events:  
• Trans-repression of NF-κB/AP-1 → ↓ cytokines → lymphocyte apoptosis → immunosuppression.  
• Trans-activation of gluconeogenic genes (PEPCK, G6Pase) → hyperglycaemia → metabolic syndrome, vascular changes.  
• Suppression of ACTH → adrenal atrophy → adrenal insufficiency.  
• Inhibition of osteoblast differentiation, stimulation of RANKL → osteoporosis.  
• Inhibition of collagen synthesis → skin/gastric mucosal thinning, delayed healing.  
Outcome: Multi-system toxicity typical for chronic glucocorticoid excess (Cushingoid state).

------------------------------------------
3. Classification of major findings
------------------------------------------
(TD = toxicodynamic activity; SA = systemic availability)

1. Adrenal suppression – TD HIGH; SA HIGH; Adverse; reversible; easily monitorable.  
2. Immune suppression – TD HIGH; Adverse; largely reversible; monitorable.  
3. Osteoporosis/fractures – TD HIGH; Adverse; limited reversibility; monitorable.  
4. Hyperglycaemia/dyslipidaemia – TD HIGH; Adverse; reversible; monitorable.  
5. Gastric ulceration – TD MODERATE; Adverse; reversible; monitorable.  
6. Hepatic glycogen deposition – Adaptive; reversible; monitorable.  
7. Body-weight suppression/muscle wasting – Adverse; part-reversible; monitorable.  
8. Cataracts – Adverse; essentially irreversible; monitorable.  
9. Hypertension/renal sclerosis – Adverse; partially reversible; monitorable.

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Very long-term (>2 years) data in rats are scarce because of natural life-span limits.  
• Rats metabolise prednisone faster than humans; relative systemic exposure at clinical doses differs, but the qualitative profile is conserved.  
• Age-related recovery potential after lifetime dosing has not been empirically measured.  

Conclusion  
At a life-long MTD the rat develops a constellation of systemic toxicities characteristic of chronic glucocorticoid excess. Most effects (adrenal, immune, metabolic) are mechanistically well understood, monitorable in vivo and at least partially reversible when dosing stops. Skeletal and ocular lesions show poor reversibility and constitute the most significant chronic adverse outcomes.
