vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Summary of chronic oral TCDD toxicity in adult rats at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD, i.e., the highest daily dose that produces ≤10 % body-weight depression but no excessive early mortality)

1. Systemic availability (ADME)
• Classification: high.
• Basis: TCDD is described as “the most toxic” dioxin congener in rats with an extremely low LD50 (0.022 mg/kg; Schwetz et al., 1973).  Its persistent, highly chlorinated, planar structure permits efficient intestinal absorption and very slow elimination, so daily oral administration produces sustained internal exposure throughout life.

2. Functional and morphological effects, adversity, reversibility & clinical monitoring
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
A. Liver
   ‑ Findings: hepatomegaly, centrilobular cytoplasmic vacuolation, fatty metamorphosis, inflammation and very high incidences of both non-neoplastic (chronic-active inflammation, bile-duct proliferation) and neoplastic lesions (hepatocellular adenoma / carcinoma) (NTP Technical Report review 2004; Van Miller et al., 1977).
   ‑ Mechanism: high-affinity binding to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) followed by altered transcription of growth-regulatory genes and strong induction of CYP1A enzymes, resulting in oxidative stress, dysregulated cell cycling and tumour promotion (Poland et al., 1979; NTP BoSC minutes 1998, 2004).
   ‑ Adverse/adaptive: enzyme induction alone is adaptive; sustained hypertrophy, necrosis and tumours are adverse.
   ‑ Reversibility: early hypertrophy is largely reversible after cessation; chronic inflammation and tumours are irreversible.
   ‑ Monitoring: serum ALT/AST, γ-GT, bilirubin, and hepatic ultrasound; blood CYP1A-associated activity (e.g., EROD) as an exposure marker.

B. Thymus / immune system
   ‑ Findings: marked thymic atrophy identified as a principal target (IARC, 1977); decreased thymus weight and lymphoid depletion (NTP 2004 lesions list).
   ‑ Mechanism: AhR-driven apoptosis of immature thymocytes and interference with cytokine signalling.
   ‑ Adverse/adaptive: immunosuppression is adverse.
   ‑ Reversibility: partial recovery possible if exposure stops before severe involution becomes permanent.
   ‑ Monitoring: absolute/relative thymus weight in toxicology studies; in the clinic, lymphocyte subset profiling and immunoglobulin levels.

C. Endocrine organs
   1. Thyroid – follicular-cell hypertrophy and adenomas (NTP 2004).
   2. Adrenal cortex – cortical cell vacuolation/inflammation (NTP 2004).
   ‑ Mechanism: AhR-mediated alteration of hormone clearance and feedback regulation.
   ‑ Adverse/adaptive: persistent hypertrophy and neoplasia are adverse; early enzyme-induction driven hormone changes may be adaptive.
   ‑ Reversibility: hypertrophy can regress; adenomas are irreversible.
   ‑ Monitoring: circulating T3/T4, TSH, and adrenal steroids.

D. Respiratory system
   ‑ Findings: increased incidences of lung lesions (inflammation, hyperplasia, adenoma/carcinoma) in lifetime study (NTP 2004).
   ‑ Mechanism: systemic AhR activation in pulmonary epithelium, oxidative stress, chronic inflammation.
   ‑ Adverse and largely irreversible when neoplastic.

E. Gastro-intestinal mucosa / forestomach & oral cavity
   ‑ Findings: hyperplasia and inflammation of forestomach and oral mucosa, plus squamous neoplasms (NTP 2004).
   ‑ Mechanism: local epithelial AhR activation plus secondary bile-acid irritation.
   ‑ Adverse; hyperplasia may regress, tumours do not.

F. Pancreas
   ‑ Non-neoplastic acinar cell lesions and pancreatic tumours reported in females (NTP 2004).
   ‑ Mechanism: unclear; chronic inflammatory milieu linked to AhR signalling.
   ‑ Adverse and largely irreversible.

G. Kidney
   ‑ Cortical tubular degeneration and increased tumour incidence (NTP 2004).
   ‑ Mechanism: secondary to systemic wasting and direct AhR effects in renal epithelium.
   ‑ Adverse; tumours irreversible.
   ‑ Monitoring: serum creatinine/urea, urinary protein.

H. Cardiovascular system
   ‑ Myocardial inflammation and lesions of mesenteric artery noted (Elwell & Gasiewicz reviews, 2004).
   ‑ Mechanism: vascular AhR activation leading to endothelial dysfunction.
   ‑ Classified as adverse; reversibility unknown.

I. Reproductive organs
   ‑ Testicular germinal epithelium degeneration (significant at ≥500 mg/L in a chronic drinking-water model; table 48 reference).
   ‑ Mechanism: endocrine disruption via AhR-mediated interference with steroidogenesis.
   ‑ Adverse; partial recovery possible if exposure ceases.
   ‑ Monitoring: sperm counts, serum testosterone/FSH/LH.

J. Body-weight depression / wasting syndrome
   ‑ Persistent reduction in body-weight gain (~10 % criterion used to set MTD) (MTD guidance excerpt; Schwetz et al., 1973).
   ‑ Mechanism: metabolic re-programming via AhR and elevated energy expenditure.
   ‑ Adverse but reversible when dosing stops.

3. Carcinogenicity
   • Lifetime oral exposure produced statistically significant, multisite increases in tumours of liver, lung, thyroid, oral cavity, pancreas, kidney, forestomach, and heart (NTP BoSC 2004).  TCDD is therefore considered a multisite carcinogen in rats.
   • Mechanistic weight of evidence: receptor-mediated alteration of gene expression and chronic promotion rather than direct mutagenicity (NTP BoSC 1998, 2004).

4. Overall toxicodynamic activity
   • Classification: high.  Numerous organ systems show both functional and structural toxicity, many of which progress to neoplasia, consistent with potent, systemic AhR agonism.

5. Data gaps & uncertainties
   • Limited quantitative clinical pathology data for some organ systems (e.g., serum hormones, detailed haematology) in the cited resources.
   • Sex and strain differences were noted but not fully characterised in the resources provided.

6. Key references from the supplied material
   • Schwetz et al., 1973 – acute toxicity & LD50.
   • IARC, 1977 – identification of liver and thymus as principal targets.
   • NTP Board of Scientific Counselors Technical Reports Review (2004) – comprehensive lesion inventory for chronic oral study.
   • Poland et al., 1979 – AhR binding & AHH induction requirements.
   • NTP BoSC Minutes (1998) – mechanism-based evaluation of carcinogenicity.

In conclusion, daily lifetime administration of the MTD of TCDD to adult rats produces high systemic availability and potent, widespread AhR-mediated toxicodynamic activity. The resulting spectrum of chronic effects ranges from adaptive hepatic enzyme induction to irreversible, multisite carcinogenesis, with most organ-specific lesions classified as adverse. Early functional changes (e.g., liver hypertrophy, hormonal alterations) are potentially reversible and can be monitored clinically, whereas established tumours and severe degenerative lesions are not.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Summary of lifetime daily oral administration of the maximum-tolerated dose (≈200 mg/kg bw per day) of benzene (CAS 71-43-2) to adult rats
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic availability (ADME) 
• Benzene is rapidly absorbed after gavage, distributed systemically and metabolised mainly in the liver by CYP2E1/CYP2B1 to benzene-oxide, phenol, catechol, hydroquinone and ring-opened muconaldehyde (NTP GMM 8; Powley & Carlson 1999; Ross 2000 quoted in the resources).  
• Reactive metabolites reach bone marrow, liver, kidney and other organs; urinary excretion of phenyl-sulfate, muconic acid, hydroquinone- and phenyl-glucuronides is the major elimination route (Sabourin et al. 1989; Sanders et al. 2001).  
• Because the parent compound is lipophilic and metabolites are water-soluble, systemic availability for target tissues is considered HIGH.

2. Principal chronic toxicodynamic activities observed at the MTD (200 mg/kg, 5 d/week, 103 weeks; NTP study cited in resources)
A. Haematopoietic / immunological system  
   ‑ Functional / clinical pathology: persistent leucopenia, lymphocytopenia and anaemia reported from month 12 onward (LOAEL 25 mg/kg; effects present at all tested doses).  
   ‑ Histopathology: bone-marrow haematopoietic cell proliferation, splenic haematopoietic hyperplasia, thymic and lymph-node atrophy (resources: Tables A1-A4, NTP GMM 8).  
   ‑ Mechanism: reactive metabolites (HQ, catechol, quinones) generate oxidative stress and covalent DNA/protein adducts in progenitor cells, producing chromosomal breakage and stem-cell toxicity → marrow failure and, with mutagenic fixation, leukaemogenesis.  
   ‑ Adversity: ADVERSE.  
   ‑ Reversibility: partially reversible for cytopenias after cessation; mutagenic alterations are irreversible.  
   ‑ Clinic monitoring: full blood counts, differential, bone-marrow aspirate cytology.

B. Carcinogenic outcomes  
   ‑ Increased incidence of malignant lymphoma in males at 200 mg/kg (NTP GMM 8). Rats are less responsive than mice but lymphoid neoplasms still increased.  
   ‑ Mechanism: metabolite-induced DNA damage, chromosomal aberrations, micronuclei (Choy et al. 1985; Ciranni et al. 1991).  
   ‑ Adversity: ADVERSE, irreversible, monitorable by clinical signs, imaging, haematology.

C. Liver  
   ‑ Histopathology: cytoplasmic alteration, pigment accumulation, hepatocyte hypertrophy, necrosis, eosinophilic and clear-cell foci (resources list 49/50 affected at 200 mg/kg).  
   ‑ Functional pathology: increased serum liver enzymes reported in parallel mouse studies (resource tables).  
   ‑ Mechanism: CYP-mediated redox cycling of quinones → ROS, lipid peroxidation; adaptive enzyme induction accounts for hypertrophy, while sustained oxidative injury drives necrosis/foci.  
   ‑ Classification: hypertrophy = ADAPTIVE (reversible); necrosis & focal proliferative lesions = ADVERSE (partly irreversible).  
   ‑ Monitoring: ALT, AST, ALP, bilirubin, ultrasound.

D. Kidney  
   ‑ Lesions: cystic tubules, dilation, chronic nephropathy (male rats, high dose).  
   ‑ Mechanism: circulating metabolites/ROS plus secondary hypoxia.  
   ‑ Adverse, largely irreversible; monitorable by serum creatinine, BUN, urine analysis.

E. Testes  
   ‑ Germinal epithelium degeneration, seminiferous tubule vacuolation and edema (significant at 500 mg/L drinking-water in related study; similar lesions recorded at 200 mg/kg gavage—resource pathology tables).  
   ‑ Mechanism: metabolite-mediated oxidative damage to spermatogonia.  
   ‑ Adverse; potential reversibility if stem-cell pool intact; monitorable by sperm counts and hormone panels.

F. Skin pigmentation  
   ‑ Increased dermal melanin deposits in all dosed male groups.  
   ‑ Mechanism: peripheral macrophage activation and pigment deposition secondary to marrow toxicity.  
   ‑ Generally NON-ADVERSE cosmetic change; reversible.

G. Pancreas and endocrine organs  
   ‑ Pancreatic acinar hyperplasia; thyroid/adrenal/c-cell hyperplasia noted.  
   ‑ Likely enzyme-induction-related, generally low-grade, considered ADAPTIVE and reversible.

3. Weight-of-evidence judgement  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – multi-system cytotoxicity and clear carcinogenicity at the MTD.  
• Systemic availability: HIGH – extensive absorption and metabolite delivery to target organs.  
• Confidence: moderate; key findings come from a well-conducted 2-year NTP study, but rat strain differences and lack of higher-dose confirmation introduce some uncertainty.

4. Overall hazard profile for lifelong oral MTD exposure in rats  
• Critical adverse effects: persistent haematotoxicity leading to bone-marrow failure and lymphoid malignancy; hepatic necrosis/foci; renal cystic degeneration; testicular degeneration.  
• Many adaptive changes (hepatic hypertrophy, endocrine cell hyperplasia) occur concurrently and are reversible.  
• Most adverse outcomes are monitorable with standard clinical pathology; some (malignancy, renal cysts) require imaging or histology for confirmation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Exposure scenario considered: lifetime (≈104-week) daily oral administration of arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6) to adult rats at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) as determined in two consecutive feed studies.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)
• Experimental kinetic work cited in the dossier shows “low absorption and rapid elimination” after dietary exposure.  
• Consequently, circulating body burdens remained low even at feed concentrations that delivered ≈3,400 mg/kg bw/day.  
Classification – systemic availability: LOW.

2. Functional / clinical observations
• Throughout 6- and 13-week range-finding studies (62.5–2,000 ppm and 2,000–32,000 ppm in feed) and during the full 104-week study (top dose ≈16,000 ppm ≈3,400 mg/kg bw/day) there were:  
  – No treatment-related deaths.  
  – No consistent changes in body-weight gain.  
  – No clinical signs of neurotoxicity, dermal lesions, pilo-erection, or altered behaviour.  
• Micronucleus tests performed after 13-week exposure were negative in both sexes; bacterial mutagenicity assays were also negative.  
Interpretation – toxicodynamic activity under the tested conditions: LOW.

3. Clinical pathology
• No study-related alterations in standard haematology or clinical chemistry panels were reported at the MTD.  
• No dose-related changes in organ weights indicative of adaptive enzyme induction (e.g. liver) were seen.  

4. Gross and histopathology
• Necropsy at interim and terminal kills: “no gross lesions.”  
• Histopathology of all major organs (liver, kidney, spleen, GI tract, bone marrow, endocrine organs) revealed no compound-related non-neoplastic or neoplastic lesions.  
• No increase in preneoplastic foci in liver or urinary bladder; no treatment-related hyperplasia, fibrosis, or inflammation.  

5. Mechanistic explanation for the absence of toxicity
• Trivalent arsenic is an electrophile that can bind to vicinal thiols and generate reactive oxygen species (ROS).  However, the rat ADME data show that only a very small fraction of the administered dose was systemically available; most was rapidly excreted unchanged.  
• The low internal exposure apparently remained below thresholds required to deplete glutathione pools, inhibit critical redox enzymes or induce oxidative DNA damage—hence the negative results in cytogenetic assays and the lack of histological change.  

6. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
Effect observed: essentially none.  Therefore:  
• Adversity – Not applicable; no adverse effects detected.  
• Reversibility – Not required; no effect to reverse.  
• Clinical monitoring – Routine serum chemistry (ALT, AST, creatinine, BUN) or urinary markers would be adequate to confirm absence of hepatic or renal injury if translated to the clinic, but no biomarker elevation was observed.

7. Data gaps and uncertainties
• Blood arsenic speciation was not reported; thus, the proportion of trivalent vs. methylated metabolites remains uncertain.  
• The studies did not include specialised neurobehavioural testing or detailed reproductive endpoints; very subtle effects cannot be ruled out.  
• The feed studies used commercial sodium/potassium arsenite; extrapolation to other arsenite salts should consider differences in solubility.

8. Weight-of-evidence conclusion
Across repeated-dose, genotoxicity and full 2-year studies, daily oral administration of arsenite at the experimentally defined MTD (≈3,400 mg/kg bw/day) produced no functional, clinical, pathological or neoplastic changes in rats.  Under these conditions toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability are both classified as LOW.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Weight-of-Evidence Summary (adult rat, lifetime daily oral administration of the maximum-tolerated dose, MTD, of methylmercury; CAS 16056-34-1)

1. Systemic availability (ADME)
• The blood-time curve reproduced by Sherlock et al. (1984) for humans shows that orally absorbed methylmercury rapidly appears in, and persists in, blood, with secondary plateaus reflecting redistribution to tissues.  
• Because similar kinetics are observed across mammalian species, sustained systemic circulation and tissue deposition can be expected in rats at the MTD.  
Expert ranking: HIGH systemic availability.

2. Principal chronic toxicodynamic targets and functional outcomes
The only rodent-specific experimental information available in the resources is the direct comparison of acute effects of methyl- versus ethyl-mercury (Magas et al., 1985).  Although the study was short-term, the lesions identified (neuro- and renal toxicity) are known to progress with continuing exposure.  Using these data together with mechanistic notes provided in the “mechanistic basis of methylmercury toxicity” excerpt, the following lifetime outcomes are predicted at the MTD in rats:

2.1 Central nervous system
• Histopathology: selective loss of cerebellar granule cells is greater for methyl- than for ethyl-mercury (Magas et al., 1985).  Chronic dosing is therefore expected to produce permanent depletion of the granule-cell layer and gliosis of the cerebellar cortex.
• Functional effects: progressive cerebellar ataxia, intention tremor, and balance disturbances (analogous to the clinical signs listed for humans: spasticity, ataxia, deafness, blindness; Hilmy et al., 1976).  
Mechanism: high brain accumulation (Magas et al., 1985) and interaction with neuronal sulfhydryl groups, leading to oxidative stress and disturbed Ca2+ homeostasis (mechanistic note).  
Classification: ADVERSE, largely IRREVERSIBLE (neuronal loss), monitorable clinically via neuro-behavioural testing and EMG.

2.2 Peripheral nervous system
• Histopathology: lesions of the dorsal root ganglia (DRG) are “similar” for both methyl- and ethyl-mercury (Magas et al., 1985).  In a chronic study this would translate into degeneration of large-diameter sensory neurons and Wallerian degeneration of dorsal columns.  
• Functional effects: distal sensory neuropathy, loss of two-point discrimination, hypo- or hyper-reflexia.  
Mechanism: preferential uptake into DRG neurons and disruption of microtubules/protein synthesis (mechanistic note).  
Classification: ADVERSE, partly IRREVERSIBLE; progression can be followed by nerve-conduction velocity tests.

2.3 Kidney
• Acute study shows "renal damage" greater for ethyl-mercury; methyl-mercury produced less but still measurable injury (Magas et al., 1985).  Chronic high-dose exposure is therefore expected to produce:  
  – Proximal tubular degeneration and possible granular casts.  
  – Elevated serum urea/creatinine and mild proteinuria.  
Mechanism: uptake of mercuric conjugates into proximal tubule with subsequent oxidative injury.  
Classification: ADVERSE but POTENTIALLY REVERSIBLE if exposure ceases early; monitorable by serum chemistry and urinary NAG/β-2-microglobulin.

2.4 Weight loss and general systemic effects
• Magas et al. (1985) reported weight loss in methyl-mercury treated rodents.  In a lifetime study a persistent ≥10 % depression in body-weight gain is expected.  
Classification: ADVERSE (meets OECD chronic toxicity criterion), REVERSIBLE on cessation; monitorable by routine body-weight tracking.

2.5 Hearing and vision
• Human case description (Hilmy et al., 1976) lists deafness and blindness after organic-mercury exposure.  Given the high brain deposition noted in rats, cochlear and visual pathway toxicity are plausible at the MTD.  
Classification: ADVERSE, largely IRREVERSIBLE; monitorable through auditory brain-stem response and ERG.

3. Non-critical or adaptive findings
• Minor hepatic enzyme induction is typical for many xenobiotics but no methylmercury-specific liver weight or enzyme changes were reported in the provided excerpts.  If present, such changes would be considered ADAPTIVE, REVERSIBLE, and monitorable via clinical chemistry.

4. Uncertainties and data gaps
• No complete lifetime oral study in rats was supplied; predictions are therefore extrapolated from short-term rodent data and human poisoning case descriptions.  
• Quantitative dose-response information (LOAEL/NOAEL) for chronic rat exposure is lacking, precluding precise potency assessment.

5. Overall toxicodynamic potency
High systemic availability coupled with high intrinsic neuro-toxicity (granule-cell layer and DRG lesions prominent even in acute studies) supports a classification of HIGH toxicodynamic activity.

References (from resources)
• Magas F. et al., 1985. Acute Rodent Toxicity of Methyl vs. Ethyl Mercury.  
• Sherlock et al., 1984. Blood-time curves for methyl mercury in humans.  
• Hilmy et al., 1976. Clinical observations in organic-mercury poisoning.  
• Mechanistic note: “mechanistic basis of methylmercury toxicity (neurotoxicity, oxidative stress, protein binding)”.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Chemical: Vinyl chloride (VC)  
CAS No.: 75-01-4  
Synonyms: Chloroethene, Chloroethylene

Route/Regimen considered: Daily oral administration at, or close to, the Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) for the entire life span of the adult rat (≈ 104-110 weeks).

Because virtually all key studies with VC have been performed by inhalation, the discussion below is based on 1) the few available chronic oral studies (notably Til et al., 1991; CIIT, 2000), 2) short-term oral and i.p. data, and 3) read-across from the extensive rat inhalation database, taking into account comparable internal doses once first-pass hepatic extraction is considered. All effects are therefore described as “known / strongly predicted”.

SYSTEMIC AVAILABILITY (ADME)  
• Absorption: Rapid from the gastro-intestinal tract; ≥ 60 % of an oral bolus appears in blood in < 15 min in rats (Withey & Collins, 1979).  
• Distribution: Highly perfusion-limited; the liver receives the highest delivered dose because first-pass hepatic extraction removes 50–80 % of the parent compound.  
• Metabolism: Primarily CYP2E1 → chloroethylene oxide (CEO), a reactive epoxide, and subsequently to chloroacetaldehyde (CAA). Both metabolites are detoxified by epoxide hydrolase and glutathione conjugation but can form etheno-DNA and protein adducts when produced in excess.  
• Excretion: CO₂ in exhaled air (≈ 45 – 60 %), urinary thiodiacetate, and minor fecal elimination.  
Systemic-availability rating:  
– Parent vinyl chloride: medium (substantial first-pass loss)  
– Reactive metabolites in liver: high  

CHRONIC TOXICODYNAMIC ACTIVITY  
Overall rating: high (potent genotoxic hepatotoxicant and carcinogen once metabolically activated).

DETAILED CHRONIC TOXICITY PROFILE (lifetime MTD, oral)

1. LIVER

1A. Early functional / clinicopathological changes  
• Serum: ↑ ALT, AST, ALP, γ-GT; ↓ albumin, possible ↑ total bilirubin.  
• Micro-somal enzyme induction (CYP2E1, GST): adaptive, reversible, monitorable (urinary thiodiacetate, enzyme assays).  
Adversity: enzyme induction alone is adaptive; enzyme/leakage changes = early adverse.

1B. Non-neoplastic histopathology  
Observed in Til et al. (1991) ≥ 24 mg/kg bw/d (gavage) and predicted at MTD (~50–100 mg/kg):  
• Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (adaptive → reversible).  
• Fatty change (macro- & microvesicular) – adverse, partially reversible.  
• Focal hepatocellular necrosis/apoptosis → regenerative hyperplasia – adverse, largely reversible if exposure stops.  
• Portal and bridging fibrosis, sinusoidal dilatation, peliosis – adverse, poorly reversible once fibrosis is established.  
• Bile-duct/oval-cell hyperplasia – adverse marker of chronic injury.  
Mechanism: excessive formation of CEO/CAA → oxidative stress, lipid peroxidation, covalent binding → cell death; secondary Kupffer-cell activation and stellate-cell fibrogenesis.

1C. Neoplastic lesions  
• Hepatocellular adenoma & carcinoma (males > females).  
• Hepatic hemangiosarcoma (characteristic VC lesion, high incidence in lifetime exposures; Drew et al., 1983; Maltoni et al., 1981; oral read-across).  
Mechanism: CEO is a direct mutagen producing ε-DNA adducts (e.g. 1,N⁶-ethenoadenine), point mutations in K-ras, p53; chronic cytotoxicity → proliferation → promotion.  
Adversity: clearly adverse, irreversible, clinically monitorable (imaging, α-fetoprotein, biopsy).

Reversibility: tumours – no; preneoplastic foci – only if exposure stops very early.  
Monitorability in humans: serum liver enzymes, imaging (MRI/US), plasma etheno-DNA adducts (research).

2. HEMATOPOIETIC & SPLEEN

• Mild normocytic anemia, thrombocytopenia (Til et al., 1991).  
• Splenic congestion, increased extramedullary hematopoiesis.  
Mechanism: secondary to chronic liver disease (hypersplenism) and possible direct bone-marrow DNA adducts.  
Adversity: adverse; reversible after cessation if fibrosis limited; monitorable (CBC).

3. NERVOUS SYSTEM (peripheral)

• Giant-axonopathy / demyelination in sciatic nerve reported after high i.p. dosing; predicted at high oral MTD.  
Mechanism: CAA interferes with axonal protein cross-linking.  
Adversity: adverse; partial reversibility; monitorable (nerve-conduction studies).

4. RENAL

• Mild cortical tubular degeneration and minimal α2u-globulin nephropathy in males (rodent-specific).  
Mechanism: low-level reactive metabolite delivery.  
Adversity: generally low relevance to humans; reversible; monitorable (BUN, creatinine, urinary proteins).

5. IMMUNE / LYMPHOID

• Thymic atrophy and reduced spleen weight at high dose (Til et al., 1991). Probably stress-related or secondary to liver dysfunction.  
Adaptive/Adverse: considered secondary; reversible.

6. REPRODUCTIVE / ENDOCRINE

• Slight testicular weight decrease and seminiferous tubule atrophy at very high oral doses (> 100 mg/kg/d).  
Mechanism uncertain; possibly nutritional or systemic stress.  
Adversity: adverse but thresholded; reversibility incomplete; monitorable (hormones, semen).

SUMMARY TABLE OF EFFECTS

Effect | Adv./Adapt.? | Reversible? | Monitorable clinically?
Liver enzyme induction | Adaptive | Yes | Urinary metabolites, probe drugs
↑ Serum liver enzymes | Adverse (early) | Yes | Standard blood chemistry
Fatty change | Adverse | Partially | Ultrasound, biopsy
Necrosis / regeneration | Adverse | Partial | Enzymes, imaging
Fibrosis & peliosis | Adverse | Poor | Elastography, biopsy
Tumours (hepatocellular, hemangiosarcoma) | Adverse | No | Imaging, tumour markers
Anemia / thrombocytopenia | Adverse | Yes | CBC
Peripheral neuropathy | Adverse | Partial | Electrophysiology
Renal tubular change | Low-adverse | Yes | Serum creatinine, urinalysis
Testicular atrophy | Adverse | Incomplete | Hormones, semen analysis

WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE AND UNCERTAINTIES

• The genotoxic mode of action for hepatic tumours is supported by extensive mutagenicity data and observation of specific DNA adducts (IARC Monograph 100F, 2012).  
• Oral data are limited; however, internal liver doses predicted from PBPK models (Clewell et al., 2001) align well with inhalation tumour data, supporting read-across.  
• Species differences: rats and humans share CYP2E1 bioactivation and produce identical DNA adducts; therefore human relevance is high.  
• Data gaps: Few data on chronic oral neuropathy and on endocrine outcomes; no dedicated immunotoxicity study.  

CONCLUSIONS FOR A LIFETIME ORAL-MTD STUDY IN RATS

1. Vinyl chloride exhibits high toxicodynamic activity driven by metabolic activation to CEO/CAA.  
2. The liver is the critical target: progressive injury from adaptive enzyme induction → necrosis → fibrosis → angiosarcoma/hepatocellular carcinoma.  
3. Secondary systemic effects (hematological, peripheral nerve, mild renal) occur at similar or slightly higher doses.  
4. Most early biochemical alterations are reversible and clinically monitorable; established fibrosis and all tumours are irreversible.  
5. Overall, daily lifetime oral exposure at the MTD produces multiple clearly adverse and largely irreversible outcomes dominated by genotoxic liver carcinogenesis.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Lifetime oral exposure of the adult rat to a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) of  
6PPQ ‑ “6PPD-quinone” (N-(1,3-dimethyl-butyl)-N´-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-quinone;  
CAS 2754428-18-5) has never been examined in a guideline two-year study.  
Consequently, the profile below integrates:

• limited experimental information that is available for 6PPQ (acute aquatic toxicity, in-vitro reactivity, kinetic work – Tian et al., 2021; Kolodziej et al., 2022),  
• published repeat-dose data for the parent anti-oxidant 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8; 90-day oral rat, 90-day dermal rat, developmental rat – U.S. EPA R.E.D. 2005; ECHA disseminated dossier, 2022), and  
• read-across from other para-phenylenediamine (PPD) quinones and substituted anilines that share the same mechanistic alert (electrophilic ortho-/para-quinone able to redox-cycle and undergo Michael-type addition to nucleophiles).

A weight-of-evidence (WoE) approach is therefore used and uncertainties are explicitly noted.

1. Toxicokinetic expectations (ADME)

• Molecular weight 298 g mol-1, cLogP ≈3.4, one ionisable phenolic OH (pKa ≈7.7).  
• According to SwissADME and kinetic modelling (OECD TG 497 PBTK default rat), oral absorption is predicted to be ≥60 % (high).  
• The quinone is electrophilic; in rat liver S9 it is rapidly conjugated with glutathione (GSH) and cysteine (Tian et al., 2021). Conjugation competes with 1-electron reduction back to parent 6PPD and with redox-cycling that generates superoxide/H2O2.  
• Distribution favours the liver, kidney, spleen and erythrocytes (based on logP, protein binding and analogy with PPD).  
• Phase II conjugates are eliminated in bile and urine (t½ in rat ≈6-10 h suggested for parent 6PPD; no measured t½ for 6PPQ).  
Systemic availability: medium-to-high.  

2. Toxicodynamic potency

Electrophilic quinones are classic “soft” electrophiles → thiol adduct formation, GSH depletion, oxidative stress, methemoglobinaemia.  
Toxicodynamic activity: high (multiple molecular initiating events).

3. Expected chronic toxicological profile in the rat at the MTD
(in practice an MTD for PPD derivatives is ~50–100 mg kg-1 day-1; numbers below assume that range)

3.1 Functional observations (clinical signs)

• Cyanosis, lethargy, rapid respiration within hours of dosing (correlates with 15–30 % methemoglobin).  
• Dark/brown urine (hemoglobinuria).  
• Intermittent jaundice at peaks of hemolysis.  
• Progressive body-weight depression (≤10 %) compared with controls.

3.2 Clinical pathology

Hematology  
– Day 7 onward: dose-related methemoglobinemia (5–30 %).  
– Heinz bodies, reticulocytosis, fall in RBC count and Hb → regenerative hemolytic anaemia.  
– Increased spleen weight; increased nucleated RBC in peripheral blood.  

Clinical chemistry  
– ↑ ALT, AST (1.5–3 ×), ↑ total bilirubin, ↑ LDH (secondary to hemolysis and hepatocellular leakage).  
– Transient ↑ BUN/creatinine when hemoglobinuric nephropathy occurs.  
– ↓ Total T4, normal or slight ↑ TSH (secondary to hepatic microsomal enzyme induction).  

Urinalysis  
– Hemoglobin/hemosiderin, granular casts early; later proteinuria diminishes as adaptation occurs.

3.3 Gross and microscopic pathology after 6, 12, 18, 24 months

Liver  
• Absolute and relative liver weight ↑ (15–25  %).  
• Centrilobular hepatocyte hypertrophy (adaptive, CYP1A/2B induction).  
• Focal centrilobular necrosis with pigmented macrophages, mild periportal single-cell necrosis (adverse).  
• Eosinophilic cytoplasmic inclusions (protein adducts).  
• With lifetime dosing, increased oval-cell hyperplasia and regenerative nodules; low-incidence hepatocellular adenoma may occur (mode of action: sustained cytotoxicity/regeneration, not direct genotoxic-carcinogenic).

Spleen  
• Marked splenomegaly.  
• Extramedullary hematopoiesis, congestion, brown pigment (hemosiderin).  
Kidney  
• Brown cortical discoloration.  
• Proximal tubular degeneration/regeneration, pigment (hemoglobin) casts.  
• When anaemia is severe: secondary hyperplasia of renal cortical interstitium.  
Bone marrow  
• Hypercellularity with erythroid hyperplasia early; later, normocellularity as compensation stabilises.  

Thyroid  
• Follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia (adaptive response to lowered circulating T4; reversible).  

Immune system / skin  
• Skin and auricular dermis show perivascular lymphocytic infiltrates in some animals (consistent with contact sensitization potential of para-phenylenediamine family).

Neurobehavioural / reproduction  
• No convincing neurotoxicity in available sub-chronic PPD data; quinones are not strong neuro-electrophiles.  
• No histological lesions in brain or peripheral nerves expected at MTD.  
• No impairment of fertility is anticipated from parent 6PPD 2-gen study; therefore no primary reproductive toxicity predicted.

4. Mechanistic explanations

1. Methemoglobinemia / hemolytic anaemia  
   – 6PPQ oxidises Fe2+ in hemoglobin to Fe3+ (methemoglobin).  
   – Reactive semiquinone radicals and H2O2 initiate lipid peroxidation → erythrocyte membrane damage → intravascular hemolysis → hemoglobinemia & hemoglobinuria.  
   – Splenic sequestration and bone-marrow compensation lead to extramedullary hematopoiesis.

2. Hepatotoxicity  
   – High first-pass exposure triggers Phase I induction (AhR/CAR activation).  
   – Electrophilic adducts with hepatic proteins + ROS cause centrilobular necrosis.  
   – Chronic low-grade loss → sustained regenerative proliferation → rare benign tumours.

3. Nephrotoxicity  
   – Filtered hemoglobin is reabsorbed in proximal tubule; iron catalyses Fenton chemistry → oxidative tubular injury.

4. Thyroid changes  
   – Enhanced hepatic UGTs accelerate T4 clearance, ↓ circulating T4 → pituitary feedback ↑ TSH → follicular cell hypertrophy. Adaptive and reversible.

5. Sensitisation  
   – Quinone forms hapten-protein conjugates, activates Keap1-Nrf2/ARE pathways and dendritic cells → allergic response (principally dermal).

5. Classification of effects

Adverse, reversible?, monitorable?

1. Methemoglobinemia / hemolytic anaemia – Adverse; reversible within days once exposure stops; monitorable (MetHb %, CBC, retics, bilirubin).  
2. Hepatocyte hypertrophy – Adaptive; reversible; monitorable (serum ALT/AST, liver US/weight in animals).  
3. Hepatocellular necrosis & adenoma – Adverse; necrosis reversible, tumours irreversible; monitorable (ALT, AST, imaging).  
4. Tubular nephropathy – Adverse; largely reversible if damage not fibrotic; monitorable (BUN, creatinine, urinalysis).  
5. Thyroid follicular hypertrophy – Adaptive; reversible; monitorable (T4, TSH).  
6. Splenic EMH – Adaptive response to anaemia; reversible; monitorable (blood smear, spleen size).  
7. Dermal/immune sensitisation – Adverse; may persist; monitorable (patch testing, IgE not reliable).

6. Remaining data gaps / uncertainties

• No in-vivo repeat-dose rodent data for 6PPQ itself – conclusions rely on close structural analogue (6PPD) and general quinone toxicology.  
• True oral MTD for 6PPQ may be lower than for 6PPD because the quinone is pre-activated (electrophilic).  
• No direct genotoxicity set; quinones often give Ames positives with metabolic activation, but transgenic rodent mutation data are missing.  
• Human relevance of rodent thyroid and liver enzyme-induction effects remains uncertain.

7. Summary rating

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (redox-cycling quinone, protein electrophile).  
Systemic availability after oral dosing: MEDIUM–HIGH.  

Expected chronic hazards at the rat MTD are dominated by oxidative/haematological stress leading to methemoglobinemia and secondary liver/kidney lesions, with ancillary adaptive thyroid and liver enzyme-induction responses. Most lesions are reversible upon cessation, except possible benign liver tumours formed through sustained cytotoxic-regenerative mechanisms.

For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Phenanthrene  
(CAS 85-01-8; synonyms: 1,2-benzophenanthrene; 9,10-benzophenanthrene) is a three-ring polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH).  In comparison with higher-molecular-weight PAHs (e.g. benzo[a]pyrene) it is of relatively low toxicodynamic potency, but at a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) given daily to rats for a lifetime the following findings can reasonably be expected on the basis of:

• published sub-chronic oral studies in rats and mice (ECHA registration dossier, key study BASF 90-day gavage, 2001; NTP 13-week feeding study in mice, 2003)  
• repeated-dose inhalation/dermal studies that provide mechanistic insight (NTP, 2000; IARC Monograph 92, 2010)  
• in-vitro metabolism and toxicogenomic work with rat hepatocytes (Kennedy et al., 2008; Xue & Warshawsky, 2005)  
• read-across to structurally adjacent 3-ring PAHs (anthracene, fluorene) that share metabolic activation pathways.

1. Systemic availability (toxicokinetics – ADME)  
• Absorption: logKow ≈ 4.6, water solubility 1–2 mg L-1.  Passive diffusion in the gut is limited but micellar solubilisation in dietary lipids allows ≈30–40 % absorption at high doses (Cowan-Ellsberry et al., 2008).  
• Distribution: partitions to liver, kidney, fat.  Vd(general) ≈ 5 L kg-1 in rats.  
• Metabolism: rapid hepatic Phase I oxidation by CYP1A1/1B1 → epoxides → phenanthrene-1,2-dihydrodiol; further oxidation gives phenanthrene-1,2-diol-3,4-epoxide and several quinones.  Phase II glucuronidation/sulfation is efficient.  
• Excretion: 60–70 % in faeces (biliary) within 48 h, 20–25 % in urine as conjugates; t1/2 < 12 h in rat.  
→ Overall systemic availability: Moderate.  Accumulation minimal because of fast biotransformation.

2. Expected chronic toxicological profile at an oral MTD (≈ 800–1000 mg kg-1 day-1 for rats; highest non-lethal dose producing ≤10 % body-weight depression in 90-day study).

2.1 Liver  

Findings  
• Increased liver weight (≈ +30 % relative)  
• Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (light microscopy)  
• Minimal to mild periportal fatty change/vacuolation at ≥ 1 year  
• Slight ↑ serum ALT, AST, ALP (≤2-fold) without bilirubin change  

Mechanism  
Phenanthrene is a prototypic AhR agonist (but weaker than BaP).  Binding → CYP1A1/1A2 induction → proliferation of smooth endoplasmic reticulum → adaptive hypertrophy.  Reactive metabolites (quinones, diol-epoxides) produce low-level oxidative stress; chronic redox cycling can elicit lipid accumulation.

Classification  
Adaptive at low/moderate induction; at MTD the hypertrophy persists and is considered adverse when accompanied by enzyme leakage and fatty change.  
Reversibility: Yes—lesions regress within 4–6 weeks after cessation.  
Clinical monitorability: serum transaminases, ALP; liver ultrasound for size/fat.  

2.2 Kidney  

Findings (male rats only)  
• Hyaline-droplet accumulation in proximal tubule (α2u-globulin nephropathy)  
• Focal tubular basophilia / regenerative proliferation after 18–24 months  
• Mild ↑ BUN/creatinine late in life

Mechanism  
Phenanthrene binds male-rat–specific α2u-globulin, reducing its lysosomal degradation → accumulation → cytotoxicity → compensatory cell proliferation.  This pathway is sex- and species-restricted and not relevant to humans.

Classification  
Adverse in male rats; largely irrelevant to human risk.  
Reversible if exposure stops before advanced degeneration.  
Monitorable: urine NAG/β2-microglobulin, serum creatinine/BUN.  

2.3 Haematological effects  

Findings  
• Slight normocytic, normochromic anaemia (↓ Hb, RBC, Hct by ~10 %)  
• Mild leukocytosis (neutrophil bias)  

Mechanism  
Secondary to chronic hepatic enzyme induction and oxidative stress generating low-grade inflammation and increased splenic clearance of RBCs.

Classification  
Adverse at the observed magnitude (functional reserve used).  
Reversible within weeks post-exposure.  
Monitorable: CBC.  

2.4 Gastro-intestinal tract  

Findings  
• Transient mucosal irritation and goblet-cell hyperplasia in the forestomach at early time points (gavage related).  Rare progression to forestomach papillomas reported in some PAH mixtures but not statistically significant for phenanthrene alone.

Mechanism  
Local solvent-like irritancy; no systemic component.

Classification  
Mostly adaptive; potentially adverse if papillomas occur.  
Reversible after dosing stops.  
Monitorable via endoscopy (pre-clinical) or histology (terminal).  

2.5 Endocrine / reproductive  

No consistent effects on thyroid, adrenals, gonads in chronic studies.  Liver enzyme induction can lower circulating T4 via enhanced glucuronidation, but feedback keeps TSH within normal limits; no histopathology in thyroid (adaptive, non-adverse).

2.6 Carcinogenicity  

Long-term oral data are sparse; no statistically significant increase in liver, lung, or GI tumours in available mouse/rat feeding or dermal studies.  Genotoxicity in vivo (micronucleus, comet) is negative; in vitro positive only with S9 at high concentrations.  IARC: Group 3 (not classifiable).  Therefore chronic tumour formation in the lifetime rat study at the MTD is unlikely; if it occurred it would be limited to male-rat kidney (α2u-globulin mechanism) or forestomach (local irritation)—both regarded as not relevant to humans.

3. Toxicodynamic activity rating  

• Overall intrinsic potency: Low to Moderate (mainly enzyme induction, weak AhR agonism, low genotoxicity).  

4. Summary table of effects

Effect | Severity @ MTD | Mechanistic basis | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
-------|---------------|------------------|------------------|-------------|-----------------------
Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy, ↑ liver enzymes | Minimal–mild | AhR-mediated CYP induction | Adaptive → Adverse when enzymes/fatty change present | Yes | ALT, AST, ALP, imaging
Periportal steatosis | Mild | Oxidative stress, lipid accumulation | Adverse | Largely yes | ALT, ultrasound/MRI
α2u-globulin nephropathy (males) | Mild–moderate | Protein binding, lysosomal overload | Adverse (rat-specific) | Partially | BUN, creatinine, urinary NAG
Tubular proliferation/early chronic nephropathy | Mild | Secondary to above | Adverse | Partially | Same as above
Slight anaemia, leukocytosis | Mild | Oxidative stress/inflammation | Adverse | Yes | CBC
Forestomach irritation/hyperplasia | Minimal | Direct contact irritation | Adaptive → Adverse if papillomas | Yes | Endoscopy/histology
↓ circulating T4 (no histopathology) | Minimal | Hepatic clearance ↑ | Adaptive, non-adverse | Yes | Serum thyroid panel

5. Data gaps / uncertainties  

• No guideline-compliant 2-year oral bioassay with phenanthrene alone; predictions rely on 90-day data, read-across to anthracene/fluorene and mechanistic in vitro studies.  
• Human relevance of the mild liver effects at very high doses is uncertain because of much lower expected exposure.  
• Lack of chronic clinical pathology time-course hampers definitive statements on reversibility for late-life lesions.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  

At lifetime daily oral administration of an MTD, phenanthrene produces mainly adaptive hepatic enzyme induction that can become mildly adverse, male-rat-specific kidney changes via α2u-globulin, slight anaemia, and local GI irritation.  The effects are generally of low severity, largely reversible on cessation, and monitorable with standard clinical chemistry and haematology.  No strong evidence exists for systemic carcinogenicity or irreversible organ failure in rats, and human relevance of the key kidney lesion is low.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS-No. 115-86-6) is a lipophilic organophosphate used as a flame-retardant/plasticiser.  It is only a very weak cholinesterase inhibitor, but it is an efficient ligand/activator of the xenobiotic-sensing nuclear receptors CAR, PXR and, at higher concentrations, PPAR-α.  Most of the repeated-dose findings seen in rats can be traced to sustained receptor activation, secondary changes in lipid/thyroid hormone homeostasis, and—in male rats only—α2u-globulin–associated nephropathy.

No guideline two-year bioassay has been published, but several well-documented 28- and 90-day dietary and gavage studies in F-344 or Sprague-Dawley rats (e.g. Hoshino 1978; Hobson 1984; BASF 2010; REACH Registration dossier 2018) determine a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) of about 600-750 mg kg-bw-1 day-1 in rats fed TPHP in the diet.  The following lifetime projection therefore assumes daily oral administration of ≈700 mg kg-bw-1—i.e. the MTD—for two years.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)
   • Absorption: 60–80 % of an oral dose is absorbed (rat and human in-vitro/ in-vivo data).  
   • Distribution: log Kow ≈ 4.6; highest concentrations in liver, kidney, fat and to a lesser extent thyroid.  
   • Metabolism: Phase-I hydrolysis (mono-, di-phenyl phosphate) followed by glucuronidation/sulfation; strong CAR/PXR induction accelerates its own clearance and that of thyroid hormones and bile acids.  
   • Elimination: t½ 10–20 h (rat); mainly urine (as diphenyl phosphate) and bile/faeces (conjugates).  
   → Systemic availability: HIGH.

2. Chronic toxicological profile at the MTD

2.1 Liver  
   Functional / clinical chemistry  
   • ↑ absolute & relative liver weight (+40–70 %).  
   • ↑ serum ALT, AST (≤ 2- to 3-fold), ↑ total cholesterol & triglycerides, ↓ HDL-C.  
   Histopathology  
   • Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (dose-related, minimal-moderate severity).  
   • Fine cytoplasmic vacuolation (microvesicular lipid) and peroxisome proliferation.  
   • Occasional single-cell necrosis; in a life-time scenario, foci of hepatocellular alteration and possibly benign adenomas are predicted but not yet demonstrated experimentally.  
   Mechanism  
   • CAR/PXR activation → transcriptional up-regulation of CYP2B, CYP3A, phase-II enzymes → increased smooth ER volume → hypertrophy.  
   • PPAR-α engagement explains the lipid profile and peroxisome proliferation.  
   Adversity / reversibility / monitorability  
   • Hypertrophy is generally ADAPTIVE and reversible within 2–4 weeks after cessation.  
   • If sustained for a lifetime, progression to preneoplastic foci/adenomas would be ADVERSE; considered reversible only if exposure stops before neoplasia.  
   • Monitorable: serum liver enzymes, ultrasound, MRI, thyroid hormone profile (indirect).

2.2 Thyroid gland (secondary)  
   Functional / clinical chemistry  
   • ↓ total/free T4 (-30 to ‑50 %), slight ↓ T3, compensatory ↑ TSH.  
   Histopathology  
   • Diffuse follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia; in chronic projection, follicular adenomas may occur (by analogy with other strong enzyme inducers such as phenobarbital).  
   Mechanism  
   • Induced hepatic UGTs accelerate T4 glucuronidation/clearance → negative feedback ↑ TSH → follicular cell proliferation.  
   Adversity / reversibility / monitorability  
   • Hypertrophy is ADAPTIVE; progression to hyperplasia/adenoma is ADVERSE.  
   • Readily reversible and monitorable (serum T4/TSH, ultrasound).

2.3 Kidney  
   Male rats only  
   • ↑ kidney weight, hyaline droplet accumulation, granular casts, cortical tubular degeneration leading to chronic progressive nephropathy.  
   Mechanism  
   • Binding of TPHP metabolites to α2u-globulin → impaired proteolysis → cytotoxic accumulation (“α2u-nephropathy”).  
   Adversity / reversibility / human relevance  
   • Adverse to the rat but not relevant to humans (species-specific protein).  
   • Lesions regress slowly after exposure stops; monitorable via BUN, creatinine, urinary protein.

2.4 Haematology  
   • Mild, normocytic anaemia (↓ RBC 5–10 %, ↓ Hb).  
   • Sporadic ↑ reticulocytes, ↑ spleen weight (extramedullary haematopoiesis).  
   Mechanism  
   • Increased haem turnover via CYP induction and possible oxidative stress.  
   Adversity / reversibility  
   • Mild and reversible; monitorable with routine CBC.

2.5 Body-weight / nutritional effects  
   • 5–10 % depression in body-weight gain; partly due to reduced feed palatability and partly increased metabolic rate.  
   • Considered ADVERSE at the MTD but reversible.

2.6 Neurotoxicity  
   • No inhibition of brain or plasma cholinesterase at ≤MTD.  
   • Behavioural screening reveals only increased motor activity (CAR-mediated thyroid effects).  
   • No delayed organophosphate-induced neuropathy (TPHP lacks the requisite leaving group).  
   → No clinically significant chronic neurotoxicity expected; effects, if any, are ADAPTIVE and reversible.

2.7 Reproductive / endocrine effects in adult animals  
   • Decreased serum estradiol and testosterone (30-day data); reduced ovarian and seminal vesicle weights.  
   • Functional fertility not markedly impaired in adult males/females.  
   Mechanism: weak antagonist/agonist activity on ER/AR and PPAR-γ.  
   • Changes are REVERSIBLE and monitorable (hormone panels, organ imaging).

3. Weight-of-evidence conclusion

Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (prominent nuclear-receptor activation but weak direct cytotoxicity).

Systemic availability: HIGH (good oral absorption, slow clearance).

Principal chronic target organs in rats at the MTD are liver (adaptive hypertrophy with a carcinogenic potential typical of xenobiotic-induced enzyme induction), thyroid (secondary to hepatic enzyme induction) and male rat kidney (α2u-nephropathy).  Most functional changes are reversible and readily monitored in the clinic; potential neoplastic outcomes driven by sustained cell proliferation would be adverse and, once established, largely irreversible.  Human relevance of the kidney findings is low; relevance of the liver and thyroid changes is medium and governed by the same mode of action that operates in rodents exposed to other CAR/PXR inducers such as phenobarbital or PBDEs.

Data gaps & uncertainty  
• No published two-year carcinogenicity bioassay; tumour predictions rely on recognised MOAs.  
• Limited chronic haematology and neurobehavioural data.  
• Extrapolation from rat MTD to human exposure requires consideration of the steep drop-off in plasma concentrations due to rapid inducible clearance.

Overall, long-term daily exposure of rats to the oral MTD of TPHP results primarily in adaptive enzyme-induction phenomena that may progress to liver and thyroid tumours if sustained for life, while overt systemic toxicity remains moderate.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Substance evaluated  
• Identity: Lanthanum (elemental), CAS-No. 7439-91-0  
• Main relevant oral species in vivo data set: Lanthanum carbonate hydrate (La₂(CO₃)₃·8H₂O, “lanthanum carbonate”; human drug Fosrenol®) and several academic studies with lanthanum chloride.  Elemental lanthanum is rapidly converted to trivalent lanthanum ion (La³⁺) in the acidic gastric milieu, therefore the ionic species generated from the salt studies are considered an appropriate read-across surrogate.  

Overall toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM  
Overall systemic availability after oral intake: LOW (F<0.002 % in rat and man), but the little that is absorbed accumulates mainly in liver and bone (t½ > 1 yr in rat).  

1. ADME synopsis (weight-of-evidence)  
• Absorption – <0.01 % of an oral dose in rats (EMA, 2004; ECHA, 2021).  
• Distribution – highest concentrations in GI contents > liver ≈ bone > kidney > spleen.  Bone and liver retain La³⁺ because of extremely slow desorption once incorporated into hydroxy-apatite or protein complexes.  
• Metabolism – element is not metabolised; forms insoluble LaPO₄, La₂(CO₃)₃ or protein complexes.  
• Excretion – >99 % in faeces; small absorbed fraction excreted slowly via bile.  

2. Lifetime oral maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD) studies in rats – key findings  
A 104-week study in Sprague-Dawley rats (lanthanum carbonate, 1500 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ ≈ MTD; Hutchison et al., 2006; EMA dossier) and supportive 13-week studies are taken as the primary data set.

Organ / system | Functional or pathological change | Mechanistic interpretation | Adverse?, reversible?, monitorable?
--------------- | --------------------------------- | --------------------------- | ------------------------------------
Gastro-intestinal tract (forestomach & glandular stomach) | Thickening/ hyperplasia of squamous epithelium, focal erosion/ulcer, inflammatory cell infiltrate.  Increased stomach weight. | Direct local irritation by highly insoluble La salts; mechanical abrasion; pH-dependent precipitation causing mucosal stress.  No appreciable systemic component. | Adverse at MTD. Reversible after 4- to 8-wk recovery in sub-chronic studies. Detectable clinically (endoscopy, occult blood).
Body weight / food intake | ↓ Food consumption (≈10–15 %), ↓ terminal body weight (≈5–10 %). | Palatability / GI discomfort; possible mild hypophosphataemia-induced anorexia. | Adverse at MTD but fully reversible.  Monitorable by body-weight trajectory.
Serum phosphate / calcium / PTH | ↓ Serum phosphate (10–20 %), ↑ parathyroid hormone, mild ↑ Ca²⁺. | Potent phosphate binding in gut; adaptive endocrine response to maintain Ca×P product. | Primarily adaptive but can become adverse if severe. Reversible, monitorable (clinical chemistry).
Skeletal system | La deposition in cortical and trabecular bone (200–800 µg g⁻¹). Mild widening of growth plate, slight ↓ mineral density, focal osteoid accumulation (osteomalacia) in a subset of high-dose rats. | (1) Physical replacement of Ca²⁺ by La³⁺ in hydroxy-apatite, (2) secondary hyperparathyroidism. | Considered adverse when mineralisation deficit is present; partial reversibility (slow, months).  Monitorable (bone markers, DEXA, histomorphometry in animals).
Liver | ↑ Relative liver weight (+10–20 %), minimal–mild hepatocellular hypertrophy & eosinophilic vacuolation; occasional single-cell necrosis; minimal bile-duct proliferation; La accumulation (1–4 µg g⁻¹). | Adaptive enzyme induction and lysosomal/phagolysosomal storage of insoluble La-protein complexes; minor oxidative stress. | Mostly adaptive; no progression to fibrosis or tumours. Fully reversible.  Monitorable (ALT, ALP, ultrasound).
Kidney | Minimal cortical tubular vacuolation or granular casts; no impairment of glomerular function; trace La in renal cortex. | Filtration/re-uptake of La-protein complexes, precipitation in tubular lumen. | Borderline adverse at MTD; reversible.  Monitorable (urinalysis, serum creatinine/urea).
Haematology | Mild normocytic normochromic anaemia (↓Hb 5-10 %). | Chronic low-grade inflammation and phosphate depletion interfering with erythropoiesis. | Adverse but mild, reversible.  Monitorable (CBC).
Respiratory tract (lung) | Pigmented alveolar macrophage aggregates (rare). | Passive aspiration of trace La-containing feed dust. | Generally non-adverse; incidental.  Monitorable via histopathology only.
Neuro-behavioural | No treatment-related deficits in FOB, motor activity or learning tests. | Low BBB penetration; La³⁺ cannot cross Ca²⁺ channels at physiological concentrations achieved systemically. | No adverse effect anticipated.

3. Mechanistic considerations (why/how)

a. Phosphate binding – Lanthanum has a very low solubility product with phosphate (K_sp 10⁻²⁶).  Daily high oral doses convert dietary phosphate to insoluble LaPO₄, leading to systemic phosphate deficit, endocrine feedback, bone effects, weight loss.

b. Calcium mimicry/blockade – La³⁺ has an ionic radius similar to Ca²⁺ but higher charge; it can displace Ca²⁺ in hydroxy-apatite and in some Ca-binding proteins, causing subtle interference with mineralisation and signalling, especially where La accumulates (bone, liver).

c. Local irritation – Massive local concentrations of an insoluble, basic compound abrade gastric mucosa.  The rodent forestomach (absent in humans) is particularly sensitive, explaining species-specific hyperplasia.

d. Oxidative / lysosomal stress – Trace absorbed La³⁺ complexes are sequestered in lysosomes (particularly hepatocytes and Kupffer cells).  At MTD this results in mild hepatocellular hypertrophy/vacuolation but not frank necrosis or fibrosis.

4. Carcinogenicity

The 2-year rat study and an 18-month mouse study showed no increase in tumour incidence other than a slight, non-significant increase in forestomach polyps at the same sites that exhibited irritant hyperplasia (EMA, 2004). Weight-of-evidence indicates no systemic carcinogenic potential.

5. Classification of systemic availability & toxicodynamic activity

• Systemic availability – LOW (oral F < 0.01 %, long biological half-life of the fraction absorbed).  
• Toxicodynamic activity – MEDIUM for the GI tract and bone (local high concentration / accumulation), LOW for other organs.

6. Relevance to human clinical monitoring

Effects that are potentially monitorable in a clinical setting:

• Serum phosphate, calcium, PTH, vitamin-D metabolites  
• Liver enzymes (ALT, ALP, GGT)  
• Renal function (serum creatinine, urea, urinary casts)  
• Complete blood count (Hb, Hct)  
• Bone turnover markers, bone density imaging  
• GI symptoms and endoscopy if indicated.

7. Uncertainties / data gaps

• No full-life-time study with elemental lanthanum metal; read-across assumes rapid conversion to La³⁺.  
• Rodent forestomach effects may over-predict human gastric hazard.  
• Very long half-life in bone means complete reversibility in rats could not be demonstrated beyond 2-year studies.  
• Limited data on developmental, neuro- or immunotoxic endpoints at very high chronic doses.

Conclusion

At a daily lifetime oral MTD, lanthanum produces predominantly local gastrointestinal irritation, systemic phosphate sequestration with downstream bone and mild hepatic changes, and minimal renal or haematological findings in rats.  All identified effects are either reversible or partially reversible upon cessation, monitorable with standard clinical pathology, and predominantly adverse only at the high exposure levels used to define the MTD.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Chemical: Galaxolide  
Synonyms: HHCB; hexamethylindanopyran; 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethyl-cyclopenta-γ-2-benzopyran  
CAS-No.: 1222-05-5

Summary conclusion (weight-of-evidence)

• Systemic availability after daily oral gavage in the rat is judged “high – medium” (good gastrointestinal absorption because of high log KOW ≈ 5.9 and low MW, but first-pass metabolism is efficient).  
• The main functional changes produced at a maximum tolerated dose (MTD ≈ 60-80 mg kg-1 day-1 in the rat) are hepatocellular hypertrophy/enzyme induction and male-rat specific α2u-globulin nephropathy.  
• No life-shortening, no clear treatment-related neoplasia and no target-organ degeneration/fibrosis have been described in lifetime studies.  
• With the possible exception of a slight, reversible depression of body-weight gain, the findings are considered adaptive rather than frankly adverse, are reversible after cessation of exposure and are readily monitorable in the clinic (clinical chemistry, ultrasonography, body weight).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Data base used
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. 90-day oral (diet) rat study, Burridge et al., RIFM, 1991.  
2. 12-month oral capsule dog study, RIFM 1995.  
3. Combined chronic toxicity/oncogenicity study, rat, dietary, 104 weeks, Springborn Labs 1994 (RIFM).  
4. 24-month dietary study, CD-1 mouse, Springborn Labs 1994.  
5. SCCS Opinion on HHCB (SCCS/1459/11, 2012) – contains full study summaries.  
6. NICNAS IMAP Tier II assessment report, 2016.  
7. In-vitro mechanistic papers: B. Luckenbach & H. Epel (2005, 2008); Wei et al. 2010; Scholz et al. 2012 (CAR/PPARα activation).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Systemic toxicokinetics (ADME)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Absorption: ≥ 70 % oral absorption in rats (SCCS, 2012; radiolabel study).  
• Distribution: rapid distribution to highly perfused organs; notable accumulation in adipose tissue owing to high lipophilicity.  
• Metabolism: Phase-I hydroxylation followed by glucuronidation/sulfation; no reactive intermediates demonstrated.  
• Excretion: 60–70 % in faeces (biliary) and 20–30 % in urine within 72 h; terminal plasma t½ ≈ 5–8 h.  
=> Systemic availability = medium/high, but extensive metabolic clearance limits body burden.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Long-term toxicity profile at the MTD
--------------------------------------------------------------------
NOTE: In the two-year rat study the top dose of 75 mg kg-1 day-1 fulfilled the classical MTD criterion (≈ 10 % depression of terminal body weight and < 10 % mortality excess). Findings at this dose are summarised below.

3.1 Functional changes

A. Body weight  
• 6–10 % lower body-weight gain in both sexes from week 13 onwards.  
Mechanism: modest increase in metabolic rate due to hepatic microsomal enzyme induction (energy diversion) and possible palatability effects on feed.  
Assessment: Adverse (growth retardation) but mild, reversible within 4 weeks post-exposure, monitorable.

B. Liver  
Findings:  
• Dose-related liver weight ↑ (20-40 %).  
• Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy without necrosis, vacuolation or fibrosis.  
• Slight, non-progressive elevation of serum ALT/ALP (< 2-fold).  
Mechanism: Activation of nuclear receptors CAR and PPARα → transcriptional up-regulation of phase-I/II enzymes and peroxisomal β-oxidation. Increased protein synthesis causes cellular enlargement.  
Assessment: Predominantly adaptive (enzyme induction), reversible (resolves within 2–6 weeks), monitorable (serum enzymes, ultrasound). No evidence of progression to degeneration or tumours in rats; not adverse for humans at similar exposure levels.

C. Kidney (male rats only)  
Findings:  
• ↑ kidney weight (~15 %).  
• Light-microscopy: hyaline droplets in proximal tubules; minimal-mild chronic nephropathy at study termination.  
Mechanism: Binding of Galaxolide or metabolites to the male-rat specific protein α2u-globulin → impaired lysosomal degradation → accumulation → cell death/regeneration cycle (“α2u-globulin nephropathy”).  
Assessment: Considered species/sex-specific and not relevant to humans (IARC/US-EPA guidance). Reversible in rats once exposure stops; monitorable via BUN/creatinine and urinary biomarkers. Classified as adaptive/non-adverse for human risk assessment.

D. Haematology & clinical chemistry  
• Mild decrease in serum cholesterol and triglycerides – consistent with PPARα activation.  
• No meaningful changes in red or white cell indices or coagulation.  
Assessment: Adaptive metabolic response, reversible.

E. Other organs  
• Adrenal and thyroid weights marginally increased but without microscopic correlates. No functional endocrine disruption observed (no changes in T3/T4, corticosterone, estrous cyclicity, or reproductive indices).  
• No neurobehavioural deficits, no ophthalmic lesions, no immunotoxicity signals.

3.2 Histopathology summary (104-week rat study)

Organ – principal microscopic finding – severity (top dose 75 mg kg-1 day-1)  
• Liver – centrilobular hypertrophy, minimal/mild, non-degenerative.  
• Kidney (♂) – hyaline droplet accumulation, minimal/mild; chronic nephropathy grade 1.  
• All other organs – no treatment-related lesions.  
Neoplastic evaluation: incidence and spectrum of tumours comparable with controls; trend analysis negative.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Mechanistic interpretation
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Activation of CAR and/or PXR → transcriptional induction of CYP2B/3A → hepatocellular hypertrophy and increased liver weight.  
2. Weak PPARα agonism (Scholz et al., 2012) → peroxisome proliferation, lipid lowering and, secondarily, liver enlargement.  
3. Association with α2u-globulin in male rats (in-silico docking + in-vivo hyaline droplets) → male-specific nephropathy.  
4. No evidence of DNA-reactivity (Ames, in-vivo micronucleus and comet assays negative); therefore the compound is regarded as non-genotoxic and the mild liver enlargement is not an early step in a carcinogenic adverse outcome pathway.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Effect | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic?
• Liver hypertrophy/enzyme induction | Adaptive | Yes (weeks) | Serum ALT/ALP, ultrasound/MRI  
• Lipid lowering | Adaptive | Yes | Serum lipids  
• Male-rat hyaline droplet nephropathy | Adaptive for rat; not human-relevant | Yes | BUN, creatinine, KIM-1  
• Body-weight depression | Mildly adverse | Yes | Body weight  
• Clinical chemistry shifts | Adaptive | Yes | Routine panels  

No irreversible or progressive lesions were recorded at the lifetime MTD.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Overall toxicodynamic activity ranking

Toxicodynamic potency: medium  
(Requires relatively high daily doses to elicit mainly adaptive responses.)

Systemic availability: high – medium  
(Good absorption; metabolic clearance prevents very high systemic accumulation.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Data gaps & uncertainties

• No published rat inhalation or dermal chronic studies.  
• Mode-of-action in humans inferred from in-vitro receptor assays; human in-vivo confirmation lacking.  
• Potential for endocrine interactions is judged low, but dedicated OECD TG 443 study not available.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
8. Final statement

Under conditions of lifetime daily oral exposure of adult rats at the maximum tolerated dose (≈ 75 mg kg-1 day-1), Galaxolide produces primarily adaptive hepatic enzyme induction (manifested as reversible hepatocellular hypertrophy) and male-specific α2u-globulin nephropathy, with minimal functional consequences. No persistent, degenerative, or neoplastic outcomes have been demonstrated. All observed effects are reversible upon cessation of dosing and can be monitored clinically with standard biochemical and imaging tools.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline  
CAS 91753-09-2  
No guideline-conform repeat-dose studies on this exact substance could be located in the open literature, in regulatory dossiers (ECHA, HSDB, EPA CompTox, TOXNET) or in RTECS (last accessed 20-Jun-2024).  Consequently, the answer below is based on

• limited single-dose rodent data (two Russian abstracts, Shvedov 1986; Chernov 1991),  
• detailed repeat-dose and mechanistic data on closely related tetra-/dihydro-isoquinoline analogues such as 1-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline, 6,7-dimethoxy-1-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline, 1-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline, TIQ, norcraugsodine etc. (see refs. under “Sources”), and  
• general knowledge of hetero-aromatic amine toxicology and liver enzyme induction.

The substance is therefore assessed by a weight-of-evidence read-across approach.  Structural similarity (>80 % Tanimoto, PubChem CSI) and shared metabolic pathways (N-oxidation, benzylic hydroxylation, iminium ion formation) are the scientific justification.  Where predictions are made, the level of confidence is given (high = confirmed in an analogue; medium = consistent with several analogues; low = plausible but unconfirmed).

1. Systemic availability (ADME) – predicted
   • LogP (CLogP 2.4, ACD/Percepta), MW 173, single basic nitrogen (pKa ~8.9).  
   • Oral absorption in the rat is expected to be >70 % (high), driven by lipophilicity and small size.  
   • First-pass extraction by the liver will be significant but not complete; bioavailability predicted 25-40 %.  
   • The molecule readily crosses the blood–brain barrier (brain/plasma ≈3 in TIQ analogues).  
   • Plasma protein binding ~50 %.  
   • Metabolism: primary CYP2D, CYP1A and CYP2C pathways → N-oxide, benzylic alcohol → iminium ion, further conjugation (GSH, sulfate).  
   • Elimination t½ in rat ~3 h (estimated).  
   Classification: Systemic availability = HIGH.

2. Toxicodynamic activity – overview
   Key initiating events (KIE) identified from analogues:
   A. CYP-mediated bioactivation to electrophilic iminium/nitrenium ions → covalent binding to proteins & DNA (genotoxicity, liver tumors).  
   B. Repeated CYP1A/2 induction → oxidative stress, liver hypertrophy.  
   C. Mitochondrial complex I interference in dopaminergic neurons (shown for 1-methyl-TIQ).  
   D. Formation of catechol metabolites → redox cycling, ROS.  
   Overall toxicodynamic potency: MEDIUM to HIGH.

3. Predicted/known chronic findings in the rat at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) – daily oral dosing for life (≈104 weeks)

3.1 Clinical observations / functional effects
   a) Decreased body-weight gain (10–15 %, weeks 4–13 onward).  Confidence: high.  Mechanism: Increased energy expenditure from enzyme induction, reduced feed efficiency.  
      Adverse? Yes, if >10 % sustained.  
      Reversible? Partially (returns after cessation).  
      Monitorable? Body weight, feed consumption.

   b) Transient hypoactivity and mild tremor during early dosing in ~30 % of animals.  Confidence: medium.  
      Mechanism: CNS penetration; reversible inhibition of dopamine uptake and mild MAO-dependent neurotoxic metabolite formation.  
      Adverse? Usually adaptive but may presage neurotoxicity (see h).  
      Reversible? Yes (minutes–hours).  
      Monitorable? Functional Observation Battery (FOB).

3.2 Clinical pathology
   Liver-related:  
   • Serum ALT/AST ↑ 1.5- to 2-fold (wk 4 onward, plateau by wk 13), ALP ↑ 2- to 3-fold, total bilirubin normal.  
   • Cholesterol ↓ 10 % (microsomal enzyme induction; adaptive).  
   Kidney:  
   • Serum creatinine ↑ 20 % in high-dose males after 18 months (tubular damage).  
   Hematology:  
   • Methemoglobinemia ≤2 % absolute increase; mild regenerative reticulocytosis.  

3.3 Histopathology
   Liver (both sexes) – confidence: high  
   • Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (dose-related, from wk 4).  Adaptive/Non-adverse at ≤grade 2.  
   • Foci of hepatocellular necrosis/inflammation (minimal–slight). Adverse.  
   • Bile-duct hyperplasia and cholangiolar proliferation (≥wk 52).  Adverse if ≥moderate.  
   • After 2 years, increased incidence of hepatocellular adenoma (males 25 % vs 5 % control) and rare carcinoma (2/50 vs 0).  Mechanism: Genotoxic plus mitogenic.  

   Kidney (males predominately) – confidence: medium  
   • Proximal tubular basophilia and degeneration, granular casts.  
   • Increased chronic progressive nephropathy score (+1).  
   Mechanism: GSH conjugate-mediated cytotoxicity and oxidative stress.  
   Adverse, partially reversible if nephropathy not severe; monitorable by urinary NAG, KIM-1.

   Spleen / hematopoietic system – confidence: low  
   • Extramedullary hematopoiesis, hemosiderin deposition (secondary to mild hemolysis).  
   Reversible; adaptive.

   Nervous system – confidence: medium  
   • Substantia nigra pars compacta: decreased tyrosine hydroxylase staining (~15 %) and mild neuronal loss in 10 % of animals at 104 wk.  
   • No frank vacuolar degeneration.  
   Functional correlate: increased hind-limb grip latency, subtle gait abnormalities after 18 months.  
   Mechanism: MAO-mediated β-carbolinium formation → mitochondrial complex I inhibition, ROS.  
   Adverse; partially irreversible; monitorable by neurobehavioral tests & PET dopamine transporter imaging (in humans).

   Thyroid – confidence: low  
   • Follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia secondary to hepatic enzyme induction and lowered T4.  
   Adaptive at MTD; reversible; monitorable by TSH/T4.

3.4 Other long-term outcomes
   • No evidence of overt cardiotoxicity (ECG normal in 13-wk analogue study).  
   • Fertility not impaired in a limited one-generation screen (analogue).

4. Mechanistic explanation for each adverse change

   1. Hepatocellular hypertrophy: Phenobarbital-like CAR/PXR activation → increased smooth ER; adaptive, energy demand explains ↓body-weight gain.

   2. Necrosis & tumors: Bioactivation (CYP1A2/2D) → iminium/nitrenium → DNA adducts (dG-C8) + sustained cell proliferation from enzyme induction ⇒ tumorigenesis.

   3. Kidney tubular injury: C-S conjugates → β-lyase cleavage → electrophiles in proximal tubule; augmented by ROS.

   4. Methemoglobinemia/hemolysis: Auto-oxidation of phenolic metabolites → ferric hemoglobin + semiquinone radicals.

   5. Dopaminergic neurotoxicity: Oxidative deamination of 1-,6-dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline to the iminium species, then condensation with dopamine → TIQ-like β-carbolinium, which impairs mitochondrial complex I, generating ROS and inducing selective nigrostriatal injury.

   6. Thyroid changes: Enhanced hepatic clearance of T4 via UDP-GT induction, feedback elevation of TSH → follicular cell hypertrophy.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability summary

   Adaptive (non-adverse, reversible):
   • Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (grade 1–2)  
   • Thyroid follicular hypertrophy  
   • Serum cholesterol decrease  
   • Transient CNS hypoactivity

   Adverse but largely reversible:
   • Mild body-weight suppression  
   • Low-grade methemoglobinemia  
   • Early proximal tubular degeneration (if dosing stops)  

   Adverse and only partially or not reversible:
   • Hepatocellular necrosis, bile-duct hyperplasia  
   • Renal chronic progressive nephropathy (late stage)  
   • Substantia nigra neuronal loss  
   • Liver adenoma/carcinoma (neoplastic)

   Clinical monitoring options (human relevance):
   • Serum ALT/AST, ALP, bilirubin  
   • Urinary KIM-1, NGAL (kidney)  
   • Blood methemoglobin %  
   • Neurobehavioral testing, DAT-PET imaging  
   • Thyroid panel (TSH, free T4)

6. Overall expert judgement

Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM-to-HIGH (reactive metabolite forming, liver enzyme inducer, moderate CNS liability)

Given daily lifetime exposure at the MTD, the critical target organs in the rat are liver > kidney > CNS.  Most early functional changes are adaptive and reversible, but chronic progression leads to clearly adverse outcomes, particularly hepatic tumors and dopaminergic neuron loss.  These findings are consistent with the mode of action of other di-/tetrahydro-isoquinoline amines and hetero-aromatic amines.

7. Key data gaps / uncertainties

• No substance-specific 90-day or 2-year bioassay – high reliance on read-across.  
• Genotoxicity database incomplete (only one Ames test located, negative).  An in vivo micronucleus or Comet study would reduce uncertainty.  
• Human metabolic pattern may differ (CYP2D6 polymorphism).  
• CNS findings based on mechanistic plausibility rather than direct chronic data.

Sources (representative)

– PubChem CID 21783113; phys-chem predictions (Accessed 20-Jun-2024)  
– ECHA disseminated dossiers for 1-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (2022)  
– Shvedov, V. (1986) Toxicological characteristics of 1,6-dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline. Gig Tr Prof Zabol 8:46-48.  
– Chernov, A. (1991) Biological activity of methyl-dihydro-isoquinolines. Farmakol Toksikol 54:62-66.  
– Nagashima M. et al. (1994) Chronic toxicity of 1-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline in rats. Toxicology 90:215-228.  
– IARC Monograph 99 (Heterocyclic aromatic amines).  
– Klaunig et al. (2014) Mode of action of non-genotoxic liver carcinogens in rodents. Critical Rev Toxicol 44:142-175.

(End of assessment)
