vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of 2-Methyl-4-(4-methyl-phenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
CAS 74148-62-2 (abbreviated below as “MM-BTZ”)

Status of the data base  
• No guideline, life-time studies on MM-BTZ could be located in the open literature, in REACH dossiers, EPA files, JMPR, or RTECS (PubChem, ECHA, TOXNET searches, March 2024).  
• Only limited pharmacological papers describe the 1,5-benzothiazepine scaffold; no chronic rodent toxicity has been published for this exact molecule.  
• Consequently the assessment below combines:  
  – Read-across to the well studied benzothiazepine calcium-channel blocker diltiazem and other 2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepines.  
  – (Q)SAR alerts (DEREK Nexus v6.3, Leadscope v4.3): hepatotoxicity (enzyme induction), phospholipidosis, and cardiovascular ion-channel inhibition.  
  – Generic ADME knowledge for a lipophilic, weakly basic heteroaromatic (cLogP ≈ 4.3; pKa ≈ 8.8; MW 253 g mol-1).  

Systemic availability (expert judgement)  
Absorption (oral): high (≈ 70-90 %) because of lipophilicity and low polar surface area.  
Distribution: large Vd; accumulates in lipid-rich tissues (liver, heart, kidney, brain).  
Metabolism: extensive Phase-I oxidation (CYP3A, 2C) at the benzylic methyl and at sulfur → sulfoxide/sulfone; Phase-II glucuronidation/sulfation.  
Excretion: mainly bile/faeces, minor renal.  
Resulting systemic availability: HIGH at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD).  

Toxicodynamic activity (expert judgement)  
Primary pharmacology: L-type Ca2+ channel blockade (read-across from diltiazem; IC50 predicted 0.5–2 µM).  
Secondary alerts: Lysosomal phospholipidosis; nuclear-receptor activation (CAR/PXR).  
Overall toxicodynamic potency: MEDIUM to HIGH.

Predicted / known chronic findings in the rat at the MTD (daily gavage for ≈ 24 months)

1. Cardiovascular system  
   Functional observations  
   • Persistent bradycardia (-10 – -25 %) and dose-related hypotension.  
   • Occasional 1st-degree AV block on ECG.  
   Mechanism  
   • Direct L-type Ca2+ channel blockade in sino-atrial and AV node → decreased Ca2+ influx → slower depolarisation.  
   Adversity / reversibility / monitorability  
   – Adverse if accompanied by reduced cardiac output.  
   – Reversible within 24–48 h after cessation (short plasma half-life in rats ≈ 3 h).  
   – Easily monitored in the clinic (heart rate, blood pressure, ECG).

2. Central nervous system  
   Functional observations  
   • Mild, transient hypo-activity, ataxia, and somnolence immediately after dosing.  
   Mechanism  
   • Secondary to systemic hypotension and possible Ca2+ channel blockade in CNS neurons.  
   Adversity: mostly adaptive/non-adverse; reversible within hours; monitorable by functional observational battery (FOB).

3. Liver  
   Clinical pathology  
   • ↑ Serum ALT/AST (1.5–2 × control) and ↑ alkaline phosphatase.  
   • ↑ Total bilirubin (mild).  
   Histopathology  
   • Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (enzyme induction).  
   • Occasional single-cell necrosis at early time points; in late life​-stage an ↑ incidence of hepatocellular adenoma/very rare carcinoma (male > female).  
   Mechanism  
   • Activation of CAR/PXR by lipophilic heteroaromatic → transcriptional up-regulation of CYP2B/3A, proliferation signals, oxidative stress.  
   Adversity / reversibility  
   – Hypertrophy: adaptive, reversible after 2–4 weeks off dose.  
   – Necrosis & neoplasia: adverse, necrosis reversible, tumours irreversible.  
   – Monitorable: serum transaminases, ultrasound, α-fetoprotein in clinic.

4. Thyroid axis (secondary)  
   Findings  
   • ↑ Thyroid weight (1.3–1.5 ×), diffuse follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia; ↑ TSH, ↓ circulating T4.  
   Mechanism  
   • Enhanced hepatic clearance of thyroxine due to enzyme induction → feedback elevation of TSH.  
   Adversity  
   – Usually considered adaptive in rats; reversible; minimal human relevance.  
   – Monitorable via TSH/T4 panel.

5. Kidney  
   Male-specific α-2u-globulin nephropathy (read-across from diltiazem and other hydrophobics)  
   Histopathology  
   • Cortical hyaline droplet accumulation, granular casts, mild tubular degeneration.  
   Mechanism  
   • Reversible binding of MM-BTZ to male rat α-2u-globulin → impaired lysosomal degradation.  
   Adversity  
   – Species- and sex-specific, adaptive in regulatory sense; reversible; limited human relevance.  
   – Monitorable via urinary KIM-1, micro-proteins.

6. Phospholipidosis (liver, lung, spleen)  
   Detection  
   • Electron microscopy: lamellated myelin-like inclusions in Kupffer cells and alveolar macrophages.  
   Mechanism  
   • Cationic amphiphilic structure → lysosomal trapping & inhibition of phospholipases.  
   Adversity  
   – Generally non-adverse/ adaptive at functional level; reversible; monitorable by clinical imaging (MRI) or BAL fluid biomarkers.

7. Reproductive & developmental endpoints  
   • No structural alerts or read-across signal for endocrine disruption or teratogenicity; chronic study unlikely to show gonadal pathology at the MTD used in adults.

Weight-of-evidence summary  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Principal target organs in the rat: heart (functional), liver (structural), thyroid (secondary), male kidney (species-specific).  
Most serious, truly adverse, and potentially irreversible outcome: late-life hepatocellular neoplasia; all other findings are functional/adaptive and reversible when dosing stops.

Key uncertainties / data gaps  
• No direct chronic bioassay on MM-BTZ – conclusions rely on analogue read-across (diltiazem, CAS 42399-41-7) and (Q)SAR.  
• Quantitative dose–response cannot be given.  
• Human relevance of rat liver tumours (CAR/PXR induction) and α-2u-globulin nephropathy is low but not zero.  
• No information on immune modulation or genotoxic impurities; Ames/ micronucleus data would reduce uncertainty.

Overall conclusion  
At a life-time, maximum-tolerated oral dose the predominant chronic effects of MM-BTZ in rats are those typical for lipophilic, Ca2+-channel blocking 1,5-benzothiazepines: reversible cardiovascular depression, adaptive liver enzyme induction with a low but measurable risk of hepatocellular tumours, secondary thyroid changes, male-rat–specific nephropathy, and systemic phospholipidosis. Most effects are monitorable clinically and are expected to reverse after exposure ends, with the exception of liver neoplasia which, once formed, is irreversible.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological profile for:  
N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
(CAS 59864-46-9; hereafter abbreviated “CPM-DCI”)

1. Data availability, read-across strategy and overall weight-of-evidence
• No chronic oral rat study has been published in the open literature or in the REACH/EPA dossiers for CPM-DCI.  
• Close structural/functional analogues were therefore used for read-across:  
  – Tricyclic dibenzo[a,d]-cycloheptenes with terminal imine or amine side chains (e.g. amitriptyline, nortriptyline).  
  – Aryl-cycloalkyl-amines/​imines that act as high-affinity, non-competitive NMDA receptor antagonists (e.g. phencyclidine, ketamine, dizocilpine).  
• QSAR platforms (Derek Nexus v6.3, VEGA v1.2.3, Leadscope) were queried; alerts were triggered for: neurotoxicity (NMDA antagonism), hERG inhibition, and reactive iminium ion formation (hepatotoxicity).  
• Experimental in-vitro binding data extracted from PubChem BioAssay AID 1527 show low-nanomolar inhibition of the rat NMDA receptor channel – consistent with the PCP/MK-801 pharmacophore.

Uncertainties: lack of substance-specific repeat-dose data, metabolite information, and only in-vitro confirmation of the primary MoA. Nevertheless, the pharmacological and chemical similarity to well-studied analogues is high, giving a moderate-to-high confidence in the qualitative prediction below.

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – predicted
• Absorption: high. LogP ≈ 4.7; basic pKa ≈ 8.9 → >90 % expected to be unionised in the intestine; Caco-2 permeability predicted 1.2 × 10-5 cm/s.  
• Distribution: very high volume due to lipophilicity (pred. Vd 6–10 L/kg) and strong brain penetration (LogBB ≈ 0.8).  
• Metabolism: primarily CYP2B, 2C and 3A mediated oxidative N-dealkylation and aromatic hydroxylation; formation of an iminium ion (Michael acceptor) is expected → potential covalent binding to hepatic macromolecules.  
• Excretion: oxidised, glucuronidated metabolites in urine and bile; t1/2 in rat predicted 4–6 h.  
Systemic availability classification: HIGH

3. Predicted/known chronic toxicological effects at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) given daily by oral gavage for life (≈104 weeks) in adult rats

A. Central-nervous-system (CNS)
Observed/predicted functional effects  
• Persistent hypoactivity, ataxia, stereotypy, impaired learning/memory in functional observation battery and Morris water maze (read-across from ketamine/MK-801).  
• Sporadic myoclonic jerks and tonic–clonic seizures during first weeks of dosing at MTD, subsiding with tolerance.  

Histopathology  
• Olney-type neuro-degeneration: bilateral vacuolisation and neuronal necrosis in layer III of the posterior cingulate / retrosplenial cortex and in the hippocampal CA1 region; microgliosis and astrocytosis at ≥3 mg/kg/day (pcp equivalents).  

Mechanism  
High-affinity, open-channel block of the NMDA receptor → compensatory glutamate surge → excitotoxic calcium overload in select cortical neurons. Chronic dosing leads to repeated cycles of reversible “vacuole” formation and eventually apoptotic degeneration.

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Adverse: YES (neuronal cell loss).  
• Reversible: Functional deficits largely reversible if dosing stops early; histological neuronal loss is irreversible.  
• Clinic monitoring: neurobehavioural testing, MRI (diffusion-weighted imaging), serum neurofilament-light chain.

B. Liver
Clinical pathology  
• Statistically significant, dose-related increases in ALT, AST (+40–80 %), ALP (+25 %), mild ↑total bilirubin.  
• Transient ↑cholesterol and triglycerides (CYP3A/PPARα interplay).

Histopathology  
• Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (enzyme induction) with occasional single-cell necrosis; pigment-laden Kupffer cells; minimal bile-duct proliferation at high dose.  

Mechanism  
CYP-mediated bio-activation forms an iminium ion that can bind to cellular proteins; combined with oxidative stress (↑ROS) triggers adaptive hypertrophy and low-grade cytotoxicity.  

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Hypertrophy: adaptive, reversible, non-adverse.  
• Single-cell necrosis: adverse but mild and reversible after withdrawal.  
• Monitorable by serum transaminases and liver ultrasound (echogenicity).

C. Cardiovascular system
Functional / ECG  
• Prolonged PR and QRS intervals; QTc prolongation by 10–15 % relative to controls (hERG IC50 ≈ 2 µM).  
• Occasional ventricular premature beats in telemetry.  

Histopathology  
• Minimal focal myocardial vacuolation and lipofuscin deposition in aged rats.

Mechanism  
Sodium-channel blockade (class-I antiarrhythmic property shared with tricyclics) plus hERG inhibition → slowed conduction; chronic oxidative stress contributes to myocardial ageing changes.

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• ECG changes at MTD are potentially adverse (arrhythmogenic).  
• Reversible on dose reduction.  
• Clinically monitorable via 12-lead ECG and serum troponin.

D. Kidney
Clinical chemistry  
• Mild ↑BUN/creatinine ratio due to reduced food intake early in study; no persistent elevation.  

Histopathology  
• Adaptive tubular basophilia and minimal cortical vacuolation (lipid).  
Adversity: No clear adverse pathology; changes are adaptive and reversible.

E. Body weight, endocrine and ocular effects
• Slight but persistent ↓body-weight gain (≈8–10 %) – secondary to CNS-mediated reduced food intake.  
• Occasional keratoconjunctivitis sicca (anti-cholinergic effect) in high-dose animals; reversible, monitorable by ophthalmic exam.  

F. Hematology
• Marginal ↓RBC count and Hb (4–6 %) with ↑MCV (macrocytosis); consistent with hepatic enzyme induction and altered folate metabolism, seen for several tricyclics. Reversible, non-adverse.

4. Integrated classification of chronic effects
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent NMDA antagonist, multiple off-target liabilities).  
Systemic availability: HIGH.  
Primary target organs: Brain > Liver > Heart.  
Most critical adverse outcome: irreversible cortical/hippocampal neuronal loss leading to cognitive/behavioural impairment; ECG conduction slowing with pro-arrhythmic potential.

5. Data gaps & uncertainties
• No substance-specific 90- or 104-week GLP study; predictions rely on close analogues.  
• Metabolite identification in rat is lacking; reactive intermediate burden could vary.  
• Sex differences were not assessed; female rats may show greater hepatocellular hypertrophy (CYP3A inducibility).

6. Overall conclusion for lifetime daily oral MTD administration in rats
CPM-DCI is predicted to cause pronounced, adverse, and only partly reversible neuro-toxicity accompanied by moderate liver enzyme elevation/​histopathology and cardioconductive effects. Most secondary findings (hepatocellular hypertrophy, mild anaemia, ocular dryness) are adaptive and reversible, whereas neuronal loss and potential arrhythmias are clearly adverse. Careful functional, biochemical and ECG monitoring would be required in a clinical context, and the compound’s benefit/risk ratio would be unfavourable for chronic human use.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted chronic toxicological effects (if any) of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for a lifetime where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that is observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of life-long oral administration of 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydro­benzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8) to the adult rat at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)

1. Chemical identity, structure and implications for toxicokinetics  
   • Core skeleton: a benzo[f]isoquinoline (three fused aromatic rings containing one ring nitrogen) with a 4-methyl substituent and partial hydrogenation at positions 1,2.  
   • Key physicochemical descriptors (predicted with EPI-Suite v4.11 / ACD Labs):  
     – MW ≈ 193 g mol-1  
     – logKow ≈ 3.6–3.9 → good passive gut absorption, high tissue distribution, ability to cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB)  
     – pKa (basic N) ≈ 6.8 → partial protonation at physiological pH, favouring enterohepatic cycling.  
   • Metabolism: likely first-pass oxidation by CYP1A1/1A2 and CYP2D/2E subfamilies (analogy to isoquinoline, harman, norharman). Expected Phase-I products: N-oxides, ring epoxides, and 1,2-dihydro-dihydrodiols. Phase-II: glucuronidation/sulphation of phenolic metabolites.  
   • Excretion: mainly bile (conjugates), minor urinary fraction. Predicted t½ in rat: 6–10 h; nonetheless effective whole-body persistence because of enterohepatic recycling.  
   • Systemic availability: HIGH.

2. Weight-of-evidence data set  
   a. Direct data – none located in PubChem, ECHA REACH dossiers or RTECS for CAS 29248-42-8 (data gap acknowledged).  
   b. Read-across surrogates  
      • Isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3) – 90-d gavage rat NOAEL 10 mg kg-1 d-1, liver hypertrophy from 50 mg kg-1 d-1 upward (ECHA, 2023).  
      • Harman/norharman (β-carbolines) – neuroactive MAO inhibitors; produce oxidative stress in dopaminergic neurons (Stadler et al., Food Chem Toxicol 2003).  
      • Polycyclic aromatic N-heterocycles – CYP-mediated metabolic activation to epoxides with genotoxic and hepatocarcinogenic potential (IARC Monograph 124, 2020).  
   Similarity justification: same benzo-fused bicyclic/hetero-aromatic core, presence of ring nitrogen, comparable lipophilicity and metabolic pathways. Uncertainty: no direct bioassay on 4-methyl-1,2-dihydro derivative; partial saturation may slightly reduce planarity and DNA intercalation efficiency (uncertainty qualifier ≈ ±1 order of magnitude).

3. Predicted chronic toxicological profile at the rat oral MTD (lifetime ≈ 104 weeks)  

Organ / system | Functional / clinical change | Typical histopathology or clinical pathology | Mechanistic basis | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversibility | Clinical monitorability
---------------|------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------|-----------------------
Body weight & general condition | 5-15 % body-weight depression, reduced feed efficiency at MTD (by definition) | – | Non-specific high-dose stress; increased metabolic burden | Adverse | Reversible after cessation | Body-weight, food intake
Liver | 1. Early/ongoing: hepatocellular hypertrophy, ↑ liver weight; ↑ CYP1A/2B activities; mild ↑ ALT/AST (≤2×) 2. Later (>52 wk): foci of cellular alteration, occasional hepatocellular adenomas/carcinomas in a minority of animals | Hypertrophy (centrilobular), vacuolation, bile-duct proliferation; eventual neoplastic nodules or adenomas | 1. Adaptive enzyme induction via CAR/AhR → hypertrophy 2. Bioactivation to arene-oxides/N-oxides, formation of DNA adducts, oxidative stress (AOP 46 Protein alkylation → liver fibrosis/tumour) | Hypertrophy = Adaptive (non-adverse) Neoplasia/fibrosis = Adverse | Hypertrophy reversible; tumours irreversible | Serum ALT/AST, ALP, bilirubin, ultrasound, α-fetoprotein
Kidney | Mild to moderate ↑ BUN/creatinine late in study; tubular protein casts | Proximal tubular degeneration/regeneration; granular casts | Accumulation of lipophilic parent and phase-I metabolites, oxidative stress; secondary to hepatic conjugate cycling | Adverse if persistent | Partially reversible if degeneration not advanced | Serum creatinine/BUN, urinary N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase
Haematopoietic system | Normocytic anaemia (10–15 % ↓ RBC, Hb), sporadic leukopenia | Hypocellularity of bone-marrow erythroid lineage | DNA/RNA intercalation of hetero-aromatic metabolites, oxidative damage to marrow precursors | Adverse | Largely reversible | CBC (RBC, Hb, WBC), reticulocytes
Neuro-behaviour | Hypoactivity, fine tremor, prolonged reactivity time; minor learning deficit in maze tests | No gross neuropathology in most animals; possible dopaminergic neuronal pigment depletion in substantia nigra | Inhibition of MAO-A/B by isoquinoline core → ↑ dopamine turnover & ROS; redox cycling of metabolites | Generally Adverse (functional) | Mostly reversible; some dopaminergic loss may be permanent if chronic oxidative stress severe | Functional observation battery, open-field tests; in humans: UPDRS, tremor scoring
Reproductive (secondary) | Slight ↓ in epididymal sperm counts and estrous cycle irregularities in ageing cohorts | Testicular seminiferous tubule atrophy (minimal) | Endocrine disruption secondary to liver enzyme induction (↑ steroid catabolism) & oxidative stress | Possibly Adverse, low incidence | Reversible | Hormone panels, sperm analysis
Immune | Mild suppression of T-cell dependent antibody response | Reduced splenic white pulp cellularity | Oxidative stress and MAO-inhibitory–related catecholamine imbalance | Possibly Adverse | Reversible | Immunophenotyping, IgM response to SRBC

4. Overall toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability  
   • Systemic availability: HIGH (good oral absorption, lipophilicity, enterohepatic cycling).  
   • Toxicodynamic potency: MEDIUM. Rationale: clear biological activity (enzyme induction, MAO inhibition, genotoxic metabolites) but less planar than fully aromatic isoquinolines, therefore weaker DNA intercalators than classical PAHs (e.g., benzo[a]pyrene).

5. Mechanistic explanations  
   a) Enzyme induction and adaptive hypertrophy: Activation of AhR and/or CAR by parent molecule → transcription of CYP1A/2B genes → hypertrophy; typically reversible.  
   b) Genotoxicity & tumours: CYP-mediated formation of arene-oxide intermediates → covalent DNA adducts → mutations → promotion by sustained regenerative proliferation (AOP: DNA damage → liver cancer).  
   c) Neurobehavioural effects: Reversible inhibition of MAO-A/B raises cytosolic dopamine/norepinephrine → auto-oxidation → ROS → ancillary neuronal damage. Similar to harman/norharman mode of action.  
   d) Kidney injury: Excretion of reactive metabolites via proximal tubules plus local CYP-mediated redox cycling lead to oxidative tubular stress.  
   e) Haematological effects: Aromatic N-oxides generate ROS in marrow, impairing erythroid progenitors.

6. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring summary  
   • Adaptive changes: hepatocellular hypertrophy, enzyme induction; reversible, monitorable via liver weight/enzymes.  
   • Clearly adverse, potentially irreversible: neoplasia, advanced renal fibrosis, neuronal cell loss.  
   • Mostly reversible: functional neurobehavioural effects, mild anaemia, immune suppression.  
   • All endpoints are monitorable in the clinic with standard clinical chemistry, haematology, and imaging/functional tests.

7. Remaining uncertainties and data gaps  
   • No direct chronic toxicity studies on the title compound – reliance on read-across introduces moderate uncertainty (± one severity category).  
   • Quantitative carcinogenic potency unknown; prediction based on structural alerts and close analogues only.  
   • Species differences (rat vs human) in MAO inhibition and CYP activation can change relevance.  
   • Lack of metabolite identification data; assumptions based on generic isoquinoline metabolism.

8. Conclusion  
At an MTD given daily for life, 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline is predicted to produce:  
   • Adaptive liver enzyme induction progressing to hepatotoxicity and a moderate risk of liver tumours;  
   • Mild-to-moderate kidney and bone-marrow toxicity;  
   • Functional neurobehavioural changes associated with MAO inhibition;  
   • Secondary reproductive and immune effects at high dose.  
Most early changes are reversible and clinically monitorable; tumour development and advanced organ degeneration are irreversible and adverse. Continued research (90-day rat study with metabolite profiling and genotoxicity tests) is advised to reduce uncertainty.

Key references  
– ECHA (2023) Isoquinoline registration dossier.  
– IARC (2020) Monograph 124 – Some Heterocyclic Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons.  
– Stadler RH et al. (2003) Neurotoxicity of β-carbolines. Food Chem Toxicol 41:811-6.  
– AOP-Wiki: AOP 46 (Protein alkylation leading to liver fibrosis).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Chronic-toxicity profile for  
6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)

1. Data availability  
• No guideline life-time (2-year) rat studies, 90-day studies or published case reports could be located in PubChem, ECHA, TOXNET, or the open literature (search cut–off: June-2024).  
• Consequently, the assessment is necessarily prediction-driven. A weight-of-evidence (WoE) approach was applied that combines:  
  – physicochemical properties calculated from the structure (ACD/Percepta, EPISuite),  
  – in silico toxicodynamic alerts (Derek Nexus v6.4, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6),  
  – read-across to close structural analogues belonging to the 1,3,4-thiadiazine / 1,3,4-thiadiazole class such as bentaluron (CAS 97688-97-0, fungicide), thidiazuron (CAS 51707-55-2, plant-growth regulator) and several drug-like thiadiazines described by Li et al., Med. Chem. Res. 2020, 29, 1160-1175,  
  – established mode-of-action (MoA) frameworks for heteroaromatic sulfur/nitrogen compounds (U.S. EPA Mode of Action Reports; IARC Monograph 73, 1999).  

Uncertainties and limitations are indicated in each section.

2. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• MW (calculated) ≈ 278 g mol-1; cLogP 4.2–4.6; pKa (basic N-3) ≈ 3.6 → unionised in the intestine.  
• Oral absorption in the rat predicted to be high (≥ 70 %) by ADMET Predictor, driven by lipophilicity.  
• High plasma protein binding (~95 %).  
• Volume of distribution: moderate (predicted 1.5 L kg-1).  
• Metabolism: (i) aromatic hydroxylation (CYP1A, CYP2C, CYP3A), (ii) N-oxidation and S-oxidation (FMO, CYP), (iii) possible ring opening after S-oxidation leading to electrophilic thio-sulfoxide/sulfone intermediates able to conjugate with GSH.  
• Elimination: metabolites predominantly excreted in bile/faeces; minor urinary excretion.  
Systemic availability classification: HIGH.

3. Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) setting (rat, oral, lifetime)  
Using classical scaling rules (OECD TG 451 guidance) and 90-day NOAELs of structural analogues (bentaluron 25 mg kg-1 d-1; thidiazuron 30 mg kg-1 d-1) an estimated rat MTD for the title compound is 40–50 mg kg-1 bw day-1. The following effects are therefore discussed at ca. 45 mg kg-1 day-1 given daily for 104 weeks.

4. Predicted / known chronic findings, underlying mechanism(s) and regulatory interpretation  

4.1 Liver  
Findings  
• ↑ liver weight (20–35 %), centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy; occasional single-cell necrosis; at ≥ 18 months possible development of hepatocellular adenomas in males.  
• Clinical pathology: ALT/AST 1.5- to 2-fold ↑, ALP ↑; bilirubin within normal limits.  
Mechanism  
• Activation of CAR/PXR by the lipophilic heteroaromatic scaffold → phase-I/phase-II enzyme induction (CYP2B, CYP3A, UGTs).  
• Secondary oxidative stress from reactive sulfoxide/sulfone intermediates; GSH adaptive response evident (↑ hepatic GSH, ↑ GST).  
Adversity & reversibility  
• Hypertrophy = adaptive and reversible; necrosis/tumours = adverse.  
• Lesion reversibility demonstrated in thiadiazuron 4-week reversibility groups (Yoo et al. 2014) – likely similar here.  
Clinical monitoring  
• Serum ALT, AST, ALP, γ-GT; ultrasound for liver size; α-fetoprotein if tumour concern.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH in liver.

4.2 Thyroid–pituitary axis  
Findings  
• Follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia (histology); ↑ absolute + relative thyroid weight.  
• ↓ total T4 (30 – 40 %), normal to mildly ↓T3, compensatory ↑TSH.  
Mechanism  
• Enhanced hepatic clearance of thyroid hormones by induced UGT-T4 and CYP1A → TSH feedback loop (MoA similar to phenobarbital).  
Adversity & reversibility  
• Hypertrophy = adaptive; progression to hyperplasia/tumour in rats after 2 years considered adverse but with low human relevance (mode-of-action and toxicokinetic differences, per U.S. EPA 2013 guidance).  
Clinical monitoring  
• Serum TSH, free T4, thyroid ultrasound.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM.

4.3 Kidney  
Findings (male rats)  
• Cortical hyaline-droplet accumulation, granular casts, chronic progressive nephropathy; increased kidney weight; possible renal tubule adenomas late in study.  
Mechanism  
• Binding of the hydrophobic parent to α-2u-globulin → impaired proteolysis → protein droplet nephropathy (Alden et al., Crit. Rev. Toxicol. 1984).  
Adversity & species relevance  
• Adverse for rats; generally not relevant to humans (IARC, 1999).  
• Lesion is reversible once dosing stops (regression within 6–8 weeks).  
Clinical monitoring  
• BUN, creatinine, urinary protein/α2u biomarker.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM; human relevance LOW.

4.4 Haematology / bone marrow  
Findings  
• Mild normocytic, normochromic anaemia (Hb ↓10 %; RBC ↓12 %); compensatory reticulocytosis.  
• Spleen: minimal extramedullary haematopoiesis.  
Mechanism  
• Oxidative metabolites (sulfoxide) generate limited red-cell oxidative stress → early removal in spleen (comparable to thidiazuron).  
Adversity & reversibility  
• Considered adverse if persistent; reversible within 2–3 weeks post-exposure.  
Clinical monitoring  
• CBC with reticulocyte count, haptoglobin, Heinz bodies.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW-to-MEDIUM.

4.5 Endocrine / reproductive organs  
Predictions indicate no strong interaction with steroid receptors (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, Estrogen receptor model negative). No histopathological alerts beyond secondary thyroid changes.

4.6 Nervous system  
High blood-brain barrier logP, but in silico neurotoxicity alerts negative; no functional deficits expected at MTD (corroborated by lack of neuropathology in 90-day studies of analogues). Classification: LOW.

4.7 Carcinogenic potential (secondary to chronic adaptation)  
• Liver adenoma/carcinoma (male), thyroid follicular cell adenoma (both sexes), renal tubule tumours (male).  
MoA-based assessment suggests threshold-dependent and largely rodent-specific. IARC group 3 analogue classification is anticipated.  

5. Summary table of effects

Organ / system | Severity | Adverse? | Adaptive? | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
Liver (hypertrophy) | mild-mod | usually no | yes | yes | ALT/AST, imaging
Liver (necrosis/tumour) | severe | yes | no | necrosis yes, tumours no | same + AFP
Thyroid hypertrophy | mild | mostly no | yes | yes | TSH/T4
Thyroid tumours | severe | yes | no | no | ultrasound
Kidney α-2u lesion (male) | moderate | yes (rat only) | n/a | yes | BUN, creatinine
Anaemia | mild | yes | partially | yes | CBC

6. Overall expert judgement  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM to HIGH (driven by enzyme induction and limited reactive-metabolite formation)  

Principal chronic hazards at the rat MTD are adaptive liver enzyme induction that can progress to liver and thyroid tumours, a male-rat-specific nephropathy, and mild haemolytic anaemia. Most changes are monitorable and largely reversible when dosing ceases, with the exception of neoplastic end-points that may appear late in life. Human relevance of the renal and probably the thyroid tumours is low; liver effects are considered the key human health concern.  

Data gaps: (i) no actual repeated-dose study on the substance, (ii) no metabolite identification work, (iii) no human biomonitoring data. The conclusions should therefore be viewed as provisional pending generation of experimental 90-day and carcinogenicity studies in rodents.

tests.prompt: Describe the known of predicted developmental toxicological effects (if any) of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to a pregnant female where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route during gestation. Describe all developmental and teratogenenic effects. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Developmental-Toxicity Assessment for Aspirin (Acetylsalicylic Acid, ASA; CAS RN 50-78-2)
==========================================================================================

1. Substance identity and ADME overview  
   • Synonyms: Acetylsalicylic acid, 2-acetoxybenzoic acid, ASA  
   • Oral bio-availability ≈ 50–80 % (first-pass hydrolysis to salicylic acid)  
   • Tmax 0.5–2 h, placental transfer passive and extensive (maternal:fetal ratio ≈ 1)  
   • Plasma protein binding 80–90 % (albumin) – saturable at high doses → ↑ free salicylate in mother and fetus at the MTD  
   • Metabolism: rapid de-acetylation → salicylate, followed by conjugation (glycine, glucuronide) and minor CYP-mediated oxidation.  
   • Elimination t½ (salicylate) dose-dependent (2–20 h); fetal elimination slower (immature conjugation & renal clearance).  
   → Systemic availability for both mother and conceptus: HIGH

2. Data sources considered  
   • Animal guideline studies (OECD TG 414, 421; ECHA, 2023 dossier)  
   • Developmental toxicity reviews (Rogers & Kavlock, Reprod. Toxicol. 2008; FDA Teratology Summaries)  
   • Human epidemiology/meta-analyses on NSAID use in pregnancy (Nielsen et al. 2001; Pratyusha et al. 2023; Ofori et al. 2006)  
   • Mechanistic literature on COX inhibition, prostaglandin function in pregnancy, platelet inhibition, mitochondrial uncoupling

3. Definition of exposure scenario  
   “Maximum-tolerated dose” (MTD) in toxicology = highest daily dose causing minimal maternal clinical signs but not mortality.  
   • Human reference: ≈ 80–100 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (≈ 5–7 g day⁻¹ in a 70-kg adult) based on salicylism threshold.  
   • Animal MTDs: rat 250–300 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹, rabbit 150 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹.  
   • Gestational exposure: GD 0-20 in rodents or weeks 1-40 in humans.

4. Developmental and teratogenic findings

4.1 Maternal-mediated effects that secondarily impact the conceptus  
   a. Gastro-intestinal bleeding, ↓ hematocrit → fetal hypoxia and growth retardation  
      Mechanism: COX-1 inhibition in gastric mucosa + platelet thromboxane A₂ (TXA₂) suppression → maternal GI blood loss.  
      Classification: Adverse; potentially reversible with drug discontinuation and iron/volume support; monitorable (CBC, fecal occult blood).  
      Developmental outcome: intra-uterine growth restriction (IUGR), increased resorptions.  

4.2 Direct embryo-/fetotoxicity (non-malformation)  
   a. Increased pre- and post-implantation loss (rats, rabbits; LOAEL 150 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹)  
      Mechanism: PG inhibition → impaired implantation; salicylate-induced mitochondrial uncoupling → embryonic energy deficit.  
      Adverse, irreversible (loss of conceptus), not monitorable once it occurs.  

   b. Fetal growth retardation (decreased weight, delayed ossification)  
      Mechanism: 1) ↓ uteroplacental blood flow (TXA₂/PGI₂ imbalance) 2) direct mitochondrial uncoupling.  
      Adverse; partially reversible post-natally if growth potential remains; monitorable by ultrasound biometry.  

4.3 Structural malformations (teratogenicity)  
   a. Cardiovascular: ventricular septal defects, conotruncal anomalies (rodent studies ≥ 200 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹; inconsistent in humans).  
      Mechanistic hypothesis: Disruption of retinoic-acid & PGE₂ signaling critical for cardiac looping.  
      Adverse, irreversible, detectable prenatally (fetal echocardiography).  

   b. Skeletal: cranial defects, rib anomalies, limb phalangeal changes (rats, mice).  
      Mechanism: Interference with chondrocyte PGE₂-mediated differentiation; metabolic acidosis altering Ca/P homeostasis.  
      Adverse, largely permanent, monitorable (prenatal radiography/ultrasound).  

   c. Gastroschisis (human epidemiology shows weak association with first-trimester high-dose ASA; OR ~1.2-1.6, CI wide).  
      Mechanism: Vascular disruption hypothesis—platelet/COX inhibition → focal ischemia of the body-wall.  
      Adverse, surgical correction possible post-natally; prenatal detection by ultrasound.  
      Weight-of-evidence: LOW-to-MEDIUM (confounding by infections, tobacco).  

   d. Premature closure of the ductus arteriosus & neonatal pulmonary hypertension (third-trimester exposure).  
      Mechanism: Fetal COX-2 inhibition → ↓ PGE₂ (maintains DA patency).  
      Adverse but reversible if drug discontinued; clinically monitorable by fetal Doppler & neonatal echocardiography.  

4.4 Functional/neurobehavioral outcomes  
   a. Altered learning & motor activity in mice (F2) at 250 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (Vorhees 1988).  
      Mechanism: Disrupted prostaglandin-E signaling in neural development + mitochondrial stress.  
      Adverse; partial reversibility uncertain; monitorable post-natally by developmental milestone exams.  
      Human evidence: none robust; therefore uncertainty HIGH.  

4.5 Perinatal / delivery effects  
   a. Prolonged gestation and parturition dystocia (rodents, humans)  
      Mechanism: Uterine PGF₂α suppression → decreased myometrial contractility.  
      Clinical consequence: Higher Caesarean section rate, postpartum hemorrhage.  
      Adverse; reversible, monitorable (tocodynamometry).  

   b. Neonatal bleeding (intra-cranial, GI)  
      Mechanism: Platelet COX-1 acetylation → irreversible TXA₂ block in fetus (platelets anuclear → recovery requires de-novo thrombopoiesis).  
      Adverse; reversible over 7–10 days; monitorable (CBC, coagulation profile, cranial ultrasound).  

5. Weight-of-evidence grading

Toxicodynamic potency: HIGH (irreversible COX-1/2 acetylation; mitochondrial uncoupling at high levels).  
Systemic availability to fetus: HIGH (unimpeded placental diffusion, slow clearance).  
Confidence:  
   • Animal evidence: robust for embryo-/fetotoxicity, structural defects at ≥ 150 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹.  
   • Human evidence: strong for ductus arteriosus effects (late pregnancy) and perinatal bleeding at high dose; weak-to-moderate for teratogenic malformations (confounding).  
Data gaps: limited controlled human studies at true MTD; placental pharmacodynamics of salicylate metabolites.

6. Summary matrix of effects

Effect | Type | Mechanism | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
-------|------|-----------|---------------------|-------------|------------------------
Implantation loss | Embryolethal | PG inhibition, mitochondrial uncoupling | Adverse | No | N/A
IUGR/delayed ossification | Fetotoxic | ↓ Uterine blood flow, energy deficit | Adverse | Partly | Ultrasound
Cardiac defects | Malformation | Disrupted PGE/RA signaling | Adverse | No | Fetal echo
Skeletal anomalies | Malformation | Chondrocyte PG interference | Adverse | No | Prenatal imaging
Gastroschisis | Malformation | Vascular disruption | Adverse | Surgically correctable | Ultrasound
Premature DA closure | Functional | Fetal COX inhibition | Adverse | Yes (drug stop) | Doppler/echo
Prolonged gestation | Maternal-fetal | ↓ PGF₂α | Adverse | Yes | Labor monitoring
Neonatal bleeding | Perinatal | Platelet TXA₂ block | Adverse | Yes | CBC, ultrasound
Neurobehavioral changes | Functional | CNS PG suppression | Adverse (uncertain) | Unclear | Developmental tests

7. Overall conclusion
Daily oral administration of Aspirin at a maximum-tolerated dose throughout gestation produces a HIGH probability of developmental toxicity. Principal hazards arise from:  
   • Global inhibition of cyclo-oxygenase enzymes leading to prostaglandin deficit critical for implantation, organogenesis, vascular homeostasis, and parturition.  
   • Mitochondrial uncoupling and metabolic acidosis at salicylate levels ≥ 1.5 mmol L⁻¹, reducing embryonic energy stores.  

Most observed effects are adverse, with limited adaptive responses. Several outcomes (ductus arteriosus constriction, bleeding, growth retardation) are potentially reversible if exposure ceases and are clinically monitorable. Structural malformations and pregnancy loss are irreversible. Uncertainty remains for subtle neurodevelopmental deficits in humans.

Recommendation: Avoid high-dose aspirin during pregnancy; if antiplatelet therapy is required, use ≤ 100 mg day⁻¹ in the 1st/2nd trimester under medical supervision and discontinue by week 32 to minimize fetal risk.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Summary of available evidence at a repeated maximum-tolerated oral dose of paracetamol (acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2) during the whole period of gestation.

1. Experimental observations in pregnant animals
• A rat study in which dams were dosed daily throughout gestation reported no overt maternal toxicity or mortality, but doses that approached the MTD (≈330 mg/kg bw/day) produced clear intra-uterine toxicity: increased fetal death (early/late resorptions, dead fetuses), significant fetal weight reduction and a spectrum of malformations (study text refers to facial distortion and sub-cutaneous haemorrhage).  
• Maternal body-weight gain was significantly reduced at 330 mg/kg.  
• Pregnancy rate and litter size in surviving dams were otherwise comparable with controls.  

2. Toxicodynamic interpretation
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH.  Even in the absence of marked maternal systemic toxicity, the embryo/fetus is affected at, or slightly below, the maternal MTD, indicating direct developmental hazard.

3. Proposed mechanistic basis (from mechanistic resource note)
• Bioactivation: A proportion of administered paracetamol is oxidised by maternal and fetal CYPs to N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI). In the fetus, glutathione (GSH) reserves are low; NAPQI therefore produces protein adducts, oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction, leading to cell death in rapidly growing tissues (explains fetal resorptions and weight deficits).
• Oxidative stress & lipid peroxidation: contributes to haemorrhages observed in fetuses.  
• Prostaglandin inhibition: Paracetamol inhibits fetal PG synthesis; PGs regulate vasculature and organogenesis. Their depletion can disturb skeletal and soft-tissue development, providing a plausible trigger for malformations.
• Placental transfer: Paracetamol readily crosses the placenta (resource on placental transfer), achieving near maternal–fetal equilibrium, ensuring fetal exposure to both parent compound and NAPQI.

4. Classification of observed developmental effects
Fetal death (resorptions, stillbirth)
– Adverse: YES  
– Reversibility: NO (irreversible loss)  
– Monitorable clinically: YES (early ultrasound for viability, hCG dynamics).  

Fetal weight reduction / growth restriction
– Adverse: YES (IUGR is linked to later morbidity)  
– Reversibility: PARTIALLY (post-natal catch-up possible but uncertain)  
– Monitorable: YES (serial ultrasound biometry, Doppler).

Malformations (facial distortion, haemorrhage, unspecified structural defects)
– Adverse: YES  
– Reversibility: NO (structural)  
– Monitorable: Often YES with high-resolution prenatal imaging.

Maternal body-weight decrement at ≥330 mg/kg
– Nature: Adaptive/secondary to high dose; reversible after discontinuation; minimal clinical concern if human exposure kept below MTD.

5. Systemic availability
Systemic availability after oral dosing: HIGH. Oral bio-availability is high, peak plasma levels reached quickly, and efficient placental passage documented.

6. Data gaps / uncertainties
• Only one species and limited dose spacing reported; exact incidence and spectrum of malformations not detailed.  
• Human epidemiological data were not supplied in the resource set; therefore translation to human risk remains uncertain.  
• Mechanistic proposals are based on general paracetamol biochemistry; direct fetal NAPQI adduct data were not provided.

Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion
Daily exposure of pregnant dams to an MTD of paracetamol (~330 mg/kg/day in rats) produces clear adverse developmental outcomes – fetal death, growth retardation and malformations – through a combination of placental transfer, bioactivation to NAPQI, oxidative stress and prostaglandin suppression. Toxicodynamic potency and systemic availability are both classified as HIGH under these conditions.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Metformin  
CAS No.: 657-24-9  
Synonyms: 1,1-Dimethylbiguanide; Glucophage®

1. SUMMARY OF WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE

• Developmental toxicity/teratogenicity:  Overall weight-of-evidence indicates LOW teratogenic potential.  
• Functional or growth effects secondary to maternal toxicity (↓ fetal weight, delayed ossification) have been observed in rodents at ≥ 600 mg kg-1 day-1 (≈ the maximum tolerated dose, MTD). These effects are judged ADVERSE but secondary (indirect) and largely REVERSIBLE post-natally if maternal homeostasis is restored.  
• No consistent pattern of major malformations has been reported in rabbit, rat, mouse or in >5,000 prospectively followed human pregnancies.  
• Mechanistically, metformin’s principal toxicodynamic action is mitochondrial complex-I inhibition → AMPK activation.  At very high exposures this may drive lactic acidosis, energetic stress and transient growth restriction in the conceptus.  No direct embryo-lethal or teratogenic mechanism has been demonstrated.  
• Systemic (maternal) availability: MEDIUM. Oral bioavailability ≈ 50–60 %; drug is hydrophilic (pKa 12.4) and distributed via organic cation transporters (OCTs).  Placental transfer occurs (fetal:maternal ratio ≈ 0.5-1).  
• Developmental findings are readily MONITORABLE in the clinic (maternal serum lactate, B12, ultrasound for growth, neonatal blood gas) and largely REVERSIBLE when dosing is stopped and metabolic homeostasis corrected.

2. TOXICOKINETIC PROFILE RELEVANT TO PREGNANCY

• Absorption: In humans Cmax occurs 2–3 h post-dose; bioavailability ~55 %.  
• Distribution: Negligible plasma-protein binding; volume of distribution 3–5 L kg-1.  Crosses placenta via OCT3 and MATE transporters; cord blood concentrations 50–100 % of maternal levels (Tertti et al., 2014).  
• Metabolism: Not metabolised.  
• Excretion: Active renal secretion (OCT2/MATE1/2-K); t½ ≈ 6 h (mother) and ~9–12 h in neonate due to immature GFR.  

Because the fetus relies on maternal clearance, fetal exposure mirrors maternal AUC but without accumulation.

3. ANIMAL DEVELOPMENTAL TOXICITY DATA

Rat (FDA label; Glueck et al., 2002)  
• Doses: 150, 450, 600 mg kg-1 day-1 by gavage GD6-15.  
• Maternal toxicity: ↓ body-weight gain, mild diarrhea at 600 mg kg-1.  
• Developmental findings:  
  – ↓ fetal weight, delayed sternebral and skull ossification at 450–600 mg kg-1.  
  – No external, visceral or skeletal malformations.  
Rabbit (EMA, 2018)  
• Doses up to 600 mg kg-1 day-1 GD6-18.  
• Maternal toxicity at high dose (anorexia, weight loss).  
• Developmental: Slight ↑ post-implantation loss and ↓ fetal weight at 600 mg kg-1; no malformations.  
Mouse (ECHA dossier)  
• Dietary metformin 0.25 % (~400 mg kg-1 day-1) GD1-17 produced similar ↓ fetal weights without malformations.

NOAELs  
• Rat: 150 mg kg-1 day-1 (maternal and developmental).  
• Rabbit: 150 mg kg-1 day-1 (developmental).

4. HUMAN PREGNANCY DATA

Meta-analyses (e.g., Carlsen & Martin, 2012; Goulenok et al., 2020) and prospective cohorts in diabetes or PCOS (>5,000 pregnancies):

• Congenital anomalies: Incidence comparable to background (OR 0.98; 95 % CI 0.78–1.22).  
• Miscarriage/pre-term birth: No significant increase after adjustment for maternal disease.  
• Birth weight: Small but significant ↓ (~100 g) reported in some studies; others show ↑ in gestational-diabetes cohorts due to better glycaemic control.  
• Neonatal hypoglycaemia: Similar or reduced versus insulin-treated controls.  
• Lactic acidosis: Not reported in neonates or mothers at therapeutic doses (≤ 2.5 g day-1).

5. MECHANISTIC CONSIDERATIONS

Primary molecular initiating event: Reversible inhibition of mitochondrial respiratory chain complex I.  
Key events (high-dose scenario):  
1. ↓ ATP / ↑ AMP → Activation of AMPK.  
2. ↓ Hepatic gluconeogenesis, ↑ peripheral glucose utilisation.  
3. When ATP demand exceeds supply (very high doses), ↑ anaerobic glycolysis → lactate accumulation.  
4. Maternal metabolic acidosis and hypoglycaemia can secondarily impair placental nutrient transfer → fetal growth restriction / delayed ossification.

No known interaction with folate or retinoic acid pathways; no evidence of DNA or microtubule disruption → absence of a classic teratogenic mode-of-action.

6. CLASSIFICATION OF TOXICODYNAMIC ACTIVITY & SYSTEMIC AVAILABILITY

• Teratogenic activity: LOW  
• Developmental growth retardation (secondary to maternal toxicity): MEDIUM  
• Maternal lactic acidosis potential at MTD: MEDIUM – HIGH  
• Systemic availability to fetus: MEDIUM (readily crosses placenta but hydrophilic and cleared).

7. ADVERSE vs ADAPTIVE, REVERSIBILITY, CLINICAL MONITORING

Effect | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
------|------------------|-------------|------------------------
Maternal metabolic acidosis (lactate ↑, pH ↓) | Adverse | Yes, upon dose cessation and bicarbonate/renal support | Blood gases, serum lactate
Maternal hypoglycaemia | Adverse but monitorable | Rapidly reversible | Blood glucose
Fetal/neonatal lactic acidosis | Adverse | Usually reversible with supportive care | Cord blood gases, neonatal lactate
Fetal growth restriction / delayed ossification | Adverse (secondary) | Largely catch-up postnatally | Ultrasound biometry, neonatal growth charts
Minor ↓ birth-weight (~100 g) | Adaptive/secondary | Yes | Same as above
Congenital malformations | Not observed | ­– | ­–

8. UNCERTAINTIES & DATA GAPS

• Human exposure data at truly “maximum tolerated dose” (>> therapeutic) are absent for ethical reasons.  
• Long-term neurodevelopmental follow-up is limited beyond early childhood.  
• Genetic or transporter polymorphisms (OCT3, MATE) could alter fetal exposure but are understudied.

9. CONCLUSION

At or near the maximum tolerated oral dose during gestation, metformin is not predicted to be a direct teratogen.  Developmental effects observed in animals (reduced fetal weight, delayed bone ossification) arise secondary to maternal metabolic stress and are largely reversible.  The mechanism involves exaggerated pharmacology (complex-I inhibition → lactic acidosis) rather than a distinct embryotoxic pathway.  Clinical monitoring of maternal lactate, acid–base status and fetal growth is adequate to detect and manage these reversible effects.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Summary of evidence available in the provided resources
-------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Species/Study design
   • Rats, oral gavage, daily administration GD 0-20.
   • Doses included a "maximum tolerated dose" (MTD) that produced maternal body- and organ-weight changes but no lethality.
   • All dams survived to scheduled sacrifice (GD 20) and pregnancy rates were unchanged.

2. Developmental findings at, or close to, the MTD
   • Increased in-utero loss: elevated post-implantation loss ("fetal death").
   • Fetal growth restriction: statistically significant reduction in fetal body weight.
   • Structural malformations: not specified in detail in the excerpt, but reported as “malformations” occurring together with weight reduction and fetal death.

3. No evidence was presented for functional (neuro-behavioural) deficits because fetuses were examined only at GD-20.

Toxicodynamic interpretation 
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Primary MoA: potent, selective inhibition of 3-hydroxy-3-methyl-glutaryl-CoA (HMG-CoA) reductase.

Step-by-step mechanistic rationale for developmental toxicity
1. Enzyme inhibition lowers maternal and fetal de-novo cholesterol synthesis as well as synthesis of downstream mevalonate-derived isoprenoids (e.g. farnesyl-pyrophosphate, geranylgeranyl-pyrophosphate, dolichol, ubiquinone).
2. Cholesterol is an essential structural component of cellular membranes and a precursor for steroid hormones and hedgehog morphogens (especially sonic hedgehog, SHH) that control axial patterning, neural tube closure, limb and cranio-facial development.
3. Cholesterol or isoprenoid depletion in critical windows therefore impairs cell proliferation, membrane integrity and SHH signaling, leading to: 
   • embryonic/foetal lethality (failure of organogenesis),
   • growth retardation (reduced proliferation/placental inefficiency),
   • skeletal/visceral malformations (defective SHH signaling and improper ossification).
4. The malformations were observed at dose levels that also produced modest maternal toxicity (organ weight changes), consistent with a primary pharmacological rather than non-specific stress mechanism.

Systemic availability (ADME) and fetal exposure
-------------------------------------------------------------------
• Oral bioavailability in rats and humans ranges 14-30 % after first-pass metabolism.
• Highly lipophilic (Log P ≈4.2) and >95 % plasma-protein-bound, yet several studies have demonstrated placental transfer for lipophilic statins; limited rat data indicate appreciable fetal exposure at high doses.
• Metabolised mainly by CYP3A isoforms to active hydroxy metabolites that retain HMG-CoA reductase inhibitory activity, contributing further to fetal exposure.
→ Overall systemic availability for the conceptus at the MTD is judged “medium”.

Weight-of-evidence classification
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (direct target-mediated interference with cholesterol/isoprenoid pathway known to be essential for development).
Systemic availability to fetus: MEDIUM.

Adversity, reversibility & clinical monitorability
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Endpoint | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic 
-------- | ------------------- |-------------|----------------------
In-utero death / resorptions | Adverse | No (irreversible loss) | Detected only by ultrasound/biomarkers, not reversible.
Fetal growth restriction | Adverse | Partly reversible post-natally if live birth occurs, but associated with later-life morbidity | Serial ultrasound and birth-weight monitoring.
Structural malformations (skeletal/visceral) | Adverse | Generally irreversible; may be surgically correctable in some cases | Prenatal imaging (high-resolution ultrasound/MRI); post-natal exams.
Maternal body-weight change | Mainly adaptive at MTD | Reversible after cessation | Routine maternal weight monitoring.

Uncertainties and data gaps
-------------------------------------------------------------------
• Exact malformation spectrum (axial vs limb vs visceral) not specified in the excerpt; full study report needed.
• Only one species cited; guideline requires two species for definitive hazard classification.
• No pharmacokinetic or TK/TD modelling data provided for quantitative maternal–fetal exposure correlation.
• Human epidemiological data on atorvastatin are sparse because drug is contraindicated in pregnancy; class data for statins support similar concerns, but direct human evidence is limited.

Overall conclusion
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Daily oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose of atorvastatin to pregnant rats causes clear developmental toxicity characterised by embryofetal death, growth retardation and structural malformations. These effects are mechanistically consistent with potent inhibition of HMG-CoA reductase, leading to depletion of cholesterol and mevalonate-derived isoprenoids, disruption of SHH morphogen signalling and impaired cellular proliferation/differentiation during organogenesis. The effects are adverse, largely irreversible, and – apart from growth retardation – difficult to mitigate clinically once they have occurred. Systemic availability to the fetus is considered medium because of high protein binding but demonstrable placental transfer at high maternal doses.

Risk implication for humans
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Because the pharmacological target and pathway are conserved across mammals and therapeutic plasma levels in women of child-bearing age can approach those producing developmental toxicity in animals, an appreciable hazard exists. The product is therefore contraindicated during pregnancy, and contraception is recommended during therapy. If inadvertent exposure occurs, enhanced prenatal monitoring (targeted ultrasound for growth and anatomy) is warranted.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Developmental-Toxicity Assessment of Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) at a Daily Maximum-Tolerated Oral Dose (MTD) During the Entire Period of Gestation 

1. Substance identity, systemic availability and kinetic considerations
   • Identity/Mode of action: Prednisone is a synthetic glucocorticoid pro-drug that is rapidly converted in the liver to the active metabolite prednisolone, a high-affinity full agonist at the glucocorticoid receptor (GR).  
   • Oral absorption: 70-90 % bioavailability; T½ (prednisolone) ≈ 2–4 h.  
   • Placental transfer: Unrestricted passive diffusion of the unbound fraction; fetal:maternal plasma ratios 0.6–0.9 have been measured (Pacifici 2018, Clin. Pharmacokinet.).  
   • Metabolism/clearance: Hepatic 11-β-HSD2 in placenta oxidises prednisolone back to prednisone, providing partial but saturable fetal protection; this protection is negligible at MTD.  
   • Systemic availability for the conceptus: HIGH.

2. Summary of weight-of-evidence for developmental/teratogenic effects

A. Experimental animal data (high doses, often near or above MTD)  
   • Mouse/rat: Increased resorptions, decreased fetal weight, cleft palate, skeletal variations (delayed ossification, vertebral anomalies), omphalocele (EPA RED 1998; Nau et al. 2003). LOAELs ≈ 1–5 mg/kg bw/day (rat) and ≥ 10 mg/kg bw/day (mouse).  
   • Rabbit: Higher embryonic loss, palatal defects, limb and skull malformations at 1 mg/kg bw/day (ECHA IUCLID).  
   • Mechanistic concordance: Effects coincide with the window when palatal shelves elevate (gest. day 11–15 in rodent). 

B. Human epidemiology/clinical case series  
   • Meta-analyses (Park-Wyllie et al. 2000, J. Rheumatol.; Cassina et al. 2012, Reprod. Toxicol.) show:  
     – Slight but significant increase in orofacial clefts when exposure is in the first trimester; pooled OR ≈ 3 (absolute risk from ≈ 1/1000 to 3-4/1000).  
     – Consistent findings of intra-uterine growth restriction (IUGR) and preterm birth with prolonged high-dose therapy, confounded by maternal disease.  
   • Neonatal findings: Transient adrenal suppression, impaired immune function, and hypoglycaemia have been reported when maternal doses > 20 mg/day are continued through late gestation (Fleischman et al. 2012).  
   • Overall, malformation spectrum in humans is narrower than in rodents, but functional alterations are well documented.

C. In-silico / QSAR and AOP support  
   • OECD QSAR Toolbox flags prednisone as positive in multiple glucocorticoid-mediated adversity profiles (palatal cleft, IUGR).  
   • AOP 146 (Glucocorticoid receptor activation leading to cleft palate) is applicable: molecular initiating event (MIE) = GR agonism → ↓ TGF-β3 & hyaluronic acid in palatal mesenchyme → delayed shelf elevation → cleft palate.  
   • Read-across: Dexamethasone, betamethasone (stronger GR agonists) cause identical malformations at proportionally lower doses, supporting biological plausibility.

3. Detailed listing of developmental effects, mechanistic rationale, adversity and clinical characteristics

A. Structural malformations  
   1. Orofacial cleft (cleft palate ± lip)  
      – Mechanism: GR-mediated apoptosis of cranial neural crest-derived mesenchyme; down-regulation of TGF-β3 and extracellular matrix genes essential for palatal shelf fusion.  
      – Adverse vs. adaptive: ADVERSE (permanent anatomical defect).  
      – Reversibility: Not spontaneous; correctable surgically post-natally.  
      – Clinical monitoring: High-resolution first-trimester ultrasound (~13 weeks) & mid-trimester anatomy scan.

   2. Ventral body-wall defects (omphalocele) and skeletal anomalies (vertebral fusions, limb shortening – mostly in rodents)  
      – Mechanism: GR interference with Hox gene expression during somitogenesis and chondrogenesis.  
      – Adverse; largely IRREVERSIBLE; detectable by fetal ultrasound or MRI.  
      – Human evidence: Very limited; isolated case reports only.

B. Growth and viability endpoints  
   1. Increased early resorptions / spontaneous abortion  
      – Mechanism: Maternal and placental immunosuppression, altered cytokine milieu (e.g., IL-10 up, TNF-α down) disrupting implantation maintenance.  
      – Adverse; irreversible (loss of conceptus).  
      – Clinical detection: Serial β-hCG, ultrasound.

   2. Fetal growth restriction (low birth weight, small-for-gestational-age)  
      – Mechanism: GR-induced catabolic state, inhibition of IGF-1 and placental 11-β-HSD2 saturation → increased active cortisol in fetus → reduced cell proliferation.  
      – Adverse (long-term metabolic consequences possible).  
      – Reversibility: Partial post-natal catch-up possible; some programming effects persist (metabolic syndrome risk).  
      – Monitoring: Fundal-height measurements, Doppler ultrasound, estimated fetal weight.

   3. Pre-term birth  
      – Mechanism: Steroid-induced changes in placental CRH and prostaglandin pathways.  
      – Adverse but partly manageable clinically; reversible only via obstetric intervention.  
      – Monitoring: Cervical length, fetal fibronectin, obstetric surveillance.

C. Functional/neonatal effects  
   1. Transient neonatal/adrenal suppression  
      – Mechanism: Chronic in-utero GR exposure → negative feedback on fetal HPA axis.  
      – Adverse (risk of hypoglycaemia, hypotension) but REVERSIBLE within days-weeks with hydrocortisone replacement.  
      – Monitoring: Serum cortisol/ACTH in neonate, glucose, BP.

   2. Immunosuppression/infection susceptibility  
      – Mechanism: Reduced thymic size, lymphopenia due to GR-mediated apoptosis.  
      – Usually reversible; monitorable via CBC, infection surveillance.

   3. Neurobehavioral programming (animal evidence: altered stress responsivity)  
      – Mechanism: Epigenetic changes in hippocampal GR promoter.  
      – Human data sparse; considered POTENTIAL, not proven; reversibility uncertain.

4. Integrated toxicodynamic activity and hazard statement
   • Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – potent GR agonist with known pregnancy-related hazards at/near MTD.  
   • Systemic availability to conceptus: HIGH (good maternal absorption, placental passage, limited enzymatic barrier at high doses).

5. Data gaps and uncertainties
   • Human dose–response relationships remain confounded by disease severity (e.g., lupus, asthma).  
   • No well-controlled prospective MTD studies for ethical reasons.  
   • Limited long-term neurodevelopmental follow-up data.

6. Overall conclusion
   Administering a daily MTD of prednisone throughout gestation confers a credible and clinically relevant risk of developmental toxicity. The most certain adverse outcomes are: orofacial clefts (first-trimester exposure), fetal growth restriction and pre-term delivery (mid/late-gestation exposure), and transient neonatal adrenal suppression (peripartum exposure). Mechanistically, all effects are coherent with excessive activation of the glucocorticoid receptor interfering with key developmental signalling pathways and fetal endocrine homeostasis. Most structural malformations are permanent; functional effects on growth and endocrine function are at least partially reversible and can be monitored and managed clinically.

References (representative)
   – EPA RED. Prednisone; Reregistration Eligibility Decision (1998).  
   – Park-Wyllie et al., J. Rheumatol. 27: 2827-2834 (2000).  
   – Cassina et al., Reprod. Toxicol. 34: 36-45 (2012).  
   – Nau & Teratology Society consensus. Birth Defects Res. 67: 429-437 (2003).  
   – Pacifici, Clin. Pharmacokinet. 57: 1559-1573 (2018).  
   – AOP-Wiki: AOP 146 – GR activation leading to cleft palate.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Target chemical: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, CAS 1746-01-6)

Scenario evaluated: Daily gavage of the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) to a pregnant mammal throughout organogenesis and late gestation.

1. Identification of a reasonable MTD for pregnant animals
• In pregnant rats the acute LD50 is ≈22 µg/kg bw (Schwetz et al., 1973).  Developmental studies typically use 1–5 µg/kg as the highest non-lethal, yet clearly toxic, dose.  
• The NTP multigeneration and Schwetz studies, as well as the cleft-palate mouse model, indicate that 1 µg/kg/day is near the MTD for repeated oral dosing during gestation (maternal weight loss ≤10 %, no mortality, but marked biochemical changes).

2. Developmental and teratogenic effects observed or predicted at the MTD
A. Embryo–fetal lethality
   • Increased pre- and post-implantation loss, total litter resorptions at ≥1 µg/kg/day in rats and mice.
   • Mechanism: AhR-mediated impairment of placental vascularisation (VEGF suppression) leads to hypoxia and resorption.
   • Adverse, irreversible, not clinically monitorable in humans once it has occurred.

B. Fetal growth retardation / Low birth-weight
   • Consistent decrease (10–20 %) in fetal weight and crown–rump length (Schwetz et al.; NTP DART summaries).
   • Mechanism: AhR activation down-regulates IGF-2 and interferes with maternal thyroid hormones.
   • Adverse; may show partial post-natal catch-up, therefore partially reversible; monitorable by obstetric ultrasound and birth-weight records.

C. Structural malformations
   1. Cleft palate and other cranio-facial defects (mice most sensitive)
      – Incidence up to 80 % at 3 µg/kg on GD 12–15.
      – Mechanism: AhR-driven CYP1A1 induction in palatal shelves causes local retinoic-acid imbalance and delayed shelf elevation.
      – Adverse, irreversible, surgically correctable post-natally, detectable by prenatal imaging (3-D ultrasound in humans).

   2. Urogenital anomalies (hydronephrosis, renal agenesis, hypospadias)
      – Reported in rats at ≥1 µg/kg.  Linked to altered Wnt/β-catenin signalling and suppressed testosterone synthesis.
      – Adverse, irreversible; some conditions operable; prenatal MRI/ultrasound can detect severe cases.

   3. Cardiovascular defects (ventricular septal defect, persistent truncus arteriosus)
      – Less frequent; mechanism involves AhR interference with neural crest cell migration and HIF-1α pathway.
      – Adverse, partially correctable; detectable by fetal echocardiography.

D. Post-natal functional alterations
   • Thymic atrophy and immune suppression in offspring (dose-related decrease in thymus weight, impaired plaque-forming response).
   • Delayed sexual maturation, reduced sperm counts, altered estrous cyclicity.
   • Neuro-behavioural changes (altered startle response, learning deficits) reported after gestational + lactational exposure.
   • These are AhR-dependent transcriptional changes (e.g., deregulated IL-7, GnRH, dopamine turnover).
   • Classified as adverse.  Some immune and neuro-behavioural effects show partial reversibility; can be monitored clinically (CBC/lymphocyte subsets, neuro-developmental testing, reproductive hormone panels).

3. Toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – TCDD is an extremely potent AhR agonist with picomolar affinity (Kd ≈0.1 nM).  Nearly all developmental effects are AhR-dependent.
• Systemic availability: HIGH – Oral absorption >80 %; slow elimination (t½ in rodents ≈10–15 days, in humans ≈7 years); readily crosses the placenta and accumulates in fetal tissues.

4. Overall weight-of-evidence judgement
The replicated animal data (rats, mice, guinea pigs) and a coherent AhR-centred adverse-outcome pathway provide high confidence that repeated daily oral exposure to the maternal MTD will cause a spectrum of adverse developmental outcomes, dominated by embryo-fetal loss, growth retardation, cleft palate, immune and endocrine dysfunction.  These effects are largely irreversible once manifested, although some functional deficits may resolve over time or be surgically/medically managed.

5. Data gaps & uncertainties
• Limited information in non-rodent species; human epidemiologic data are confounded by mixed exposures and lower doses.
• Exact MTD in humans during pregnancy is unknown; inter-species toxicokinetic differences (much slower human clearance) suggest that risk could be underestimated by rodent data.

6. Clinical monitoring recommendations (if exposure suspected)
• Maternal and cord-blood TCDD concentration (HR-GC/HR-MS).
• Serial obstetric ultrasound for fetal growth and structural anomalies.
• Neonatal thyroid function, immune phenotyping, and echocardiography.
• Long-term follow-up of neuro-development and reproductive health.

References (from supplied resources)
– Schwetz RW et al. 1973. Teratogenicity of TCDD in Sherman rats.  
– NTP Board of Scientific Counselors minutes, 2004 & 2012 (developmental effects summaries).  
– Intraperitoneal cleft-palate model (Ah-responsive vs non-responsive mice).  
– AhR mechanistic discussions (IARC 1997; NTP mechanistic reviews).

tests.prompt: Describe the known of predicted developmental toxicological effects (if any) of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to a pregnant female where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route during gestation. Describe all developmental and teratogenenic effects. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Summary of available evidence
-------------------------------------------------
1. Availability of developmental studies after oral, gestational exposure to benzene (CAS 71-43-2)
   • No guideline prenatal-developmental toxicity study by the oral route was located in the resources.  
   • The NTP 2-year gavage bioassays in rats and mice (25-200 mg kg-1 day-1, 5 days week-1) provide systemic toxicity information but were not designed to examine gestational exposure; nevertheless they establish systemic availability of benzene and identify hematotoxic LOAELs ≥25 mg kg-1 day-1.  
   • Developmental/teratogenic data for benzene are therefore very limited; most robust developmental findings within the resources are from ethylbenzene, a structural analogue used for read-across.  

2. Read-across from ethylbenzene
   • Rats exposed by inhalation to 100–1 000 ppm ethylbenzene on gestation days (GD) 1–19 showed fetuses with supernumerary ribs and reduced crown-rump length (Andrews et al., 1981).  
   • The structural similarity (aromatic ring with one small alkyl substituent) supports qualitative read-across for nonspecific growth retardation and minor skeletal variations that generally accompany maternal toxicity. Limitations: (a) route difference (inhalation vs oral), (b) ethyl side-chain alters metabolism and toxicokinetics, (c) only minor developmental changes were reported.  

3. Weight-of-evidence judgement
   • Direct oral, gestational studies with benzene are lacking; however, benzene is systemically bioavailable after oral dosing (rapid absorption, CYP2E1-mediated metabolism to phenol, catechol, hydroquinone, muconaldehyde).  
   • Reactive metabolites cross the placenta; benzene oxide, hydroquinone and muconaldehyde have been measured in fetal tissues in other species, indicating potential for embryofetal exposure.  
   • Benzene causes marked maternal hematotoxicity at ≥25 mg kg-1 day-1 (leukopenia, lymphocytopenia). Maternal hypoxia/anaemia and direct clastogenicity are plausible drivers of developmental effects.  
   • Based on read-across and mechanistic plausibility, the most likely developmental outcomes at a maximum tolerated dose (≈200 mg kg-1 day-1 in rats; defined by ≤10 % body-weight depression) are:  
        – Fetal growth delay (lower body weight, reduced crown-rump length).  
        – Increased incidence of skeletal variations (e.g., supernumerary ribs, delayed ossification).  
        – Possible increase in embryofetal resorptions if pronounced maternal toxicity occurs.  
   • There is no convincing evidence for frank structural malformations or specific teratogenic syndromes.  

4. Mechanistic explanation of identified effects
   a) Growth delay and skeletal variations  
        – Maternal mode-of-action: benzene-induced anaemia and hypoxia decrease uteroplacental oxygen delivery, slowing ossification.  
        – Direct fetal mode-of-action: benzene metabolites generate oxidative stress and inhibit topoisomerase-II, transiently slowing cell proliferation in growth plates.  
        – Classification: Adverse (growth delay) vs adaptive (minor, transient ossification delays).  
        – Reversibility: Often reversible postnatally if maternal exposure ceases; monitoring by ultrasound/bone age in the clinic is feasible.  
   b) Embryofetal loss (resorptions)  
        – Mechanism: high maternal systemic concentrations of reactive metabolites create oxidative DNA damage and trigger p53-mediated apoptosis in the conceptus.  
        – Classification: Adverse.  
        – Reversibility: Irreversible for affected embryos but risk ceases after exposure stops; monitorable via early-pregnancy ultrasonography and serum hCG trends.  

5. Toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability (expert ranking)
   • Systemic availability: High. Benzene is lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 2.1) and shows >90 % oral absorption with rapid distribution, including to the fetus.  
   • Developmental toxicodynamic activity: Medium. Reactive metabolites are genotoxic and haematotoxic, but robust teratogenicity has not been demonstrated.  

6. Clinical relevance and monitoring
   • Maternal endpoints (early indicator): Progressive anaemia/leukopenia (CBC), elevated oxidative stress biomarkers (8-OH-dG).  
   • Fetal endpoints: Ultrasound measurement of biparietal diameter/long-bone length (growth), Doppler flow for placental insufficiency.  
   • Most skeletal variations have low clinical significance and are usually not detectable in humans without specialised imaging; growth restriction, however, is clinically relevant and monitorable.  

7. Uncertainties and data gaps
   • Absence of an OECD TG 414-compliant prenatal developmental toxicity study by the oral route for benzene.  
   • Limited information on placental transfer kinetics in humans.  
   • Extrapolation from inhalation studies and from ethylbenzene introduces uncertainty.  
   • Lack of information on post-natal neurodevelopment following in-utero benzene exposure.  

Conclusion
-------------------------------------------------
At a maximum tolerated oral dose administered daily throughout gestation, benzene is expected to be highly systemically available to both mother and fetus. The weight of evidence—drawing mainly on maternal haematotoxicity data, benzene metabolism/placental transfer, mechanistic genotoxicity, and limited read-across from ethylbenzene—indicates a potential for fetal growth retardation, increased skeletal variations, and possibly increased resorptions, but not for consistent structural malformations. Identified effects are adverse when they involve sustained growth deficit or embryofetal death, and largely reversible or of low clinical concern when limited to delayed ossification. Continuous maternal blood monitoring and prenatal ultrasound constitute practical clinical surveillance tools. Key data gaps (lack of oral guideline studies) introduce moderate uncertainty into this assessment.
