vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation – Developmental / Teratogenic Potential of Arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6; generic arsenite, AsIII)

1. Substance identity and toxicokinetic profile
   • Synonyms frequently used in the literature for arsenite (AsIII) include arsenous acid, arsenic trioxide in solution, sodium- or potassium-arsenite; all release the same biologically active AsIII ion in vivo.
   • Oral absorption: ≥ 80–95 % in rodents, rabbits and humans (WHO, 2011).  
   • Distribution: rapid entry into maternal blood, efficient placental transfer; cord-blood levels typically reach 60–90 % of maternal.  
   • Metabolism: step-wise methylation (AsIII → MMAIII → DMAIII/V). In the conceptus enzymatic methylation capacity is low, leaving the more reactive AsIII species.  
   • Elimination: urinary excretion t½ ≈ 12–24 h for inorganic arsenic, but retention in fetal tissues is longer (days).  
   • Systemic availability (expert judgment): HIGH.

2. Empirical evidence for developmental toxicity
   2.1 Animal studies
   • Mouse (gestation day (GD) 6–15, gavage): 2.5–10 mg As/kg bw/d → dose-related resorptions, reduced fetal weight, exencephaly, cleft palate, digit and tail defects, delayed ossification (Hood & Harrison, Teratology 1982).  
   • Rat (GD 6–19, drinking water): 25–50 ppm AsIII ≈ 3–6 mg/kg bw/d → ↑ post-implantation loss, skeletal variations; effects occurred only at or above maternal toxicity (Dixon et al., Reprod. Toxicol. 1998).  
   • Rabbit (GD 6-18, gavage, 1–4 mg/kg/d) → increased resorptions, growth retardation; malformations above 2 mg/kg (Golub, J. Toxicol. Environ. Health 1998).  
   • Developmental neurotoxicity: GD 6-15 exposure of mice (2–8 mg/kg) → later-life deficits in spatial learning, ↓ cholinergic receptor density, oxidative stress markers in brain (Martinez-Finley et al., Neurotoxicology 2009).

   2.2 Human epidemiology (exposure to As-contaminated drinking water, 50–500 µg/L; approximate intake 2–13 µg/kg bw/d)
   • Increased risk of spontaneous abortion and stillbirth (Hopenhayn et al., Epidemiology 2003).  
   • Reduced birth weight, pre-term delivery, neonatal mortality (Rahman et al., Environ. Health Persp. 2009).  
   • Higher prevalence of neural tube, cardiac and urogenital defects in Chilean, Bangladeshi and Taiwanese cohorts (Brender et al., Birth Defects Res. 2011).

   Although environmental intakes are lower than an MTD in animals, the concordance of endpoints supports biological plausibility.

3. Predicted/known developmental and teratogenic effects at a daily MTD during gestation (weight-of-evidence)

Endpoint / effect • Likelihood • Mechanistic basis • Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability

A. Early embryonic loss (implantation failure, resorptions, spontaneous abortion)
   • Likelihood: HIGH
   • Mechanism: oxidative stress–induced apoptosis in trophoblast; AsIII inhibits thioredoxin reductase → mitochondrial dysfunction; impaired angiogenesis via down-regulation of VEGF signalling.
   • Adverse? Yes (irreversible loss of conceptus).
   • Reversible? Not applicable.
   • Monitorable? Clinically by hCG patterns, ultrasound; maternal urine/blood As as exposure biomarker.

B. Fetal growth restriction / low birth weight
   • Likelihood: HIGH
   • Mechanism: placental insufficiency from endothelial cell death; interference with IGF signalling and nutrient transporters; epigenetic down-modulation (DNA hypomethylation) of growth-related genes.
   • Adverse? Yes.
   • Reversible? Partially (catch-up growth possible, but neuro-endocrine sequelae may persist).
   • Monitorable? Serial ultrasound biometrics, Doppler, neonatal length/weight.

C. Structural malformations (teratogenicity)
   1. Neural tube defects (exencephaly, spina bifida)
   2. Orofacial clefts
   3. Digit/tail agenesis, skeletal ossification delays
   • Likelihood: MEDIUM-to-HIGH (seen consistently in mice; less so in rats)
   • Mechanisms:
        – AsIII binds protein sulfhydryls, inhibiting folate-dependent one-carbon metabolism → impaired nucleotide synthesis during neurulation.
        – Disturbance of retinoic-acid signalling critical for craniofacial patterning.
        – ROS-triggered apoptosis in limb bud mesenchyme, delayed chondrogenesis.
   • Adverse? Yes.
   • Reversibility? No, structural defects are permanent.
   • Monitorable? Prenatal ultrasound (NTD, clefts, limb), post-natal exam; maternal serum α-fetoprotein for NTD.

D. Functional developmental neurotoxicity
   • Manifestations: learning and memory impairment, altered locomotor activity, hearing deficits (observed in rodents), possible ASD-like behaviour.
   • Likelihood: MEDIUM (animal evidence strong; human data suggest IQ decrement at lower exposure).
   • Mechanism: AsIII crosses fetal BBB, induces ROS, inhibits acetylcholinesterase, alters NMDA-receptor subunit expression; epigenetic changes in BDNF, Reelin genes.
   • Adverse? Yes.
   • Reversible? Largely irreversible, though some functional rehabilitation possible.
   • Monitorable? Developmental milestone testing, auditory screening, neuropsychological assessment.

E. Endocrine and metabolic programming effects
   • Outcomes: higher childhood blood pressure, altered glucose tolerance (human evidence).
   • Mechanism: epigenetic re-programming of glucocorticoid receptor and pancreatic β-cell genes.
   • Classification: Adverse, latent, partly reversible with lifestyle; monitorable by clinical chemistry.

F. Maternal effects relevant to development
   • Severe gastrointestinal irritation, liver oxidative damage, anemia—typical at MTD—can secondarily impair fetal oxygen/nutrient supply.
   • These are adverse, largely reversible after cessation; monitorable by liver enzymes, hemoglobin.

4. Overall toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability
   • Toxicodynamic potency toward embryonic tissues: HIGH  
   • Systemic availability after oral dosing: HIGH  
   Weight-of-evidence from multiple species plus epidemiology classifies arsenite as a potent developmental toxicant and teratogen.

5. Data gaps and uncertainties
   • No OECD guideline prenatal-dev. toxicity study reported specifically for CAS 15502-74-6, but close structural analogues (Na/K-arsenite) cover data needs – read-across justified by identical active moiety (AsIII).
   • Limited information in non-rodent species besides rabbit; primate data lacking.
   • Human epidemiology complicated by co-exposures (malnutrition, other metals).

6. Regulatory and clinical implications
   • IARC: Arsenic and inorganic arsenic compounds – Group 1 carcinogen; also recognized for developmental toxicity by ATSDR, EPA (RfD 0.3 µg/kg/d).
   • Daily oral MTD in pregnant individuals would be unequivocally contraindicated; the predicted effects are primarily adverse, largely irreversible but can be detected in utero or post-natally with standard obstetric/paediatric monitoring.

References (key)
   1. Hood RD, Harrison WP. “The effects of arsenite on mouse embryogenesis and teratogenesis.” Teratology 25, 1982.
   2. Dixon RL et al. “Developmental toxicity of arsenite in rats.” Reprod. Toxicol. 12, 1998.
   3. WHO. “Arsenic in Drinking-water – Background document for WHO Guidelines.” 2011.
   4. Hopenhayn C et al. Epidemiology 14, 2003.
   5. Rahman A et al. Environ. Health Perspect. 117, 2009.
   6. Martinez-Finley EJ, et al. Neurotoxicology 30, 2009.
   7. Brender JD et al. Birth Defects Res. 91, 2011.

Conclusion
Daily gestational exposure to a maximum tolerated dose of arsenite would produce high systemic fetal exposure and a spectrum of adverse developmental outcomes: early loss, growth restriction, overt malformations, and long-term neurofunctional deficits. These effects stem from arsenite’s high reactivity with thiol enzymes, induction of oxidative stress, disruption of one-carbon and retinoic-acid pathways, and epigenetic dysregulation—processes that are particularly sensitive during organogenesis and are mostly irreversible. Continuous clinical monitoring could detect many outcomes, but no adaptive or beneficial developmental responses are anticipated.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence conclusion
Repeated daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of methylmercury (MeHg; CAS 16056-34-1) throughout gestation would be expected to produce marked developmental toxicity in the conceptus, dominated by central-nervous-system (CNS) effects and, at sufficiently high doses, embryo–fetal loss. Toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability are both classified as HIGH.

Key developmental / teratogenic findings observed or predicted at MTD
1. Functional neurodevelopmental deficits (humans, Seychelles, Faroe, Minamata, Iraq; Needham 1998; Grandjean 1997): decreased performance on sophisticated neuropsychometric tests, mental retardation, spasticity, deafness, blindness, exaggerated reflexes, cerebral-palsy-like syndrome.
   • Adversity: Adverse.  
   • Reversibility: Generally irreversible; deficits persisted to ≥66 months in Seychelles cohort.  
   • Clinical monitoring: maternal/cord blood or hair Hg concentrations, developmental milestone testing, audiometry, vision screening, neuropsychometric batteries.

2. Histopathological damage to fetal brain (animals; Magas 1985): preferential injury to cerebellar granule cell layer and dorsal root ganglia.
   • Adversity: Adverse.  
   • Reversibility: Irreversible neuronal loss.  
   • Clinical monitoring: same biomarkers as above; MRI may reveal structural loss, but data gap acknowledged.

3. Embryo-/fetal lethality (rodents given organic Hg; thimerosal study cited in resources): dose-related early and late resorptions, dead fetuses in absence of overt maternal toxicity.
   • Adversity: Adverse.  
   • Reversibility: Not reversible.  
   • Clinical monitoring: prenatal ultrasound for loss; maternal serum hCG trends.

4. Decreased fetal weight (epidemiology indicates low birth-weight in severe poisonings; paralleled by thimerosal rodent data).
   • Adversity: Adverse if persistent growth restriction; potentially adaptive if slight, but at MTD magnitude judged adverse.  
   • Reversibility: Partial catch-up growth possible but uncertain; data gap.  
   • Clinical monitoring: obstetric ultrasound, neonatal weight.

5. Structural malformations – not common; high-dose thimerosal produced no gross malformations but lethality. For MeHg, major dysmorphology is rare; therefore teratogenicity per se is LIMITED, lethality and functional CNS toxicity predominate.

Mechanistic explanation (toxicodynamics)
• High oral bioavailability; MeHg forms a cysteine conjugate that mimics methionine and is actively transported across the placenta and the fetal blood-brain barrier (placental transfer documented in resources).  
• Inside fetal tissues MeHg dissociates, binds sulfhydryl groups, disrupts tubulin polymerisation and neuronal migration, impairs mitochondrial respiration and induces oxidative stress.  
• Rapid accumulation in developing brain (Magas 1985), especially cerebellar granule cells, leads to apoptotic or necrotic cell loss; comparable damage seen in human Minamata infants.  
• Limited ability of the fetus to demethylate/excrete Hg results in higher brain levels than mother (Grandjean 1997).

Systemic availability (ADME)  
High gastrointestinal absorption; distribution facilitated by amino-acid transporters; half-life in humans ~50–70 days, allowing accumulation over gestation. Ready placental transfer results in fetal blood/brain levels equal to or exceeding maternal (placental & blood-brain barrier crossing shown in thimerosal and MeHg studies). Metabolism to inorganic Hg is slow; elimination via bile later in life.

Summary of adversity/adaptation, reversibility and monitorability
• All identified developmental outcomes are adverse, not adaptive.  
• Functional neurotoxicity and cell loss are irreversible; growth effects partially reversible; lethality irreversible by definition.  
• Clinical monitoring feasible with: total Hg in maternal hair, blood, cord blood; neonatal blood or hair Hg; neurodevelopmental testing (Bayley, Denver scales, etc.); audiology and ophthalmologic exams; routine obstetric imaging for growth and viability.  

Uncertainties / data gaps
• Limited controlled animal data at true oral MTD for MeHg; thimerosal (ethyl-Hg) used as surrogate for embryo-lethal end point (read-across justified by similar Hg moiety, but potency differences possible).  
• Structural malformation incidence at MTD remains uncertain.  
• Reversibility of mild growth restriction not well documented.

Overall hazard characterization  
At an MTD, repeated oral MeHg exposure to the pregnant female poses a HIGH developmental hazard, driven by irreversible adverse neurodevelopmental effects and increased risk of fetal demise. The effects are monitorable but not therapeutically reversible once damage is established.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Summary of available evidence
The resource block contains one targeted prenatal gavage study with vinyl chloride (VC, CAS 75-01-4) in rats in which pregnant dams received 0 (vehicle), 150, 300 or 600 mg/kg-bw/day on gestation days (GD) 6-21.  Key findings were:
• Maternal toxicity: at 600 mg/kg some dams died; body-weight gain was reduced (used to set the maximal tolerated dose, MTD). 
• Embryo-fetal effects – dose-related:  
  – Increased percent post-implantation loss driven by early/late resorptions and dead fetuses at 600 mg/kg.  
  – Significant decreases in mean fetal weight at 300 and 600 mg/kg.  
  – Sex ratio not affected; no specific structural malformations were reported in this study.  

A separate high-dose screen (1 400-2 400 mg/kg) produced maternal deaths, dose-related increases in resorbed implants, marked decreases in fetal weight/length, and ≥10 % incidence of soft-tissue malformations at ≥900 mg/kg, confirming that developmental toxicity increases steeply above the MTD.

Conclusion on developmental toxicity at an oral MTD
1. Critical developmental effects: 
   • Embryo-fetal lethality (resorptions, dead fetuses) – adverse, irreversible.  
   • Fetal growth restriction (low body weight) – adverse; may translate to intra-uterine growth restriction (IUGR) in humans.  
   • Isolated soft-tissue malformations occur only above the MTD and cannot be ruled out at very high exposures.  
2. NOAEL/LOAEL: NOAEL = 150 mg/kg; LOAEL = 300 mg/kg for decreased fetal weight; developmental MTD ≈ 600 mg/kg in rats.  

Mechanistic considerations (weight of evidence from resources)
• Toxicokinetics / systemic availability – HIGH.  VC is a small, lipophilic gas that is efficiently absorbed from the gut.  It is rapidly delivered to maternal liver where CYP-mediated oxidation forms the reactive metabolites chloroethylene oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA).  Both are capable of crossing the placenta; therefore the conceptus is exposed.  
• Toxicodynamic activity – MEDIUM-to-HIGH.  CEO/CAA form DNA adducts and generate oxidative stress, leading to:  
   – Cytotoxicity in rapidly dividing embryonic cells → resorptions.  
   – Impaired cell proliferation and protein synthesis → reduced fetal growth.  
• Maternal contribution.  Maternal weight-loss and organ toxicity at the MTD may secondarily compromise placental perfusion, reinforcing fetal growth inhibition, but embryo-lethality exceeds what would be expected from maternal effects alone, indicating direct developmental toxicity.  

Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability
Endpoint                            | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
------------------------------------|------------------|-------------|----------------------
Early/late resorptions / fetal death | Adverse          | Irreversible| Spontaneous abortion, ultrasound loss of heartbeat
Low fetal weight (IUGR)             | Adverse          | Partly (catch-up growth possible but not assured) | Prenatal ultrasound biometry, birth-weight monitoring
Soft-tissue malformations           | Adverse          | Irreversible (may require surgery) | Prenatal ultrasonography, neonatal examination

Uncertainties and data gaps
• Limited oral developmental studies; only rats evaluated.  
• Malformation spectrum at doses ≤ MTD not fully characterised.  
• Human placental transfer data are lacking; conclusions rely on physicochemical analogy and small-molecule literature.  

Overall expert judgement
Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid absorption, distribution, and placental crossing).  
Toxicodynamic potency: MEDIUM-HIGH (reactive metabolites cause DNA damage/oxidative stress).  
At or near the rat oral MTD, vinyl chloride is a developmental toxicant producing embryo-fetal loss and growth retardation that are adverse, largely irreversible, and clinically detectable through standard obstetric monitoring.
